# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Machine Cycle Time

The 8051 internally divides the oscillator clock by a fixed factor of **12**. Each instruction consists of one or more *machine cycles*, and each machine cycle contains **12 oscillator periods** (also called *T-states* or *clock pulses*).

We begin from the fundamental relationship:

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$

This is the period of one oscillation of the quartz crystal. Since one machine cycle equals 12 such oscillations, we write:

$$T_{mc} = 12 \times T_{osc}$$

Substituting the expression for $T_{osc}$:

$$T_{mc} = 12 \times \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

Therefore the relationship is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{mc} &= \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical evaluation (typical KTU question):**

Suppose the crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz** (the de-facto standard for serial-baud-rate generation in the 8051 family). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{mc} &= \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \\
       &= \frac{12}{11059200} \\
       &= 1.085\,\mu s \text{ (approximately)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This particular crystal was chosen because $11.0592 / 12 = 921.6\,kHz$, which divides cleanly by standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400) yielding integer reload values for the timer — an elegant piece of design history often tested in KTU viva.

> [!NOTE]
> For a **12 MHz crystal**, the machine cycle equals exactly **1 μs**, making timing calculations trivial. This is the most frequently assumed value in exam problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Timer Overflow Period

Let the initial 16-bit value loaded into the timer registers THx and TLx be denoted $T_{initial}$. Since the timer is a *up-counter* that increments on every timer-clock pulse, the number of pulses required to roll over from $T_{initial}$ to $65536$ (since the timer is 16-bit, and it rolls over from $FFFF_{H} + 1 = 10000_{H}$) is:

$$N_{roll} = 65536 - T_{initial}$$

The timer-clock period equals one machine cycle (because the timer clock is internally divided from the oscillator by 12):

$$T_{tick} = T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

Hence the rollover time is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{overflow} &= N_{roll} \times T_{tick} \\
             &= (65536 - T_{initial}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical example:** For $f_{osc} = 12\,MHz$ and $T_{initial} = FC18_{H}$ (= 64536 decimal), we obtain:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{overflow} &= (65536 - 64536) \times \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} \\
             &= 1000 \times 1\,\mu s \\
             &= 1\,ms
\end{aligned}
$$

This means the timer generates an interrupt (or sets the TFx flag) every **1 ms**, a typical value used to construct a real-time clock in an automotive instrument cluster.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Baud Rate Generation for Serial Port

In Mode 1 of the UART (8-bit data, variable baud rate, 1 start + 1 stop bit), the baud rate is generated using **Timer 1 in auto-reload Mode 2** (8-bit timer with TH1 as the reload value). The standard KTU derivation proceeds as follows.

The Timer 1 overflow rate is:

$$f_{overflow} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

The 8051's UART divides this overflow rate by 32 (or by 16 if SMOD = 1) to obtain the bit rate. The exact formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Baud &= \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times f_{overflow} \\
     &= \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rearranging for TH1:

$$TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{osc} \times 2^{SMOD}}{384 \times Baud}$$

**Numerical example:** For $f_{osc} = 11.0592\,MHz$, $SMOD = 0$, and $Baud = 9600$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TH1 &= 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600} \\
    &= 256 - \frac{11059200}{3686400} \\
    &= 256 - 3 \\
    &= 253 = FD_{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

The integer result (TH1 = FD hex) demonstrates why this crystal frequency is preferred — no rounding error, so actual baud rate equals 9600 exactly.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Timer Modes

| Property | Mode 0 | Mode 1 | Mode 2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Timer size | 13-bit (TLx 5 bits + THx 8 bits) | 16-bit | 8-bit auto-reload |
| Range (counts) | 0 – 8191 | 0 – 65535 | 0 – 255 |
| Reload action | Software | Software | Hardware automatic |
| Typical use | Legacy 8048 compat | Long delays, event timing | Baud-rate generation |
| Automotive app | Rare | Wiper interval, RPM sensing | OBD-II UART |
| KTU exam frequency | Low | **High** | **High** |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: 8051 Interrupt Structure

| Interrupt | Flag | Vector Address | Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | – | **0000H** | Power-on / RST pin HIGH |
| External 0 | IE0 | **0003H** | Falling edge / low on INT0 |
| Timer 0 | TF0 | **000BH** | Timer 0 overflow |
| External 1 | IE1 | **0013H** | Falling edge / low on INT1 |
| Timer 1 | TF1 | **001BH** | Timer 1 overflow |
| Serial Port | TI / RI | **0023H** | TX complete or RX byte received |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Vector addresses are spaced **8 bytes apart**. Only the very first KTU questions have you write code at these addresses; for descriptive modules, you must **memorize the addresses** because they appear in interrupt-allocation questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"List any six features of the 8051 microcontroller."**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 microcontroller possesses the following salient features:

1. **8-bit ALU and CPU** — operates on 8-bit operands natively.
2. **Harvard architecture** — separate program and data buses.
3. **On-chip 4 KB ROM** for program storage and **128 bytes of on-chip RAM**.
4. **Four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3) providing 32 I/O pins.
5. **Two 16-bit timers/counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1), each with 4 programmable operating modes.
6. **Full-duplex programmable UART** serial port for communication with other peripherals or ECUs.
7. **Five interrupt sources** (2 external + 2 timer + 1 serial) with two priority levels.
8. **64 KB external code memory** and **64 KB external data memory** address space.
9. **On-chip oscillator and clock circuitry** — needs only an external quartz crystal.
10. **Boolean processor** — single-bit operations directly on the bit-addressable RAM area.

> [!TIP]
> Listing *at least six* features scores full 3 marks. Grouping them under headings (Memory, Peripherals, Architecture) earns a "good presentation" remark.

---

### Question A.2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"Differentiate between Harvard and Von-Neumann architectures as implemented in the 8051."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Harvard Architecture (8051) | Von-Neumann Architecture |
|---|---|---|
| Memory | Separate program and data memory | Single shared memory for both |
| Bus | Two distinct buses | One shared bus |
| Fetch & Execute | Simultaneous (pipelined) | Sequential |
| Speed | Faster | Slower due to bus contention |
| Firmware safety | Code cannot be overwritten accidentally | Possible corruption of code |
| Automotive relevance | Used in ECUs (8051, Aurix) | Used in general-purpose CPUs (PCs) |
| Example device | **Intel 8051, PIC, ARM Cortex-M** | **Intel 8085, x86** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part-B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice. Sub-parts typically split as **(a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks**.

---

### Question B.1(A) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **internal architecture of the Intel 8051 microcontroller**. Explain the function of each block in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **pin configuration of the 8051 in a 40-pin DIP package** with the help of a labeled diagram. List the alternate functions of Port 3. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Architecture (7 marks)

The 8051 internal architecture consists of the following interconnected blocks:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** contains an 8-bit ALU, an accumulator (ACC), a B-register, the Program Status Word (PSW), the Stack Pointer (SP), the Data Pointer (DPTR), and a 16-bit Program Counter (PC). The ALU performs arithmetic (+, –, ×, ÷), logical (AND, OR, XOR), rotate, and complement operations. The B-register is dedicated to MUL/DIV. The PSW holds flags including Carry (CY), Auxiliary Carry (AC), Overflow (OV), Parity (P), and register-bank-select bits (RS1, RS0). **(2 marks)**

2. **Memory Subsystem** — separates program and data memory (Harvard). Internal 4 KB ROM holds firmware; internal 128 bytes RAM is split into a register-bank area (00H–1FH), bit-addressable area (20H–2FH), and general-purpose scratchpad (30H–7FH). The SFR area lies at 80H–FFH. External memory can extend each address space up to 64 KB. **(1.5 marks)**

3. **Parallel I/O Ports** — P0 (open-drain, multiplexed low-byte address/data bus), P1 (general purpose), P2 (high-byte address bus), P3 (alternate-function pins). **(1 mark)**

4. **Timers/Counters** — two 16-bit timers, each programmable into 4 modes via TMOD and controlled via TCON. **(1 mark)**

5. **Serial Port (UART)** — full-duplex, programmable via SCON and SBUF, suitable for OBD-II diagnostics. **(0.5 mark)**

6. **Interrupt Controller** — handles 5 sources, 2 priority levels via IE and IP registers. **(0.5 mark)**

7. **Oscillator and Control** — uses external quartz crystal at XTAL1/XTAL2; generates internal machine cycles by dividing oscillator by 12. **(0.5 mark)**

*Refer to the block diagram drawn in Section 4.1 for the visual answer (board examiners expect a hand-drawn equivalent).*

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Pin Configuration (7 marks)

The 8051 is housed in a 40-pin DIP package. Pin groupings:

- **Port 1 (Pins 1–8):** P1.0 through P1.7, general-purpose bidirectional I/O.
- **Pin 9 (RST):** Reset input. A HIGH on this pin for ≥ 2 machine cycles resets the 8051 (PC = 0000H, SP = 07H, all ports FFH).
- **Port 3 (Pins 10–17):** P3.0–P3.7, alternate-function pins.
- **Pins 18, 19 (XTAL2, XTAL1):** Crystal oscillator connections.
- **Pin 20 (GND):** Ground.
- **Port 2 (Pins 21–28):** P2.0–P2.7, serve as the high-order address byte (A8–A15) when external memory is used.
- **Pin 29 (PSEN̄):** Program Store Enable, active LOW; asserted when reading external code memory.
- **Pin 30 (ALE):** Address Latch Enable; used to demultiplex P0's address/data lines via an external 74HC373 latch.
- **Pin 31 (EĀ/VPP):** External Access; if tied LOW, forces external ROM use; HIGH enables internal ROM (used for programming the on-chip ROM).
- **Port 0 (Pins 32–39):** P0.0–P0.7, multiplexed address (low-byte)/data bus; requires external pull-ups in pure I/O mode.
- **Pin 40 (VCC):** +5 V supply.

**Alternate Functions of Port 3** (must be listed verbatim for full marks):

| Pin | Alternate Function | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| P3.0 | RXD | Serial input |
| P3.1 | TXD | Serial output |
| P3.2 | INT0̄ | External interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | INT1̄ | External interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | T0 | Timer 0 external clock |
| P3.5 | T1 | Timer 1 external clock |
| P3.6 | WR̄ | External data write |
| P3.7 | RD̄ | External data read |

---

### Question B.1(B) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, 14 Marks — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Describe the **interrupt structure of the 8051**. List all interrupt sources with their vector addresses, and explain the role of the **IE and IP registers** with the help of a diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Program Status Word (PSW) register** bit-by-bit. Discuss the **register bank selection mechanism** and its importance in automotive real-time systems. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Interrupt Structure (7 marks)

The 8051 supports **five interrupt sources** plus a *system reset*:

| Interrupt Source | Flag | Vector Address | Default Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reset | – | **0000H** | Highest |
| External Interrupt 0 | IE0 | **0003H** | 1 |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **000BH** | 2 |
| External Interrupt 1 | IE1 | **0013H** | 3 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **001BH** | 4 |
| Serial Port (TI or RI) | TI/RI | **0023H** | 5 (lowest) |

**Role of the IE (Interrupt Enable) register at A8H:**

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| IE.7 | EA | Global enable (must be 1 to accept any interrupt) |
| IE.6 | – | Reserved |
| IE.5 | – | Reserved |
| IE.4 | ES | Enable Serial port interrupt |
| IE.3 | ET1 | Enable Timer 1 interrupt |
| IE.2 | EX1 | Enable External Interrupt 1 |
| IE.1 | ET0 | Enable Timer 0 interrupt |
| IE.0 | EX0 | Enable External Interrupt 0 |

**Role of the IP (Interrupt Priority) register at B8H:** Sets each interrupt as *high priority* (bit = 1) or *low priority* (bit = 0). A high-priority interrupt can preempt a low-priority one, enabling nested interrupts.

In automotive ECUs, this priority scheme ensures that a **crash sensor interrupt** (External INT0) preempts a **routine wiper timer interrupt**, providing safety-critical pre-emption. **(1 mark for example)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — PSW Register (7 marks)

PSW at **D0H** is bit-addressable. Bit layout:

| Bit | Symbol | Reset | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | CY | 0 | Carry flag — set on addition overflow / borrow |
| 6 | AC | 0 | Auxiliary carry — used for BCD arithmetic |
| 5 | F0 | 0 | User-definable flag |
| 4 | RS1 | 0 | Register bank select bit 1 |
| 3 | RS0 | 0 | Register bank select bit 0 |
| 2 | OV | 0 | Overflow flag — set on signed-arithmetic overflow |
| 1 | – | 0 | Reserved (user-definable) |
| 0 | P | 0 | Parity flag — 1 if ACC has odd number of 1-bits |

**Register Bank Selection:** RS1:RS0 selects one of four register banks in internal RAM:

- **Bank 0 (00H–07H)** — default after reset.
- **Bank 1 (08H–0FH)**
- **Bank 2 (10H–17H)**
- **Bank 3 (18H–1FH)**

**Automotive Importance:** In an ECU with a *crash interrupt handler*, switching to Bank 1 immediately provides R0–R7 without pushing onto the stack, guaranteeing a **deterministic, sub-microsecond context switch**. This is critical for airbag deployment timing (typically required within 10 ms of crash detection). Hence the register-bank feature is not a programming convenience but a **safety-relevant hardware capability**. **(2 marks for automotive context)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in 8051 Architecture Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to specify machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** in any timing question. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if omitted.
> 2. **Confusing PSW address D0H with SFR general address 80H** — students often quote "SFR starts at 80H" but fail to mention PSW individually.
> 3. **Stating "5 interrupts" without listing the reset vector separately.** Reset is *not* counted as an interrupt, but it occupies vector 0000H.
> 4. **Mixing up Port 0 with Port 2** in pin diagrams — Port 0 is multiplexed address/data (lower byte); Port 2 is the high-byte address. Marks are deducted if labels are swapped.
> 5. **Not stating that P0 needs external pull-up resistors** — full marks on I/O ports require mentioning this *because examiners test it specifically*.
> 6. **Writing "32 KB memory" instead of "64 KB address space"** — KTU specifically tests whether the student understands the difference between addressable range and physically available memory.
> 7. **In interrupt diagrams, forgetting to show IE register gating the incoming requests** — the block diagram must show IE between source and CPU.
> 8. **Quoting wrong vector addresses** — memorizing 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H is mandatory; any wrong hex value costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — 8051 Microcontroller**

- **Architecture**: Harvard, 8-bit, 16-bit PC, 4 KB internal ROM, 128 B internal RAM, 64 KB external code + 64 KB external data.
- **Pin Package**: 40-pin DIP, VCC on pin 40, GND on pin 20, RST on pin 9, XTAL1/2 on 19/18.
- **Ports**: P0 (open-drain, multiplexed AD bus, needs pull-ups); P1 (general purpose); P2 (high address byte); P3 (alternate functions).
- **Port 3 Alternate Functions**: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD — must be memorized.
- **Registers to memorize**: ACC (E0H), B (F0H), PSW (D0H), SP (81H, reset to 07H), DPTR (82H/83H), PC (16-bit, no SFR).
- **SFR Range**: 80H – FFH (upper 128 bytes).
- **Bit-addressable area**: 20H – 2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits); also PSW, ACC, B, P0–P3, TCON, SCON, IE, IP.
- **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods** → for 12 MHz crystal, $T_{mc} = 1\,\mu s$.
- **Timer formula**: $T_{overflow} = (65536 - T_{initial}) \times 12 / f_{osc}$.
- **Baud rate formula**: $Baud = (2^{SMOD} / 32) \times f_{osc} / [12 \times (256 - TH1)]$.
- **Timer modes**: 0 = 13-bit (legacy), 1 = 16-bit, 2 = 8-bit auto-reload (used for baud), 3 = split timer.
- **Interrupt vectors** (must be memorized): 0000H Reset, 0003H INT0, 000BH TF0, 0013H INT1, 001BH TF1, 0023H Serial.
- **Interrupt Enable (IE)**: EA bit must be 1, then enable individual bits (EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES).
- **Register Banks**: 4 banks of 8 bytes (R0–R7), selected via PSW bits RS1:RS0.
- **Crystal frequency**: 11.0592 MHz is preferred because it divides cleanly into standard baud rates.
- **Automotive applications** to mention in any answer: BCM, instrument cluster, wiper control, central locking, climate control, OBD-I diagnostics.
- **Why 8051 survives in cars**: deterministic interrupt latency, low cost (~₹30–₹80 per chip), ultra-low power, decades of stable tool-chain support.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automotive Sensors and Actuators: Types and Functionality of Sensors - Position, Temperature, Lambda, Speed, Pressure, and Radar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT725 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS IN AUTOMOBILES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Automotive Sensors and Actuators: Types and Functionality of Sensors - Position, Temperature, Lambda, Speed, Pressure, and Radar # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Introduction to Automotive Sensors An **automotive sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical, chemical, or mechanical quantity (position, temperature, pressure, speed, chemical composition, electromagnetic wave) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, frequency, or digital word) that can be processed by an Embedded Electronic Control Unit (ECU). They are the **sensory organs** of the modern vehicle, enabling the realization of advanced driver-assistance systems (ADAS), engine management, chassis control, and infotainment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Sensors form the *input layer* of every automotive embedded system. No control loop is possible without accurate, real-time sensing. The classification by measurand (Position, Temp, Lambda, Speed, Pressure, Radar) is the examiner's favorite 2-mark opener. ## 1.2 The Six Major Sensor Families Covered | # | Family | Primary Measurand | Typical ECU User | |---|--------|-------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Position** | Angular / Linear displacement | Engine, EPS, Transmission | | 2 | **Temperature** | Thermal state (°C / K) | ECM, HVAC, BMS | | 3 | **Lambda (O₂)** | Exhaust oxygen / AFR | ECM (closed-loop fueling) | | 4 | **Speed** | Rotational / Translational velocity | ABS, ESC, Transmission | | 5 | **Pressure** | Force-per-area (Pa / bar) | ECM, TPMS, Brakes | | 6 | **Radar** | Distance & relative velocity | ADAS (ACC, AEB, BSD) | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Car as a Human Body:** > Imagine driving a car with your eyes closed. You would crash instantly. Sensors give the car its **five senses**. Position sensors are its *proprioception* (knowing where its limbs are), temperature sensors are its *thermoreceptors* (skin), the Lambda sensor is its *tongue* (tasting exhaust), speed sensors are its *vestibular system* (balance), pressure sensors are its *touch*, and radar is its *echolocation* (like a bat). The ECU (brain) fuses all these signals to make decisions in real time. ## 1.4 Functional Block of a Generic Sensor Every automotive sensor subsystem follows a common pipeline: **Physical Phenomenon → Sensing Element → Signal Conditioning (Amplification / Filtering) → ADC → ECU → Actuator Command** - **Sensing element** (resistive, piezoelectric, semiconductor, electromagnetic) - **Signal conditioning** (Wheatstone bridge, op-amp, filter) - **Output interface** (analog 0–5 V, PWM, SENT, CAN, LIN, SPI) ## 1.5 Classification Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **Classification by Output Signal:** > - **Analog sensors:** Resistive, voltage-divider, Wheatstone-bridge based (e.g., MAP, TPS, NTC). > - **Digital sensors:** Hall-effect, magnetoresistive, optical encoders, PWM/SENT output. > - **Frequency-modulated sensors:** Variable reluctance (inductive) — output is a frequency proportional to speed. ## 1.6 Why This Module Matters (Industry Context) Modern cars contain **~100–150 sensors**. Premium vehicles with Level-2+ ADAS can exceed **200 sensors**. The global automotive sensor market is valued in billions of USD and is the fastest-growing segment in automotive electronics, driven by **electrification (EVs)** and **autonomy (L2 → L5)**. # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Position Sensors ### 2.1.1 Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) - **Type:** Potentiometric (rheostat) or Hall-effect (non-contact). - **Working:** A wiper slides over a resistive track mechanically coupled to the throttle shaft. Output voltage is a **ratiometric** fraction of the 5 V reference. - **Output:** $V_{TPS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_w}{R_{total}}$ where $R_w$ is wiper resistance and $R_{total}$ is total track resistance. - **Application:** ECM uses it to compute driver demand, idle air control, and traction torque limits. ### 2.1.2 Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP) - **Type:** Variable Reluctance (VR) or Hall-effect. - **Working:** A toothed reluctor wheel (e.g., **60−2 teeth** for missing-tooth reference) passes a magnetic pickup, inducing an AC voltage in VR type or switching a Hall element in Hall type. - **Output Frequency:** $f = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{60}$ where $n$ = RPM and $N$ = number of teeth. - **Application:** Engine timing (injection, ignition), misfire detection, RPM readout. ### 2.1.3 Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP) - **Working:** Identical principle to CKP but uses a single-tooth or multi-tooth disc to identify cylinder #1 TDC for **sequential fuel injection**. ### 2.1.4 Steering Angle Sensor (SAS) - **Type:** Optical or Hall-effect encoder, multi-turn. - **Resolution:** typically **0.1°** per bit (10-bit or 12-bit). - **Application:** ESC (Electronic Stability Control), ADAS lane keeping, autonomous driving. ## 2.2 Temperature Sensors ### 2.2.1 Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor (ECTS) - **Element:** **NTC thermistor** (Negative Temperature Coefficient). - **Steinhart–Hart equation** (full accuracy): $$\dfrac{1}{T} = A + B \cdot \ln(R) + C \cdot (\ln(R))^3$$ - **Simplified B-parameter form** (industry standard): $$R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{B \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$ - where $R_0$ is resistance at reference $T_0$ (usually **25 °C = 298.15 K**) and $B$ is the material constant (**typically 3000–5000 K** for automotive NTCs). - A bias resistor forms a **voltage divider** with the NTC; the ECM reads the divided voltage. ### 2.2.2 Intake Air Temperature (IAT) Sensor - Same NTC principle; located in the intake manifold. - **Used for:** Air mass density correction (hot air is less dense → less fuel). ### 2.2.3 Other Thermal Sensors - **Oil temperature, fuel temperature, exhaust gas temperature (EGT), ambient air, evaporator temperature** — all use NTC/PTC thermistors or thermocouples (Type K for EGT up to 1000 °C). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse NTC (resistance drops as T rises) with PTC (resistance rises with T). In **95%** of automotive temperature sensing, **NTC** is used. ## 2.3 Lambda (Oxygen / O₂) Sensor ### 2.3.1 Purpose Located in the exhaust stream (pre-catalyst and post-catalyst). Provides closed-loop feedback to maintain **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio (AFR)** for three-way catalyst (TWC) efficiency. ### 2.3.2 Stoichiometric Definition $$\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$$ - For gasoline: $AFR_{stoich} \approx$ **14.7 : 1** by mass. - $\lambda = 1$ → stoichiometric, $\lambda > 1$ → lean, $\lambda < 1$ → rich. ### 2.3.3 Working Principle — Zirconia (ZrO₂) Cell - Solid-state electrolyte; oxygen ions diffuse across a porous platinum electrode. - Generates a **Nernst voltage**: $$E = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{4 \cdot F} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{O_2,\,air}}{P_{O_2,\,exhaust}}\right)$$ - where $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**, $F$ = **96,485 C/mol**, and $P_{O_2}$ are partial pressures of oxygen. - **Output behavior:** Switches between ~0.1 V (rich) and ~0.9 V (lean) — hence called **narrowband** or **switch-type** sensor. ### 2.3.4 Wideband Lambda Sensor - Uses a **pump cell** that maintains $\lambda = 1$ by pumping O₂ ions in/out. - Pump current $I_p$ is proportional to air-fuel deviation; enables precise control from $\lambda = 0.7$ to $\lambda = \infty$ (true lean-burn and Diesel support). ## 2.4 Speed Sensors ### 2.4.1 Wheel Speed Sensor (WSS) — ABS/ESC - **Type:** Active (Hall-effect) or Passive (variable reluctance). - **Output frequency:** $f = \dfrac{v \cdot N}{\pi \cdot D}$ where $v$ is vehicle speed, $N$ = number of teeth on tone ring, $D$ = tire effective diameter. - **Resolution:** 48-pole or 64-pole tone rings common. ### 2.4.2 Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) - Mounted on transmission output shaft; drives the speedometer. ### 2.4.3 Transmission Input/Output Speed Sensors - Used for **automatic transmission** shift scheduling and torque converter lockup. ## 2.5 Pressure Sensors ### 2.5.1 Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor - **Element:** Silicon piezoresistive diaphragm with a **Wheatstone bridge** of four strain gauges. - **Bridge output (balanced):** $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ - Measures intake manifold vacuum/boost in 10 kPa – 400 kPa range. - **Critical** for speed-density engine control and turbo boost regulation. ### 2.5.2 Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS) - **Direct TPMS:** In-wheel pressure sensor (piezoresistive + 433 MHz / 315 MHz RF transmitter). - **Indirect TPMS:** Software-based, infers pressure drop from wheel-speed differential (no dedicated sensor). ### 2.5.3 Other Pressure Sensors - Oil pressure, fuel rail pressure (diesel common-rail up to **2500 bar**), brake fluid pressure, HVAC refrigerant pressure, barometric pressure. ## 2.6 Radar Sensors (Automotive mmWave) ### 2.6.1 Frequency Bands - **76–77 GHz** (long-range, narrow-band, ACC / AEB) - **77–81 GHz** (short-range, wide-band, blind-spot detection) ### 2.6.2 FMCW (Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave) Principle - Transmit chirp of bandwidth $B$ and duration $T_{chirp}$. - **Beat frequency** for stationary target at range $R$: $$f_{beat} = \dfrac{2 \cdot R \cdot \Delta f}{c \cdot T_{chirp}}$$ - For **moving target**, the Doppler shift $f_D = \dfrac{2 \cdot v_r}{\lambda}$ shifts the beat frequency — enabling simultaneous range and velocity measurement. ### 2.6.3 Range Resolution - $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot B}$ - With $B =$ **1 GHz**, $\Delta R \approx$ **15 cm**. ### 2.6.4 Applications - Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), Forward Collision Warning (FCW), Autonomous Emergency Braking (AEB), Blind Spot Detection (BSD), Lane Change Assist, Cross-Traffic Alert, automated parking. ## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — at least one formula appears in every KTU exam paper for this module.** | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | NTC Thermistor | $R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | $R$ in $\Omega$, $T$ in K, $B$ in K | | 2 | Voltage Divider (NTC) | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_{NTC}}{R_{NTC} + R_{bias}}$ | V | | 3 | TPS Potentiometric | $V_{TPS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{\theta}{\theta_{max}}$ | $\theta$ in degrees | | 4 | CKP Frequency | $f = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{60}$ | $n$ RPM, $N$ teeth | | 5 | Wheel Speed | $f = \dfrac{v \cdot N}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $v$ m/s, $D$ m | | 6 | Lambda (AFR) | $\lambda = AFR_{act} / AFR_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Stoich AFR (gasoline) | $AFR_{stoich} \approx 14.7$ | dimensionless ratio | | 8 | Nernst (Lambda Cell) | $E = \dfrac{RT}{4F} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{O_2,air}}{P_{O_2,ex}}\right)$ | V | | 9 | Wheatstone Bridge | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R}$ | V | | 10 | FMCW Beat Frequency | $f_{beat} = \dfrac{2 R \Delta f}{c \, T_{chirp}}$ | Hz | | 11 | Doppler Shift (Radar) | $f_D = \dfrac{2 v_r}{\lambda}$ | Hz | | 12 | Radar Range Resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 B}$ | m | | 13 | Max Range (Radar) | $R_{max} = \dfrac{c \, T_{chirp}}{2}$ | m | | 14 | Pressure (Piezoresistive) | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | $G$ = gauge factor | | 15 | Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | dimensionless | # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation: NTC Thermistor Resistance at Engine Operating Temperature > **Problem:** A coolant temperature sensor uses an NTC thermistor with $R_0 = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25\,°\text{C}$ and $B = 3950\,\text{K}$. Find its resistance at $T = 90\,°\text{C}$ (normal operating temp). **Step 1 — Convert Celsius to Kelvin.** Reference temperature $T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15\,\text{K}$. Target temperature $T = 90 + 273.15 = 363.15\,\text{K}$. **Step 2 — Apply the B-parameter equation.** $$R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{B\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the reciprocal temperature difference.** $$\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{363.15} = 2.7537 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{T_0} = \dfrac{1}{298.15} = 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} = -6.003 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply by B.** $$B \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \times (-6.003 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.3712$$ **Step 5 — Take the exponential.** $$e^{-2.3712} = 0.0934$$ **Step 6 — Multiply by $R_0$.** $$R(90\,°\text{C}) = 10\,\text{k}\Omega \times 0.0934 \approx 0.934\,\text{k}\Omega = 934\,\Omega$$ > **Interpretation:** Resistance drops from 10 kΩ to ~934 Ω as the engine warms up — a ~91% reduction. The ECM exploits this large dynamic range to infer temperature with high sensitivity. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: CKP Frequency and Tooth-Pass Time > **Problem:** A 60−2 crank wheel spins at 3000 RPM. The ECM samples tooth edges. Calculate (a) the output frequency, and (b) the time between successive tooth edges. **Step 1 — Effective teeth count.** A 60−2 wheel has 60 − 2 = **58** teeth. **Step 2 — Mechanical frequency in Hz.** $$f_{mech} = \dfrac{n}{60} = \dfrac{3000}{60} = 50\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 3 — Tooth-pass frequency.** $$f = f_{mech} \cdot N = 50 \times 58 = 2900\,\text{Hz}$$ **Step 4 — Time between edges.** $$T_{tooth} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{2900} \approx 344.8\,\mu s$$ > **Interpretation:** At 3000 RPM, the ECM has only ~345 μs between crankshaft tooth events to compute timing, fuel pulse width, and ignition dwell — a true real-time embedded constraint. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: MAP Sensor Output Using Wheatstone Bridge > **Problem:** A silicon MAP sensor uses a Wheatstone bridge with $R = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ and strain gauge factor $G = 100$. At maximum vacuum (ΔP), the strain is $\varepsilon = 0.001$ (1000 microstrain). The bridge is excited at $V_{ex} = 5\,\text{V}$. Find the bridge output voltage. **Step 1 — Resistance change in each active gauge.** $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 100 \times 0.001 = 0.1$$ **Step 2 — Bridge output for full-bridge (4 active gauges, two in tension, two in compression).** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = 5 \times 0.1 = 0.5\,\text{V}$$ **Step 3 — Sensitivity.** $$S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ex}} = 0.1\,\text{V/V} = 100\,\text{mV/V}$$ > **Interpretation:** Modern MAP sensors include on-chip CMOS amplifiers to boost this to a 0–5 V ratiometric output. The silicon die is also temperature-compensated via diffused resistors. --- ## 3.4 Derivation: FMCW Radar Range from Beat Frequency > **Problem:** A 77 GHz automotive radar chirps from 76 GHz to 77 GHz over $T_{chirp} = 40\,\text{ms}$ ($\Delta f = 1\,\text{GHz}$). A target is detected with beat frequency $f_{beat} = 100\,\text{kHz}$. Find the range $R$. **Step 1 — Identify given values.** $\Delta f = 1 \times 10^9\,\text{Hz}$, $T_{chirp} = 40 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}$, $f_{beat} = 1 \times 10^5\,\text{Hz}$. **Step 2 — Apply the FMCW range equation.** $$R = \dfrac{f_{beat} \cdot c \cdot T_{chirp}}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute.** $$R = \dfrac{(10^5)(3 \times 10^8)(40 \times 10^{-3})}{2 \times 10^9}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify numerator.** $$10^5 \times 3 \times 10^8 \times 4 \times 10^{-2} = 1.2 \times 10^{12}$$ **Step 5 — Divide by denominator.** $$R = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{12}}{2 \times 10^9} = 600\,\text{m}$$ > **Interpretation:** A 100 kHz beat corresponds to 600 m range, exceeding typical long-radar requirement of 250 m. Closer targets yield lower beat frequencies, and the radar MCU's FFT resolution directly determines minimum detectable range. --- ## 3.5 Derivation: Range Resolution and Bandwidth > **Problem:** A short-range radar (BSM) has bandwidth $B = 4\,\text{GHz}$ (77–81 GHz). Find its range resolution. **Step 1 — Apply resolution formula.** $$\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot B}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute.** $$\Delta R = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 4 \times 10^9} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{8 \times 10^9}$$ **Step 3 — Compute.** $$\Delta R = 0.0375\,\text{m} = 3.75\,\text{cm}$$ > **Interpretation:** With ~4 cm resolution, the radar can distinguish two motorcycles riding side-by-side in adjacent lanes — a critical safety function for BSM systems. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Family Trade-offs > [!NOTE] > **This is the matrix KTU examiners expect for 7-mark comparative questions.** | Property | Position | Temperature | Lambda | Speed | Pressure | Radar | |----------|----------|-------------|--------|-------|----------|-------| | **Sensing Element** | Pot/Hall/VR | NTC / Thermocouple | ZrO₂ cell | VR / Hall | Piezoresistive | mmWave antenna | | **Output** | Analog V / PWM / SENT | Resistive / Voltage | 0.1–0.9 V or $I_p$ | AC sine / Digital | 0–5 V ratiometric | I/Q baseband | | **Range** | 0–360° / 0–L | −40 to 1000 °C | $\lambda$ 0.7 – ∞ | 0–300 km/h | 10 kPa – 2500 bar | 0.2 – 250 m | | **Bandwidth** | DC – 10 kHz | < 1 Hz thermal | < 10 Hz | 1 Hz – 20 kHz | DC – 10 kHz | DC – 100 MHz | | **Accuracy** | ±0.5° | ±2 °C | ±0.01 λ (WB) | ±1 km/h | ±1% FS | ±0.1 m, ±0.1 m/s | | **Failure Mode** | Open/short | Open (high R) | Slow response | No signal | Stuck at 0/5 V | No echo | | **ECU Interface** | ADC / Timer | ADC + lookup | ADC + lookup | Timer capture / SPI | ADC | SPI / Ethernet | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Sensor-to-ECU Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Physical World
Position Temp Speed
Pressure Lambda Radar] --> B[Sensor Cluster
6 Families] B --> C1[Position Sensors
TPS CKP CMP SAS] B --> C2[Temperature Sensors
ECTS IAT EGT] B --> C3[Lambda Sensor
ZrO2 Narrowband] B --> C4[Speed Sensors
WSS VSS CKP] B --> C5[Pressure Sensors
MAP TPMS Fuel Rail] B --> C6[Radar Sensor
77 GHz FMCW] C1 --> D[Signal Conditioning
Amplify Filter Linearize] C2 --> D C3 --> D C4 --> D C5 --> D C6 --> D D --> E[ADC
12-16 bit] E --> F[Microcontroller
DSP or MCU] F --> G1[Engine ECU] F --> G2[ABS ESC ECU] F --> G3[ADAS ECU] F --> G4[BCM Body] G1 --> H[Actuators
Injector Ignition] G2 --> H G3 --> H G4 --> H ``` ## 4.2 Sensor Classification Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A0[Automotive Sensor] --> B1[Passive Type
No External Power] A0 --> B2[Active Type
Needs Excitation] B1 --> D1[NTC Thermistor
Coolant Air Temp] B1 --> D2[Variable Reluctance
CKP WSS] B1 --> D3[Thermocouple
EGT] B2 --> E1[Wheatstone Bridge
MAP Pressure Strain] B2 --> E2[Hall Effect
CKP WSS Throttle] E2 --> E2a[Digital Switch
or Linear] B2 --> E3[Lambda Pump Cell
Wideband O2] B2 --> E4[mmWave Radar
77 GHz FMCW] E4 --> E4a[Long Range ACC] E4 --> E4b[Short Range BSM] ``` ## 4.3 NTC Thermistor Signal Conditioning Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vref 5V] --> R1[Bias Resistor Rb] R1 --> N1[NTC Rntc] N1 --> A0[GND] R1 --> N2[Node Vout] N2 --> A1[ADC Input
of ECM] A1 --> A2[Lookup Table
T to V] A2 --> A3[Temperature
in Degree C] ``` ## 4.4 Wheatstone Bridge for MAP Sensor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vex 5V] --> B1[R1 Active Tensile] B1 --> N1[Node A] N1 --> B2[R2 Active Compressive] B2 --> A0[GND] A --> B3[R3 Active Compressive] B3 --> N2[Node B] N2 --> B4[R4 Active Tensile] B4 --> A0 N1 --> A1[Differential Amplifier] N2 --> A1 A1 --> A2[Output Vout
Proportional to Pressure] ``` ## 4.5 Radar FMCW Signal Processing Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Synthesizer
Chirp Generator] --> B[PA Power Amplifier] B --> C[Tx Antenna
77 GHz] C --> D[Target
Vehicle Pedestrian] D --> E[Rx Antenna Array] E --> F[LNA
Low Noise Amp] F --> G[Mixer] A --> G G --> H[IF Beat Signal
Baseband I Q] H --> I[ADC 16 bit] I --> J[FFT
Range Doppler Map] J --> K[CFAR Detector
Peak Search] K --> L[Tracking Filter
Kalman] L --> M[Object List
Range Velocity Angle] ``` ## 4.6 Closed-Loop Engine Control with Lambda Sensor ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Driver Throttle
TPS Input] --> B[ECM Controller] C1[MAP Sensor] --> B C2[CKP Sensor] --> B C3[Coolant Temp] --> B D[Lambda Sensor
Exhaust] --> B B --> E[Injector Pulse Width
Calculation] E --> F[Fuel Injector] F --> G[Combustion] G --> H[Exhaust Gas] H --> D D --> I[Lambda Error
Lambda 1] I --> B ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS ### **Q1. Define Lambda sensor and state its location in a vehicle.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Lambda sensor** (also called **Oxygen sensor or O₂ sensor**) is an electrochemical transducer that measures the **oxygen concentration** in the exhaust gas and outputs a voltage signal proportional to the **air-fuel ratio (AFR)** relative to the **stoichiometric ratio (14.7:1)** for gasoline. It is located **in the exhaust manifold** (pre-catalyst, narrowband/wideband) and also **after the three-way catalyst** (post-cat, monitoring catalyst efficiency). The output is fed to the **Engine Control Module (ECM)** for **closed-loop fuel control**, ensuring optimum TWC conversion efficiency and minimum emissions. --- ### **Q2. List any three automotive pressure sensors and their functions.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Manifold Absolute Pressure (MAP) Sensor:** Measures intake manifold pressure (vacuum/boost) for the ECM to compute the **air mass** entering the engine using the speed-density method. 2. **Tire Pressure Monitoring System (TPMS) Sensor:** Mounted inside each wheel; measures individual tire pressure and transmits via **433 MHz / 315 MHz RF** to the BCM, alerting the driver on pressure drop. 3. **Brake Fluid Pressure Sensor:** Located in the ABS/ESC hydraulic unit; provides hydraulic pressure feedback for **anti-lock braking**, **electronic brakeforce distribution (EBD)**, and **autonomous emergency braking (AEB)** actuation. *(Other acceptable: Fuel rail pressure sensor for diesel common-rail, HVAC refrigerant pressure sensor, barometric pressure sensor.)* --- ## PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice Pattern) ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** > **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model Question, 2024 Scheme ESE]** > > **(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the working of a **Variable Reluctance (VR) Crankshaft Position Sensor**. Derive the relationship between output frequency and engine RPM. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the **NTC thermistor-based engine coolant temperature sensor** with its signal conditioning circuit. Show how resistance varies with temperature using the **B-parameter equation** and calculate the resistance at 80 °C for an NTC with $R_0 = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega$ at 25 °C and $B = 3435\,\text{K}$. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) VR CKP Sensor (7 marks)** **Diagram & Construction (2 marks):** A VR sensor consists of a **permanent magnet**, a **soft-iron pole piece**, and a **pickup coil** wound around the pole piece. A **reluctor wheel** (toothed disc) with 60−2 teeth is mounted on the crankshaft. The air gap between the pole piece and the reluctor wheel changes as teeth pass. **Working (3 marks):** As a tooth approaches the pole piece, the **magnetic reluctance** of the flux path decreases, increasing the flux through the coil. As the tooth moves away, reluctance increases and flux decreases. This **changing flux** induces an **AC EMF** in the coil per **Faraday's Law**: $$e = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$$ The output is a **bipolar sinusoidal AC voltage** whose **frequency** is proportional to RPM and **amplitude** is proportional to RPM (since $d\Phi/dt$ increases with rotational speed). The ECM uses the **zero-crossings** to determine crank position and **missing-tooth gap** to identify TDC reference. **Derivation (2 marks):** Number of teeth $N$ pass a fixed point per revolution. At $n$ RPM, revolutions per second = $n/60$. So teeth per second: $$f = \dfrac{n}{60} \cdot N = \dfrac{n \cdot N}{60} \quad \text{(Hz)}$$ For 60−2 wheel at 3000 RPM: $f = (3000/60) \times 58 = 2900$ Hz. **(b) NTC ECTS (7 marks)** **Working & Circuit (3 marks):** An NTC thermistor is a semiconductor whose resistance **decreases** as temperature **increases**. It is connected in a **voltage divider** with a fixed bias resistor $R_b$ across the 5 V reference. The divided voltage is read by the ECM's ADC. A lookup table (or inverse B-equation) converts the voltage to temperature in °C. **B-parameter equation (2 marks):** $$R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$$ **Numerical Solution (2 marks):** - $T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15$ K - $T = 80 + 273.15 = 353.15$ K - $\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0} = \dfrac{1}{353.15} - \dfrac{1}{298.15} = 2.8317 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -5.223 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹ - $B \cdot \Delta(1/T) = 3435 \times (-5.223 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.794$ - $e^{-1.794} = 0.1664$ - $R(80\,°\text{C}) = 2.2\,\text{k}\Omega \times 0.1664 \approx 366\,\Omega$ **Answer:** $R(80\,°\text{C}) \approx 366\,\Omega$ — resistance drops from 2.2 kΩ to ~366 Ω, a ~6× reduction, enabling sensitive cold-start enrichment decisions. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** > **[KTU University Exam — Module 2 Model Question, 2024 Scheme ESE]** > > **(a)** Explain the **FMCW radar principle** used in automotive Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC). Derive the **range equation** and the **range resolution** formula. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** With a block diagram, describe the working of a **piezoresistive MAP sensor** using a **Wheatstone bridge**. Compute the bridge output voltage for $V_{ex} = 5\,\text{V}$, $G = 120$, and $\varepsilon = 0.0008$. **(7 marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **(a) FMCW Radar (7 marks)** **Principle (2 marks):** In Frequency Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) radar, the transmitter emits a **linear chirp** whose frequency ramps linearly from $f_0$ to $f_0 + \Delta f$ over a duration $T_{chirp}$. The **reflected signal** from a target returns after a delay $\tau = 2R/c$. The receiver **mixes** the transmitted and received signals producing an **Intermediate Frequency (IF) / beat signal** at frequency $f_{beat}$. **Range Derivation (3 marks):** The instantaneous frequency difference at any moment is: $$f_{beat}(t) = \dfrac{2R}{c} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta f}{T_{chirp}}$$ So **range**: $$R = \dfrac{f_{beat} \cdot c \cdot T_{chirp}}{2 \cdot \Delta f}$$ **Range Resolution Derivation (2 marks):** Two targets are resolvable if their IF frequencies differ by at least **1 FFT bin = $1/T_{chirp}$**. Thus $\Delta f_{beat} = 1/T_{chirp}$ corresponds to range increment: $$\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot \Delta f \cdot T_{chirp}} \cdot T_{chirp} = \dfrac{c}{2 \cdot B}$$ where $B = \Delta f$ is the chirp bandwidth. Higher bandwidth → finer resolution. **(b) MAP Sensor (7 marks)** **Working (3 marks):** A silicon diaphragm is micromachined with **four piezoresistive strain gauges** in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration. The diaphragm deflects under manifold pressure. Two gauges experience **tension** (R↑) and two experience **compression** (R↓), maximizing bridge sensitivity and providing **temperature compensation** (self-cancelling TCR effects). The bridge is excited by a precision 5 V reference. Differential output is amplified and digitized by the ECM. **Diagram (1 mark)** — see Mermaid block 4.4 above. **Numerical (3 marks):** Given $V_{ex} = 5$ V, $G = 120$, $\varepsilon = 0.0008$. **Step 1 — Relative resistance change.** $$\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon = 120 \times 0.0008 = 0.096$$ **Step 2 — Full-bridge output.** $$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = 5 \times 0.096 = 0.48\,\text{V}$$ **Step 3 — Sensitivity.** $$S = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{ex}} = 96\,\text{mV/V}$$ > **Answer:** $V_{out} = 0.48$ V (raw); after on-chip amplifier (~10×), the ECM sees ~4.8 V (boost condition). --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Pet Peeves):** > 1. **NTC vs PTC confusion** — writing "resistance increases with temperature" for coolant sensor is a **direct 1-mark deduction**. > 2. **Stoichiometric value** — using 14.7:1 for **diesel** is wrong; diesels always run **lean** ($\lambda \approx 1.2 - 1.7$). Stoichiometric diesel AFR is ~14.5:1, but engines never operate there. > 3. **FMCW formulas** — forgetting the factor of **2** in the range equation (signal travels to target and back) leads to a halved range. This single error costs **2 marks**. > 4. **Nernst equation constants** — using $R = 8.314$ vs $R = 8.205 \times 10^{-5}$ m³·atm/(mol·K) without proper unit handling for $P$ (Pa vs atm). The $4F$ (not $2F$) is correct for O₂ sensor. > 5. **Missing units in formula derivations** — examiners strictly deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing Kelvin, Hz, m, Ω labels. > 6. **Wheatstone bridge output** — many students write the half-bridge formula instead of the full-bridge formula. Full bridge doubles the sensitivity — examiners look for this distinction. > 7. **Radar band** — confusing 24 GHz (legacy) with 77 GHz (modern automotive) costs marks. EU/India regulation is **76–81 GHz**. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 15 Bullets Before Walking Into the Exam Hall:** - [x] **Position sensors** = TPS (potentiometric/Hall), CKP/CMP (VR or Hall, 60−2 reluctor), SAS (multi-turn optical). - [x] **CKP frequency formula:** $f = (n \cdot N)/60$ — comes up in every paper. - [x] **Temperature sensor** = **NTC** (resistance drops with heat) — never PTC for coolant/air. - [x] **NTC equation:** $R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{B(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ with $B \approx 3000$–$5000$ K. - [x] **Lambda sensor** measures AFR via **Nernst voltage** across ZrO₂ electrolyte. - [x] **Stoichiometric AFR for gasoline = 14.7:1**; $\lambda = 1$. - [x] **Narrowband O₂** switches between 0.1 V and 0.9 V; **Wideband** uses a pump cell. - [x] **Wheel Speed Sensor** in ABS uses Hall-effect (active) or VR (passive), 48–64 tooth tone ring. - [x] **MAP sensor** = **Wheatstone bridge** on silicon diaphragm, full-bridge, $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R/R$. - [x] **TPMS** uses 433 MHz / 315 MHz RF transmitters; **direct** vs **indirect** methods exist. - [x] **Automotive radar** = **FMCW at 77 GHz** (76–81 GHz band). - [x] **FMCW range equation:** $R = f_{beat} \cdot c \cdot T_{chirp} / (2 \cdot \Delta f)$ — **don't forget the 2**. - [x] **Range resolution:** $\Delta R = c / (2B)$ — larger bandwidth = better resolution. - [x] **Doppler** adds velocity information via $f_D = 2v_r/\lambda$ — enables ACC + AEB. - [x] **Nernst constant** uses $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol, factor **4** (O₂ → 4 electrons).

topics.title: Linear and Rotary Actuators: Principle of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT725 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS IN AUTOMOBILES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Linear and Rotary Actuators: Principle of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Actuators — The "Muscles" of an Embedded Automotive System

In the language of embedded systems, an **actuator** is the *physical output transducer* that converts an electrical control signal from an Electronic Control Unit (ECU) into a precise mechanical motion — either a straight-line push/pull (**linear motion**) or a rotating torque (**rotary motion**). Every modern automobile is essentially a *distributed network of sensors and actuators*, with the ECU (often called the "brain") commanding the actuators (the "muscles") to do useful mechanical work.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Module 2):**
> An *automotive actuator* is an electromechanical, electrohydraulic, or electropneumatic energy-conversion device that receives an electrical command from a microcontroller/ECU and produces a controlled mechanical displacement, force, or torque to actuate engine, chassis, body, or powertrain subsystems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are sitting in a car and you twist the steering wheel. The steering column rotates, but ultimately the **wheels must turn left or right**. Between you and the wheels is a system of rods, hydraulics, or motors — these are the *actuators*. They take your small input (hand motion) and *amplify* it into useful motion (wheel turning).

- **Linear actuator** = a device that *pushes or pulls in a straight line* — like the piston in your leg when you kick a football.
- **Rotary actuator** = a device that *spins* — like the electric motor in a window wiper that goes round and round.

In a car, examples include:
- **Fuel injector solenoid** (linear push)
- **EGR valve** (linear or rotary, opens/closes a passage)
- **Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)** (rotary, opens/closes the throttle plate)
- **Turbocharger VGT vanes** (rotary, adjusts boost)
- **ABS modulator valves** (linear, modulates brake pressure)

### 1.3 The Two Fundamental Categories

| Category | Motion Type | Typical Automotive Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear Actuator** | Straight-line translation along a single axis | Fuel injectors, EGR valves, ABS modulator pistons, headlamp levelers |
| **Rotary Actuator** | Continuous or stepped rotation about an axis | Throttle body, VGT turbo, HVAC blend door, electric power steering, window lift |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU Module 2, the focus is on the *principle of operation* — meaning the student must understand (a) the underlying electromagnetic/electro-mechanical physics, (b) the mathematical force/torque equations, and (c) the typical automotive duty cycle of each actuator class.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics to Remember

- **Magnetic permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$
- **Relative permeability** of ferromagnetic steel armature: $\mu_r \approx 2000\text{–}6000$
- **Standard automotive supply voltage:** **12 V DC** (nominal), 13.8 V (running), 9 V–16 V (operational range)
- **Heavy-duty/HEV supply:** **42 V** or **48 V** mild-hybrid systems
- **Typical switching frequency of PWM actuation:** **100 Hz – 25 kHz**
- **Force unit:** Newton (**N**); Torque unit: Newton-meter (**N·m**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Linear Actuators — Operating Principles

A linear actuator produces a force $F$ along a single axis $x$. The most common automotive linear actuators are **electromagnetic solenoids**.

### 2.1.1 Solenoid Actuator (Single-Coil, Plunger Type)

**Construction:** A cylindrical coil wound on a bobbin; a soft-iron **plunger (armature)** is free to slide along the coil's axis. When the coil is energized, a magnetic flux $\Phi$ is established along the axis. The plunger experiences an axial force that pulls it into the coil (the position of **minimum magnetic reluctance**).

**Why it works:** Ferromagnetic material is attracted toward regions of stronger magnetic field. The magnetic circuit always tries to minimize its stored energy by reducing air-gap reluctance, dragging the plunger inward.

**Force equation for a solenoid:**
$$F = \frac{\Phi^2}{2\mu_0 A} = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 \ell_g^2}$$

Where:
- $F$ = axial force on plunger (N)
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux through the air gap (Wb)
- $N$ = number of turns of the coil
- $I$ = coil current (A)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of the air gap (m²)
- $\ell_g$ = effective air-gap length (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Force in a solenoid is **inversely proportional to the square of the air gap**. This is why solenoid actuators produce a *very large initial pull-in force* but a *rapidly diminishing holding force* as the plunger closes the gap. ECU control algorithms use this in **PWM duty-cycle modulation** — high duty cycle for pull-in, lower duty cycle for hold (to save power and prevent coil overheating).

### 2.1.2 Variable-Reluctance (VR) Linear Actuator

The armature is **not permanently magnetized**. Force arises purely because the magnetic circuit seeks to minimize its reluctance:
$$F = -\frac{1}{2}\Phi^2 \frac{d\mathcal{R}}{dx}$$

Where $\mathcal{R}$ is the magnetic reluctance of the flux path. The negative sign indicates force is directed to reduce reluctance (i.e., close the air gap). This actuator only pulls — it cannot push.

### 2.1.3 Permanent-Magnet (PM) Linear Actuator

A permanent magnet provides the bias flux, and the coil current either reinforces or opposes it, producing **bidirectional force**. These are used in **active suspension systems** and **active engine mounts**.

### 2.1.4 Piezoelectric Linear Actuator

Certain crystals (PZT — lead zirconate titanate) deform proportionally to an applied voltage:
$$\Delta \ell = d_{33} \cdot V \cdot \frac{n}{\ell}$$

Where $d_{33}$ is the piezoelectric strain coefficient (~300 pm/V for PZT). Used in **common-rail diesel injectors** for nanosecond-precision pilot injection.

### 2.1.5 Hydraulic and Pneumatic Linear Actuators

These are **fluid-power** devices. A pressurized fluid drives a piston inside a cylinder.

$$F = P \cdot A_p - P_0 \cdot A_r$$

Where $P$ is supply pressure, $P_0$ is return-side pressure, $A_p$ is piston head area, $A_r$ is rod-side annular area. Used in **brake boosters**, **active suspension**, and **diesel fuel injection**.

## 2.2 Rotary Actuators — Operating Principles

A rotary actuator produces torque $T$ about a shaft axis. The fundamental electromagnetic torque equation is shared by all electromagnetic rotary machines:

$$\boxed{T = k_T \cdot \Phi \cdot I}$$

Where:
- $T$ = electromagnetic torque (N·m)
- $k_T$ = machine constant (depends on geometry)
- $\Phi$ = air-gap flux per pole (Wb)
- $I$ = armature current (A)

### 2.2.1 DC Brushed Motor

A commutator mechanically switches current in the armature windings. The commutator–brush assembly ensures torque is always unidirectional. Speed is proportional to voltage; torque is proportional to current.

$$V = E_b + I_a R_a, \quad E_b = k_E \Phi \omega, \quad T = k_T \Phi I_a$$

Used in: **window lift, wiper, HVAC blower, seat adjusters**.

### 2.2.2 Brushless DC (BLDC) Motor

The rotor carries permanent magnets; the stator carries three-phase windings. An **electronic commutator** (inverter + position sensor — Hall, resolver, or sensorless BEMF) replaces the mechanical commutator. Used in: **electric power steering (EPS), radiator fan, water pump, hybrid traction motors**.

### 2.2.3 Stepper Motor

Moves in discrete angular steps when pulsed. **Step angle:**
$$\theta_s = \frac{360°}{N_{steps}}$$

Two main types:
- **Variable-reluctance (VR) stepper:** rotor is a soft-iron toothed wheel; aligns to minimum-reluctance position.
- **Permanent-magnet (PM) stepper:** rotor carries magnets; holds position without excitation (detent torque).

Used in: **instrument clusters, HVAC blend/ mode doors, idle speed control (older systems)**.

### 2.2.4 Servo Motor

A DC/BLDC motor + position sensor (potentiometer/encoder) + closed-loop controller. The ECU commands a position; the controller drives the motor until the sensor feedback matches. Used in: **electronic throttle body, electronic stability control valve, VGT vane positioning**.

### 2.2.5 Reluctance (Switched-Reluctance) Motor

Torque is produced purely by reluctance variation; no magnets, no rotor windings. Robust, fault-tolerant — used in some **aero-engine fuel pumps and EV traction** applications.

### 2.2.6 Hydraulic Rotary Actuator (Limited-Angle Torque Motor / Vane Actuator)

Pressurized fluid acting on a vane produces torque:
$$T = \Delta P \cdot A_{vane} \cdot r$$

Used in heavy machinery and some **automatic transmission clutch packs**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick Reference Table

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Solenoid axial force | $F = \dfrac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 \ell_g^2}$ | N | Pull-type solenoid force |
| 2 | Variable reluctance force | $F = -\dfrac{1}{2}\Phi^2 \dfrac{d\mathcal{R}}{dx}$ | N | General co-energy method |
| 3 | Magnetic reluctance | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{\ell}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | A/Wb (H⁻¹) | Magnetic Ohm's law |
| 4 | Hydraulic cylinder force | $F = P A_p - P_0 A_r$ | N | Double-acting cylinder |
| 5 | Motor torque constant | $T = k_T \Phi I$ | N·m | All DC-style machines |
| 6 | Back-EMF | $E_b = k_E \Phi \omega$ | V | Limits no-load speed |
| 7 | Stepper step angle | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N_{steps}}$ | degrees | Discrete positioning |
| 8 | Piezoelectric strain | $\Delta \ell = d_{33} \cdot \dfrac{V n}{\ell}$ | m | Common-rail piezo injector |
| 9 | Hydraulic vane torque | $T = \Delta P \cdot A_{vane} \cdot r$ | N·m | Rotary hydraulic actuator |
| 10 | Force → Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Convert actuator force |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - Formula 1 is the workhorse of *all fuel injector design*.
> - Formula 5 + 6 govern *every electric drive in the car* — from a tiny mirror motor to a 200 kW EV traction motor.
> - Formula 8 is what enables modern *diesel common-rail* engines to meet BS-VI / Euro-VI emission norms by allowing multiple precisely-timed pilot injections per combustion cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Solenoid Force from Energy Principles

**Goal:** Derive the force equation $F = \dfrac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2\ell_g^2}$ using magnetic co-energy.

### Step 1 — Define the magnetic circuit

A solenoid with $N$ turns carrying current $I$ establishes an MMF (magnetomotive force):
$$\mathcal{F} = NI \quad \text{(ampere-turns, A·t)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Hopkinson's Law (Magnetic Ohm's Law)

The total reluctance of the magnetic circuit is dominated by the air gap (since $\mu_r$ of iron is very large):
$$\mathcal{R} = \frac{\ell_{iron}}{\mu_0 \mu_r A} + \frac{\ell_g}{\mu_0 A} \approx \frac{\ell_g}{\mu_0 A}$$

The approximation is justified because $\mu_r \gg 1$, so the iron path reluctance is negligible.

### Step 3 — Compute the magnetic flux

$$\Phi = \frac{\mathcal{F}}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{NI \, \mu_0 A}{\ell_g}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the magnetic co-energy stored

For a linear magnetic system, co-energy equals stored energy:
$$W'_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} \mathcal{F} \Phi = \frac{1}{2} (NI)\Phi$$

Substituting flux:
$$W'_{mag} = \frac{1}{2} (NI)\left(\frac{NI \, \mu_0 A}{\ell_g}\right) = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 \ell_g}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Principle of Virtual Work

Force is the negative gradient of co-energy with respect to displacement $x$ (which changes the air-gap length $\ell_g$):

$$F = +\frac{\partial W'_{mag}}{\partial \ell_g}\bigg|_{\Phi=\text{const}}$$

Wait — careful with sign convention. The co-energy increases with *air-gap length* increase; but the actuator force acts to *reduce* the air gap. Therefore:

$$F = \frac{\partial W'_{mag}}{\partial (\text{gap-reduction})} = -\frac{\partial W'_{mag}}{\partial \ell_g}$$

Taking derivative of $W'_{mag} = \dfrac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2\ell_g}$ with respect to $\ell_g$:

$$\frac{\partial W'_{mag}}{\partial \ell_g} = -\frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 \ell_g^2}$$

Therefore the *attractive* force on the plunger (in the direction that reduces the gap) is:

$$\boxed{F = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2\ell_g^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** Force is **quadratic in current** → a small current increase produces a disproportionately large force increase. This is why solenoid pull-in is so snappy, and why **PWM duty cycle** is used to limit holding current and prevent coil burnout.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of DC Motor Steady-State Torque–Speed Characteristic

**Given:** A permanent-magnet DC motor with armature resistance $R_a$, torque constant $k_T$, back-EMF constant $k_E$, supply voltage $V$, and load torque $T_L$.

### Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law to the armature loop

$$V = I_a R_a + E_b$$

### Step 2 — Substitute back-EMF expression

Back-EMF is proportional to angular velocity:
$$E_b = k_E \Phi \omega$$

Since $k_T = k_E$ in SI units (a fundamental result from energy conservation):
$$E_b = k_T \omega$$

So:
$$V = I_a R_a + k_T \omega$$

### Step 3 — Solve for armature current as a function of speed

$$I_a = \frac{V - k_T \omega}{R_a}$$

### Step 4 — Apply electromagnetic torque equation

$$T = k_T I_a = k_T \cdot \frac{V - k_T \omega}{R_a}$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange into the standard torque-speed linear form

$$\boxed{T = \frac{k_T V}{R_a} - \frac{k_T^{\,2}}{R_a}\,\omega}$$

This is the famous **linear torque-speed characteristic** of a DC motor:
- At $\omega = 0$ (stall): $T_{stall} = \dfrac{k_T V}{R_a}$
- At $T = 0$ (no load): $\omega_{no\text{-}load} = \dfrac{V}{k_T}$
- Slope is negative: $\dfrac{dT}{d\omega} = -\dfrac{k_T^2}{R_a}$ — *softer* motor with higher $R_a$.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Linear vs. Rotary Actuators

| Parameter | Linear Actuator | Rotary Actuator |
|---|---|---|
| Output variable | Force $F$ (N), Stroke $x$ (mm) | Torque $T$ (N·m), Angle $\theta$ (rad) |
| Governing physics | $F = \frac{\Phi^2}{2\mu_0 A}$ (reluctance) | $T = k_T \Phi I$ (Lorentz/co-energy) |
| Typical stroke / range | 1 mm – 500 mm | Unlimited rotation (continuous) or limited-angle |
| Energy density | Lower per unit volume | Higher per unit volume |
| Automotive examples | Fuel injector, ABS valve, headlamp leveler | Throttle body, EPS motor, fan, wiper |
| Position feedback needed? | Often (closed loop) | Almost always (encoder/potentiometer) |
| Heat dissipation path | Through coil bobbin → housing | Through stator laminations → housing |
| Failure mode | Coil open/short, plunger seizure | Bearing wear, brush wear (brushed), magnet demag |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A solenoid actuator has $N = 500$ turns, air-gap cross-section $A = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2$, and air-gap length $\ell_g = 2\text{ mm}$. The coil current is $I = 2\text{ A}$. Calculate the pull force.

**Solution:**

$$F = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2\ell_g^2}$$

Substituting:
$$F = \frac{(500 \times 2)^2 \times (4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-4})}{2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^2}$$

Numerator: $(1000)^2 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$
$$= 10^6 \times 4\pi \times 1.5 \times 10^{-11}$$
$$= 6\pi \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\approx 1.885 \times 10^{-4}$$

Denominator: $2 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$

$$F = \frac{1.885 \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 23.56 \text{ N}$$

> **Answer:** $F \approx 23.6$ N — sufficient to actuate a typical fuel injector pintle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Automotive Actuator Channel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ECU[ECU Microcontroller] -->|PWM Command Signal| DRV[MOSFET / H-Bridge Driver]
    DRV -->|Current / Voltage| ACT[Linear or Rotary Actuator]
    ACT -->|Mechanical Motion| LOAD[Engine Chassis or Body Subsystem]
    SENS[Position / Current / Temperature Sensor] --> FB[Feedback Signal]
    FB --> ECU
    ACT -.->|Back EMF or Shaft Sensor| SENS
```

**Block description:**
- **ECU** generates a low-power command (e.g., PWM at 1 kHz, duty 0–100%).
- **DRV** is a power stage (MOSFET half-bridge, H-bridge, or relay).
- **ACT** is the physical linear or rotary actuator.
- **LOAD** is the device being moved (valve, throttle plate, injector needle).
- **SENS** closes the loop — current shunt, Hall sensor, potentiometer, or resolver.

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Actuator Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Automotive Actuators]
    ROOT --> LIN[Linear Actuators]
    ROOT --> ROT[Rotary Actuators]

    LIN --> SOL[Solenoid Plunger]
    LIN --> VR[Variable Reluctance]
    LIN --> PM[Permanent Magnet]
    LIN --> PZT[Piezoelectric Stack]
    LIN --> HYD[Hydraulic Piston]

    SOL --> APP1[Fuel Injector EGR Valve ABS Modulator]
    PZT --> APP2[Common Rail Diesel Pilot Injection]
    HYD --> APP3[Brake Booster Active Suspension]

    ROT --> DCB[DC Brushed]
    ROT --> BLD[Brushless DC BLDC]
    ROT --> STP[Stepper Motor]
    ROT --> SRV[Servo Motor]
    ROT --> SR[Switched Reluctance]
    ROT --> HVA[Hydraulic Vane]

    DCB --> USE1[Wiper Window Lift HVAC Blower]
    BLD --> USE2[EPS Motor Radiator Fan Hybrid Traction]
    STP --> USE3[Instrument Cluster HVAC Blend Door]
    SRV --> USE4[Electronic Throttle Body VGT Vane ESC Valve]
```

---

## 4.3 Solenoid Operating States — Pull-In vs. Holding

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Deenergized
    Deenergized --> PullIn: ECU applies high PWM duty e.g. 90 percent
    PullIn --> Holding: Plunger seated gap small
    Holding --> Deenergized: ECU removes drive
    Holding --> PullIn: Load disturbance exceeds holding force
    note right of PullIn: Current ILarge Flux high Force maximum coil heating risk
    note right of Holding: Current low via PWM duty reduction coil cool reliable hold
```

**Operating states:**
- **Pull-In:** Large current → large flux → large force to overcome return spring and inertia.
- **Holding:** Reduced current (via PWM duty cycle drop) → just enough force to hold plunger seated, minimizes I²R heating.

---

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Servo Actuator Block Diagram (e.g., Electronic Throttle Body)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REF[Driver Demand Pedal Position] --> SUM[Comparator]
    SUM -->|Error e| PID[PID Controller in ECU]
    PID --> PWM[PWM Duty Generator]
    PWM --> HBRIDGE[H-Bridge Motor Driver]
    HBRIDGE --> DCM[DC Motor coupled to Throttle Plate]
    DCM --> THROTTLE[Throttle Butterfly Valve]
    THROTTLE --> INTAKE[Intake Manifold Airflow]
    DCM -.->|Shaft Position via Dual TPS| SUM
```

This is the **classical servo loop** for any automotive rotary actuator (ETB, VGT, EGR, electronic valve timing). The two-track TPS (Throttle Position Sensor) provides redundant feedback for ASIL-D functional safety.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Distinguish between a linear actuator and a rotary actuator. Give one automotive example of each.

**Model Answer:**
A **linear actuator** produces force and displacement along a single straight-line axis, while a **rotary actuator** produces torque and angular displacement about a shaft axis. Linear actuators operate on the principle of magnetic reluctance minimization or fluid pressure acting on a piston; rotary actuators operate on Lorentz-force or co-energy principles producing torque $T = k_T \Phi I$. *Example:* Linear — **fuel injector solenoid** (moves a needle valve axially). Rotary — **electronic throttle body motor** (rotates the butterfly plate through a gear train).

*(3 marks — definition 1, distinction 1, example 1)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is PWM control used to drive an automotive solenoid instead of a constant DC voltage?

**Model Answer:**
A solenoid's pull force is **proportional to the square of current** ($F \propto I^2$). If held at full DC voltage, the continuous high current would (i) overheat and burn the coil, (ii) waste power, and (iii) provide unnecessary holding force. **PWM (Pulse-Width Modulation)** at a fixed high frequency with a variable duty cycle allows the ECU to: (a) supply **high duty cycle (~90%)** during *pull-in* to generate maximum force, and (b) reduce the duty cycle to **~20–40%** during *holding*, which reduces average current and I²R losses while still maintaining the seated plunger position. This gives **fast response + low thermal stress** — the hallmark of automotive solenoid control.

*(3 marks — reason for PWM: 2 marks; thermal/efficiency benefit: 1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - When asked about a "principle of operation," students often **describe construction only** and lose 4–5 marks. Always pair construction with the **underlying physics** (force/torque equation + co-energy + working cycle).
> - **Do not** omit a labeled sketch — even a simple labeled block diagram earns 2 marks.
> - **Never** write the formula without defining each symbol. Examiners explicitly check for "define $N$, $I$, $\Phi$, $\ell_g$, $A$" and deduct marks if symbols are used without definition.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, explain the **principle of operation of a single-coil plunger-type solenoid actuator** as used in an automotive fuel injector. Derive the force equation and discuss why PWM is used for actuation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the construction and working of a **Brushless DC (BLDC) motor** used in an Electric Power Steering (EPS) system. Compare its advantages over a brushed DC motor in automotive applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

1. **Construction** (2 marks): The solenoid consists of a helical copper coil wound on a non-magnetic bobbin, surrounding a movable soft-iron plunger. The plunger is biased outward by a return spring. Current enters via ECU-controlled MOSFET.

2. **Working Principle** (2 marks): When the ECU drives current $I$ through $N$ turns, MMF $= NI$ establishes flux $\Phi = NI\mu_0 A/\ell_g$. The resulting co-energy gradient creates an axial force $F$ pulling the plunger inward, lifting the needle valve and allowing fuel injection.

3. **Force derivation** (2 marks):
$$F = \frac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 \ell_g^2}$$

Derivation is shown in full in Section 3.1.

4. **PWM rationale** (1 mark): High current for pull-in, low duty cycle for hold → reduces I²R heating, prevents coil burnout, gives millisecond response.

#### Model Solution — (b)

1. **BLDC Construction** (2 marks): Three-phase stator windings on laminated silicon-steel core; rotor carries high-energy permanent magnets (NdFeB). Position sensed by three Hall sensors mounted 120° apart on the stator.

2. **Working Principle** (3 marks): The ECU (via inverter) energizes two stator phases at a time in a sequence determined by Hall-sensor feedback. The energized phase produces a rotating stator field that "drags" the rotor magnet. Torque $T = k_T \Phi I$ is unidirectional. The electronic commutation replaces mechanical brushes.

3. **Advantages over brushed DC** (2 marks):
- **Higher efficiency** (no brush friction, no commutator losses)
- **Longer life** (no brush wear)
- **Better thermal management** (heat in stator, easy to cool)
- **Higher power density**
- **Lower EMI / acoustic noise**
- **Suitable for safety-critical** EPS (ASIL-D capable)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a block diagram, describe the **principle of operation of an Electronic Throttle Body (ETB)** and explain how closed-loop servo control maintains the commanded throttle angle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle of operation of a piezoelectric actuator** in a common-rail diesel injector. State two advantages over a solenoid injector. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

1. **Block diagram** (2 marks): The ETB consists of a DC motor (often BLDC), gear train, butterfly throttle plate, return spring, and a dual-track Throttle Position Sensor (TPS). Refer to Section 4.4 block diagram.

2. **Working** (3 marks): Driver pedal position is read by an Accelerator Pedal Position Sensor (APPS). The ECU computes a *desired* throttle angle and compares with the actual TPS reading. The error drives a PID controller that outputs a PWM command to the H-bridge driver. The motor rotates the plate until error → 0. A return spring provides fail-safe default closure if power is lost.

3. **Closed-loop control** (2 marks): The dual-TPS gives redundant position feedback for functional safety (ISO 26262, ASIL-D). Two TPS signals track each other; any divergence > threshold triggers an *engine limp-home* mode.

#### Model Solution — (b)

1. **Piezo principle** (3 marks): A stack of PZT (lead zirconate titanate) ceramic disks expands by $\Delta \ell = d_{33} V n/\ell$ when a voltage is applied (inverse piezoelectric effect). In a common-rail injector, the stack is mechanically coupled via a hydraulic servo amplifier to the needle valve. Rapid stack expansion pressurizes the hydraulic fluid, lifting the needle within ~100 microseconds.

2. **Working cycle** (2 marks): ECU charges piezo capacitor (~1 ms). Stack expands. Hydraulic coupler amplifies stroke (~10×). Needle opens. Multiple pilot injections per cycle are possible due to sub-millisecond response.

3. **Advantages over solenoid** (2 marks):
- **Faster response** (~100 µs vs. ~300 µs) → enables 5–9 injection events per combustion cycle.
- **Lower power consumption** at hold (negligible leakage current, no I²R loss).
- (Bonus) Better atomization → lower emissions, lower BSFC.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

- [x] An **actuator** converts ECU electrical signals → mechanical motion (linear or rotary).
- [x] **Linear actuator force**: $F = \dfrac{(NI)^2 \mu_0 A}{2 \ell_g^2}$ — quadratic in current, inverse-square in gap.
- [x] **Rotary actuator torque**: $T = k_T \Phi I$ — Lorentz/co-energy principle.
- [x] **Motor torque-speed linearity**: $T = \dfrac{k_T V}{R_a} - \dfrac{k_T^2}{R_a}\omega$.
- [x] **Solenoid pull-in** uses high PWM duty; **holding** uses low duty → prevents coil burnout.
- [x] **Brushed DC** has commutator; **BLDC** has electronic commutation via Hall sensors → higher efficiency.
- [x] **Stepper motors** move in discrete steps ($\theta_s = 360°/N_{steps}$), used for position-critical low-power applications.
- [x] **Piezo actuators** use inverse piezoelectric effect; used in **common-rail diesel** for multi-pulse injection.
- [x] **Electronic Throttle Body** is a closed-loop servo system with dual-TPS redundancy (ASIL-D).
- [x] **Hydraulic linear actuators**: $F = P A_p - P_0 A_r$.
- [x] Always define symbols when writing formulas — examiners deduct marks for undefined variables.
- [x] Always include a labeled sketch or block diagram — easy 2 marks.
- [x] Always discuss **failure mode and safety** for automotive actuators — KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes functional safety.
- [x] Operating voltages: **12 V** (conventional), **48 V** (mild hybrid), **400–800 V** (EV traction).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT725 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS IN AUTOMOBILES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Mapping)**
> A **Piezoelectric Actuator** is a solid-state electromechanical transducer that converts an applied electrical potential (voltage) into precise, controllable mechanical displacement, force, or vibration by exploiting the **converse (inverse) piezoelectric effect** exhibited by certain non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials (most commonly Lead Zirconate Titanate — PZT).

In automotive embedded systems, piezoelectric actuators are the "silent, ultra-fast muscles" of the modern vehicle. Unlike solenoids that move a plunger by magnetic force, a piezo actuator moves by physically *stretching* its crystalline lattice the instant a voltage is applied — producing sub-millisecond response times that are unattainable with conventional electromagnetic actuators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Squeeze-Grip Cup"**
> Imagine a hollow ceramic coffee cup that looks perfectly rigid. If you wire two electrodes to its walls and apply **100 Volts DC**, the cup becomes infinitesimally *taller and thinner* (atomic lattice distortion along the poling axis). Increase the voltage, and it stretches further. Reverse the polarity, and it *contracts*. A piezo actuator stacks thousands of such "cups" in series so that their tiny individual strains add up to a useful, macroscopic push — typically **10 µm to 50 µm** of stroke under automotive drive voltages of **100 V to 200 V** (amplified to **~1000 V** for heavy-duty injector stacks). The displacement is *proportional*, *reversible*, and *near-instantaneous* (response in microseconds).

### 1.1 Why Piezoelectrics Matter in Modern Automobiles

The automotive industry adopted piezoelectric actuators because they outperform solenoids in three critical dimensions:

- **Speed:** Sub-millisecond actuation enables multiple injections per combustion cycle (up to **9 injections/stroke** in modern piezo Common-Rail Diesel engines).
- **Precision:** Sub-micron positional resolution enables precise valve-lift control and nanopositioning of fuel jets.
- **Energy Efficiency:** They consume current only during the *transition* between states, holding position with virtually zero holding power (capacitive holding).

### 1.2 The Two Halves of Piezoelectricity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Coupled Phenomena (must be memorized for KTU)**
> 1. **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** — *Mechanical stress → Electrical charge* (used in **sensors** like knock sensors, accelerometers, and tire-pressure sensors).
> 2. **Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect** — *Applied electric field → Mechanical strain* (used in **actuators** like fuel injectors and active mounts).
>
> Mathematically: Both effects are governed by the **same third-order piezoelectric tensor $d_{ij}$** (in units of **C/N** or **m/V**).

### 1.3 Standard Piezoelectric Materials Used in Cars

| Material | Typical $d_{33}$ (pm/V) | Automotive Role |
|---|---|---|
| **PZT (Lead Zirconate Titanate)** | **300 – 600** | Fuel injectors, diesel Common-Rail |
| **PVDF (Polymer film)** | ~ 30 | Seat-belt buzzers, cabin sensors |
| **Quartz (SiO₂)** | 2.3 | Knock sensors, reference oscillators |
| **PMN-PT (Relaxor single crystal)** | **> 2000** | Premium active-suspension prototypes |

> [!NOTE]
