  - CAN bus transmission of broadcast messages (e.g., engine speed, coolant temp).

### 2.2 Core Control Strategies

#### A. Open-Loop Cranking Fueling
During cold start, the **Coolant Temperature Sensor (CTS)** output is used as a lookup index into a pre-calibrated *Enrichment vs. Coolant Temp* map. The base pulse width is multiplied by the enrichment factor (typically **1.5 to 3.0×** stoichiometric).

#### B. Closed-Loop Lambda Control (PI / PID)
After warm-up, the **Heated Exhaust Gas Oxygen (HEGO)** sensor (narrow-band or wide-band) provides real-time feedback. A discrete **PI controller** drives the Short-Term Fuel Trim (STFT) integrator.

$$STFT_{k+1} = STFT_k + K_i \cdot e_{\lambda}(k) + K_p \cdot \left[ e_{\lambda}(k) - e_{\lambda}(k-1) \right]$$

where $e_{\lambda}(k) = \lambda_{target} - \lambda_{measured}$.

#### C. Adaptive Long-Term Fuel Trim (LTFT)
LTFT is the **slow integrator** that compensates for permanent hardware drift (injector aging, vacuum leaks, MAF contamination). LTFT is stored in **EEPROM / KAM (Keep Alive Memory)** and survives key-off cycles.

#### D. Torque-Based Architecture (Modern PCMs)
Modern OEM PCMs (e.g., Bosch MED17, Continental SIM271, Delphi Multec) are **torque-resolved**: every subsystem (throttle, spark, VVT, transmission) requests a torque value, and the PCM arbitrates the *minimum* and *maximum* achievable torques to prevent conflicts.

### 2.3 Safety and Diagnostic Features

- **Limp-Home Mode:** On detection of a Class-A fault (e.g., MAF failure), the PCM substitutes a default value and illuminates the **MIL (Malfunction Indicator Lamp)** per OBD-II standards.
- **Watchdog Timer:** An independent hardware watchdog (e.g., inside **MPC564 or S32K3** MCU) must be refreshed every **<50 ms**, else the PCM resets into a *fail-silent* state.
- **OBD-II Readiness Monitors:** Continuous (Misfire, Fuel System), and once-per-trip (Catalyst, Evaporative System, O₂ Sensor, EGR, etc.).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description / Use Case | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $AFR = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | Air-Fuel Ratio by mass | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Lambda (excess air ratio) | $\lambda < 1$ rich, $\lambda = 1$ stoich, $\lambda > 1$ lean |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \eta_v \cdot \dfrac{N_{rpm}}{120}$ | Air mass flow (4-stroke) | kg/s, $\eta_v \approx$ **0.85 to 0.98** |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$ | Required fuel mass flow | kg/s |
| 5 | $t_{pw} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\rho_{fuel} \cdot \dot{Q}_{inj}}$ | Injector pulse width (base) | s |
| 6 | $t_{pw, comp} = t_{pw} \cdot f(V_{batt}) \cdot f(T_{fuel}) + t_{dead}$ | Compensated pulse width | s, $f(V_{batt})$ typically **0.6 to 1.0** |
| 7 | $\theta_{total} = \theta_{base}(RPM, MAP) + \Delta\theta_{cool} - \Delta\theta_{knock} + \Delta\theta_{air}$ | Spark advance schedule | degrees BTDC |
| 8 | $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + K_d \left[ e(k) - e(k-1) \right]$ | Discrete PID (positional) | digital, sampled at $T_s$ |
| 9 | $t_{BIT} = t_{Sync} + t_{Prop} + t_{Phase1} + t_{Phase2}$ | CAN nominal bit time | s, sample point **75 % to 87.5 %** |
| 10 | $\omega_e = \dfrac{2 \pi N_{rpm}}{60}$ | Engine angular speed | rad/s |
| 11 | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{P_{brake}}{\dot{m}_{fuel} \cdot LHV}$ | Brake thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} \approx$ **25 % to 38 %** (SI), **35 % to 45 %** (CI) |
| 12 | $BSFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{P_{brake}}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption | g/kWh, best $\approx$ **220 g/kWh** (diesel) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to derive *fuel pulse width*, always include the **battery voltage compensation** term $f(V_{batt})$ — most students lose 2 marks by omitting it.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Fuel Injection Pulse Width with Full Compensation

The injector pulse width $t_{pw}$ is the fundamental output variable of the PCM's fueling strategy. We derive it step by step.

**Step 1 — Compute the volume of air inducted per engine cycle.**

For a **4-stroke** engine, one working cycle occurs every **2 revolutions**. The mass of air inducted per cycle is:

$$m_{air, cycle} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \eta_v$$

where $V_{disp}$ is the total displacement and $\eta_v$ is the volumetric efficiency.

**Step 2 — Convert to per-second air mass flow using engine speed.**

The number of working cycles per second is $\dfrac{N_{rpm}}{2 \cdot 60}$. Hence:

$$\dot{m}_{air} = m_{air, cycle} \cdot \dfrac{N_{rpm}}{2 \cdot 60} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \eta_v \cdot \dfrac{N_{rpm}}{120}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required fuel mass flow for the target lambda.**

$$\dot{m}_{fuel, target} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$$

Substituting Step 2:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel, target} = \dfrac{\rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \eta_v \cdot N_{rpm}}{120 \cdot \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the volumetric fuel flow rate through the injector.**

If the injector has a rated flow rate $\dot{Q}_{inj}$ (kg/s) at a calibration pressure differential $\Delta p_{cal}$, then:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel, actual} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot \dot{Q}_{inj} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta p_{rail}}{\Delta p_{cal}}}$$

where $\Delta p_{rail} = p_{rail} - p_{manifold}$.

**Step 5 — Compute the uncorrected pulse width.**

The injector is energized for a duration $t_{pw}$ to deliver the target mass. Setting $\dot{m}_{fuel, target} = \dot{m}_{fuel, actual}$:

$$t_{pw, base} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel, target}}{\rho_{fuel} \cdot \dot{Q}_{inj} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta p_{rail}}{\Delta p_{cal}}}}$$

**Step 6 — Apply compensations.**

Real injectors exhibit non-linear opening latency vs. battery voltage and fuel temperature. The compensated pulse width becomes:

$$t_{pw, final} = t_{pw, base} \cdot f_{V_{batt}}(V_{batt}) \cdot f_{T_{fuel}}(T_{fuel}) + t_{dead, time}$$

where:
- $f_{V_{batt}}$ is a lookup map, typically **0.6 (at 8 V) to 1.0 (at 14 V)**.
- $t_{dead, time}$ is the *injector dead time* (electromagnet pull-in delay), typically **0.5 ms to 1.5 ms**.

**Final Compiled Equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{pw, final} &= \left[ \dfrac{\rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \eta_v \cdot N_{rpm}}{120 \cdot \lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich} \cdot \rho_{fuel} \cdot \dot{Q}_{inj} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta p_{rail}}{\Delta p_{cal}}}} \right] \cdot f_{V_{batt}} \cdot f_{T_{fuel}} + t_{dead}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Derivation 2: Discrete PID Controller for Idle Speed Control

Idle speed control is a canonical closed-loop task. The PCM must regulate $N_{rpm}$ to a target $N_{target}$ by adjusting the Idle Air Control (IAC) valve duty cycle $u(k)$.

**Step 1 — Define the error signal.**

$$e(k) = N_{target}(k) - N_{actual}(k)$$

**Step 2 — Formulate the proportional term.**

$$u_P(k) = K_p \cdot e(k)$$

**Step 3 — Formulate the integral term (velocity / incremental form for anti-windup safety).**

$$u_I(k) = u_I(k-1) + K_i \cdot T_s \cdot e(k-1)$$

where $T_s$ is the controller sample time (typically **10 ms** for idle).

**Step 4 — Formulate the derivative term (using backward difference for noise rejection).**

$$u_D(k) = \dfrac{K_d}{T_s} \cdot \left[ e(k) - e(k-1) \right]$$

**Step 5 — Sum to produce the control output (incremental form).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta u(k) &= K_p \left[ e(k) - e(k-1) \right] + K_i T_s \cdot e(k) + \dfrac{K_d}{T_s} \left[ e(k) - 2 e(k-1) + e(k-2) \right] \\
u(k) &= u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply saturation and anti-windup clamping.**

$$u(k) = \text{clamp}(u(k), \ u_{min}, \ u_{max})$$

with $u_{min}=0\%$, $u_{max}=100\%$ duty cycle for the IAC valve.

> **Engineering Insight:** The Ziegler-Nichols ultimate-gain method is used during PCM calibration: $K_p$ is increased until sustained oscillation of $N_{rpm}$ is observed, then $K_u$ and $T_u$ are recorded, and final gains are computed as $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $K_i = 1.2 K_u / T_u$, $K_d = 0.075 K_u T_u$.

---

### Derivation 3: Spark Advance Schedule

The PCM determines the ignition spark advance $\theta_{total}$ (degrees before top dead center) as the sum of a base value (lookup table) and several compensation terms.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{total} &= \theta_{base}(RPM, MAP) \\
&\quad + \Delta\theta_{cool}(T_{cool}) \\
&\quad - \Delta\theta_{knock}(\text{Knock Sensor Output}) \\
&\quad + \Delta\theta_{air}(T_{intake}) \\
&\quad - \Delta\theta_{DTCC}(detonation)
\end{aligned}
$$

- $\theta_{base}(RPM, MAP)$: Read from a 2-D calibrated map indexed by engine speed and manifold absolute pressure.
- $\Delta\theta_{cool}$: Cold-engine advance enrichment (typically **+5° to +15°**).
- $\Delta\theta_{knock}$: **Per-cylinder** retard triggered by the knock sensor above a threshold (typically **-3° to -12°**).
- $\Delta\theta_{air}$: Cold-air density correction (often **+2° per 10 °C below 25 °C**).

The final value is then constrained to lie within mechanical limits:

$$\theta_{total} \in [\theta_{min}, \theta_{max}]$$

with $\theta_{min} = -10°$ ATDC (retarded for emissions) and $\theta_{max} = +50°$ BTDC (MBT advance).

---

### Derivation 4: CAN Bus Nominal Bit Time

The CAN protocol partitions each bit into four non-overlapping segments. The bit time is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{BIT} &= t_{SYNC\_SEG} + t_{PROP\_SEG} + t_{PHASE\_SEG1} + t_{PHASE\_SEG2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **sample point** is defined as:

$$SP = \dfrac{t_{SYNC\_SEG} + t_{PROP\_SEG} + t_{PHASE\_SEG1}}{t_{BIT}} \times 100\%$$

For Classical CAN, the ISO 11898-1 recommended sample point is **75 % to 87.5 %**.

The Time Quantum (TQ) is derived from the CAN controller clock $f_{CLK}$:

$$t_{TQ} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}} \cdot BRP$$

with **BRP (Baud Rate Prescaler)** typically **1 to 64**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block-Level Functional Architecture of the PCM

The following diagram illustrates the complete signal-flow architecture from sensors → microcontroller → actuators → network, as examined in KTU 2024 Scheme questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PCM_S1[Engine Speed MAP Sensor Cluster]
    PCM_S2[Mass Air Flow MAF Sensor]
    PCM_S3[Crankshaft Position CKP Sensor]
    PCM_S4[Camshaft Position CMP Sensor]
    PCM_S5[Throttle Position TPS Sensor]
    PCM_S6[Oxygen O2 Lambda Sensor]
    PCM_S7[Coolant Temperature Sensor]
    PCM_S8[Knock Sensor]
    PCM_S9[Vehicle Speed Sensor]
    PCM_S10[Transmission Speed Sensor]
    PCM_S11[Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor]
    PCM_ADC[ADC and Signal Conditioning Front End]
    PCM_DIGIN[Digital Inputs CKP CMP TDC Index]
    PCM_CPU[Microcontroller Core 32 bit e g NXP S32K3]
    PCM_ROM[ROM Flash Calibrated Maps]
    PCM_RAM[RAM Working Memory]
    PCM_EEPROM[EEPROM Adaptive Learn KAM]
    PCM_TIMER[Hardware Timers eTPU]
    PCM_WDT[Hardware Watchdog Timer]
    PCM_CAN[CAN Bus Controller Module]
    PCM_TRX[CAN Transceiver TLE7250]
    PCM_DRV1[Fuel Injector Driver Stage]
    PCM_DRV2[Ignition Coil Driver IGBT]
    PCM_DRV3[Electronic Throttle Driver PWM]
    PCM_DRV4[VVT Variable Valve Timing Driver]
    PCM_DRV5[Transmission Shift Solenoid Drivers]
    PCM_DRV6[Fuel Pump Relay Control]
    PCM_ACT1[Fuel Injectors]

    PCM_ACT3[Electronic Throttle Body]

    PCM_ACT5[Transmission Solenoid Pack]
    PCM_ACT6[Fuel Pump]
    PCM_NET1[CAN Network High Speed 500 kbps]
    PCM_NET2[Other ECUs ABS BCM TCM Standalone]
    PCM_OBD[OBD II Diagnostic Port J1962]
    PCM_MIL[Malfunction Indicator Lamp MIL]

    PCM_S1 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S2 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S5 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S6 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S7 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S8 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S9 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S10 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S11 --> PCM_ADC
    PCM_S3 --> PCM_DIGIN
    PCM_S4 --> PCM_DIGIN

    PCM_DIGIN --> PCM_CPU

    PCM_WDT --> PCM_CPU

    PCM_TRX --> PCM_OBD

    PCM_DRV1 --> PCM_ACT1
    PCM_DRV2 --> PCM_ACT2
    PCM_DRV3 --> PCM_ACT3
    PCM_DRV4 --> PCM_ACT4
    PCM_DRV5 --> PCM_ACT5
    PCM_DRV6 --> PCM_ACT6
```

### Explanation of Architecture Flow

1. **Sensor Layer (PCM_S1 to PCM_S11):** Captures physical quantities. Analog sensors feed the ADC; digital sensors (CKP, CMP) feed the eTPU / Input Capture unit.
2. **Front-End (PCM_ADC, PCM_DIGIN):** Performs anti-aliasing filtering, scaling, and level shifting.
3. **Compute Core (PCM_CPU):** Executes the calibrated control algorithms at the prescribed loop rates.
4. **Memory Hierarchy (PCM_ROM, PCM_RAM, PCM_EEPROM):** Stores calibration maps, runtime data, and adaptive learn values respectively.
5. **Driver Stage (PCM_DRV1 to PCM_DRV6):** Provides high-current drive signals with built-in fault diagnostics (open-load, short-to-ground).
6. **Actuator Layer (PCM_ACT1 to PCM_ACT6):** Converts electrical energy into mechanical action on the powertrain.
7. **Communication Subsystem (PCM_CAN, PCM_TRX):** Interfaces to the rest of the vehicle's CAN bus (ABS, BCM, Instrument Cluster, standalone TCM).
8. **Diagnostics (PCM_OBD, PCM_MIL):** Exposes fault codes (DTCs) to the OBD-II scanner per SAE J2012.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define the Powertrain Control Module (PCM). How does it differ from a standalone Engine Control Module (ECM)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The PCM is the integrated embedded electronic control unit that manages both engine and transmission functions in real time.
- **(1 mark)** It combines the Engine Control Module (ECM) and the Transmission Control Module (TCM) into a single hardware platform with shared sensors, power supply, and CAN bus interface.
- **(1 mark)** Unlike a standalone ECM, the PCM performs **torque arbitration** between engine and transmission, enabling features like *torque reduction during shift* (also called *torque hole filling*), fuel-cut during deceleration, and coordinated VVT phasing.

#### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six major sensors interfaced to the PCM and state the physical quantity each measures.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any six, 0.5 marks each):**

| Sensor | Physical Quantity |
|---|---|
| MAP (Manifold Absolute Pressure) | Intake manifold pressure (kPa) |
| MAF (Mass Air Flow) | Mass of intake air per second (kg/s) |
| CKP (Crankshaft Position) | Crank angle and RPM |
| CMP (Camshaft Position) | Camshaft phase for sequential injection |
| TPS (Throttle Position Sensor) | Throttle plate angle / driver demand |
| O₂ / Lambda Sensor | Exhaust oxygen concentration / AFR |
| CTS (Coolant Temperature Sensor) | Engine coolant temperature |
| Knock Sensor | Engine block vibration (knock detection) |
| VSS (Vehicle Speed Sensor) | Road speed (km/h) |
| FRP (Fuel Rail Pressure) | Common-rail fuel pressure |

---

### Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** With the aid of a detailed block diagram, explain the architecture of a modern Powertrain Control Module. Discuss the role of each major subsystem and indicate typical loop times. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **(2 marks)** Introduction — PCM as a real-time multi-rate embedded system; classification into *sensor layer*, *front-end*, *compute core*, *memory*, *driver stage*, *actuator layer*, and *communication*.
2. **(1 mark)** Sensor layer — MAP, MAF, CKP, CMP, TPS, O₂, CTS, knock, VSS, FRP — sampled at appropriate rates.
3. **(1 mark)** Compute core — 32-bit automotive MCU (e.g., NXP S32K3, Infineon AURIX TC3xx) with lock-step cores for ASIL-D.
4. **(1 mark)** Memory hierarchy — Flash (calibration), RAM (variables), EEPROM (adaptive learn / KAM).
5. **(1 mark)** Loop times — Crank-synchronous (6°–30°), 10 ms medium loop, 100 ms slow loop; CAN transmission **10 ms to 100 ms**.
6. **(1 mark)** Communication — CAN-FD / FlexRay for high-speed, LIN for sub-modules, OBD-II for diagnostics.

**(b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder gasoline engine has a total displacement of **2.0 L**, runs at **3000 rpm**, with a volumetric efficiency of **0.90**. The intake air density is **1.20 kg/m³**. The fuel injectors have a rated flow rate of **200 cm³/min** at a calibration pressure differential of **300 kPa**. The actual rail-to-manifold differential is **350 kPa**, battery voltage is **13.5 V** (compensation factor = 0.92), and injector dead time is **0.8 ms**. The fuel density is **740 kg/m³** and the target lambda is **1.00**. Compute the final injector pulse width. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1** — Compute mass flow rate of air.

$$\dot{m}_{air} = \rho_{air} \cdot V_{disp} \cdot \eta_v \cdot \dfrac{N_{rpm}}{120}$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 1.20 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.90 \times \dfrac{3000}{120} = 1.20 \times 0.002 \times 0.90 \times 25$$

$$\dot{m}_{air} = 0.054 \ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 2** — Compute mass flow rate of fuel at λ = 1.

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{air}}{\lambda \cdot AFR_{stoich}} = \dfrac{0.054}{1.00 \times 14.7} = 3.673 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3** — Convert rated injector flow to kg/s.

$$\dot{Q}_{inj} = 200 \ \dfrac{cm^3}{min} = \dfrac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{60} = 3.333 \times 10^{-6} \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{inj} = \rho_{fuel} \cdot \dot{Q}_{inj} = 740 \times 3.333 \times 10^{-6} = 2.467 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4** — Apply pressure differential correction.

$$\dot{m}_{inj, corrected} = \dot{m}_{inj} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{350}{300}} = 2.467 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{1.1667} = 2.467 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0801 = 2.665 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 5** — Compute base pulse width.

$$t_{pw, base} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{inj, corrected}} = \dfrac{3.673 \times 10^{-3}}{2.665 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.378 \ \text{ms}$$

**Step 6** — Apply battery voltage compensation.

$$t_{pw, comp} = t_{pw, base} \times 0.92 = 1.378 \times 0.92 = 1.268 \ \text{ms}$$

**Step 7** — Add dead time.

$$t_{pw, final} = 1.268 + 0.8 = 2.068 \ \text{ms}$$

**Final Answer:** $t_{pw, final} \approx \mathbf{2.07 \ ms}$ per injection event.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget the **pressure differential correction** $\sqrt{\Delta p_{rail} / \Delta p_{cal}}$ and lose **2 marks**. Also, do not confuse the **displacement** (2.0 L = 2.0 × 10⁻³ m³) unit conversion — this is a classic KTU trap.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the CAN bus communication protocol as used in PCM networks. Discuss message frame structure, arbitration, and error handling mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **(1 mark)** CAN introduction — multi-master, broadcast, differential signaling on CAN-H and CAN-L; bit rates up to **1 Mbps (Classical CAN)** and **5 Mbps (CAN-FD)**.
2. **(1 mark)** Frame types — Data, Remote, Error, Overload; standard (11-bit) and extended (29-bit) identifiers.
3. **(1.5 marks)** Frame structure — SOF, Arbitration field (ID + RTR), Control field (DLC), Data (0–8 bytes for Classical CAN), CRC, ACK, EOF; bit stuffing rule.
4. **(1.5 marks)** Arbitration — CSMA/CD + AMP (Arbitration on Message Priority). Dominant bit (0) wins over recessive (1). Non-destructive bitwise arbitration.
5. **(1 mark)** Error handling — Bit stuffing, CRC-15, ACK slot, Bit Monitoring; five error counters (TEC, REC); error states — Error Active, Error Passive, Bus Off.
6. **(1 mark)** PCM-specific usage — Broadcast of Engine RPM (e.g., CAN ID 0x100), Coolant Temp, Vehicle Speed; reception of brake switch, gear position, ABS wheel speed for torque management.

**(b)** Discuss the PID control algorithm used in the PCM for **idle speed control**. Show the discrete-time implementation with anti-windup and explain the Ziegler–Nichols tuning procedure. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **(1 mark)** Role of idle speed control — Maintain target RPM (typically **700 ± 25 rpm**) under varying accessory loads (alternator, A/C compressor, power steering).
2. **(1.5 marks)** PID continuous form: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$.
3. **(1.5 marks)** Discrete incremental form derivation (as in Section 3 Derivation 2), with $T_s = 10$ ms.
4. **(1 mark)** Anti-windup — Clamping of $u(k)$ within $[u_{min}, u_{max}]$; back-calculation method $u_I(k) = u_I(k-1) + K_i T_s e(k) + K_a [u_{sat} - u(k)]$.
5. **(1 mark)** Ziegler–Nichols ultimate-gain tuning — Increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation; record $K_u$ and $T_u$; compute $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $K_i = 1.2 K_u / T_u$, $K_d = 0.075 K_u T_u$.
6. **(1 mark)** Practical concerns — Derivative filter (e.g., $N = 10$ to 20), bumpless transfer from open-loop to closed-loop, and disturbance feed-forward (A/C clutch signal).

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake in KTU valuations is presenting the **positional** PID form without the anti-windup clause. For automotive idle control, the **incremental (velocity) form** is mandatory because integrator wind-up during cranking would otherwise cause a *post-start surge*. Loss: **2 marks**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PCM** integrates ECM + TCM in a single hard real-time embedded platform; targets **ASIL-D** under ISO 26262.
- **Stoichiometric AFR** for gasoline is **14.7:1**; the PCM regulates **λ → 1** in closed loop after warm-up.
- **Injector pulse width** depends on air mass, λ-target, AFRₛ, injector flow, rail/manifold Δp, battery voltage, fuel temperature, and dead time.
- **Loop rates:** Crank-synchronous (fast), **10 ms** (medium), **100 ms** (slow).
- **OBD-II** readiness monitors must all complete before the **MIL** can turn off; **DTCs** stored as per **SAE J2012**.
- **CAN** uses CSMA/CD + AMP arbitration; dominant bit (0) wins; sample point at **75 % to 87.5 %**.
- **PID tuning** for idle uses **Ziegler-Nichols** ultimate gain method; anti-windup is mandatory.
- **Adaptive learn** (LTFT) is stored in **EEPROM / KAM** and survives power cycles.
- **Limp-home mode** substitutes default sensor values on Class-A fault and illuminates MIL.
- **Knock control** triggers per-cylinder **spark retard** of typically **3° to 12°**.
- **Torque-resolved architecture** is the modern OEM approach (Bosch MED17, Continental SIM271).
- **Hardware watchdog** must be refreshed within **<50 ms** to prevent runaway ECU.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Engine Control Module (ECM) — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engine Control Module (ECM):** The Engine Control Module is a specialized, ruggedized **embedded electronic control unit (ECU)** that acts as the central computational brain of a modern internal combustion engine. It continuously acquires real-time data from a distributed network of sensors, executes deterministic control algorithms (e.g., fuel injection, ignition timing, idle speed, knock detection, emissions), and drives actuators (injectors, coils, throttle, EGR valves) via power-driver stages to maintain optimal engine performance, fuel economy, and exhaust emissions.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme context**, the ECM is studied as a canonical example of a **Hard Real-Time Embedded System** with deterministic scheduling deadlines in the millisecond range, distributed sensor/actuator I/O, and **ISO 26262 functional safety** considerations.

### Sub-Components of the Term
| Sub-Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Engine** | The internal combustion engine (Petrol / Diesel / Hybrid) |
| **Control** | Closed-loop PID, lookup-table (MAP), and model-based control |
| **Module** | A self-contained, sealed, automotive-qualified electronic unit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Synonyms used in industry / literature:** Engine Control Unit (ECU), Engine Management System (EMS), Powertrain Control Module (PCM). In KTU textbooks, **ECM and ECU are often used interchangeably**, but strictly speaking, PCM controls both engine + transmission, while ECM controls only the engine.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Think of the **ECM as the "Brain Surgeon" inside your car's engine bay**. Imagine a human body:

- **Eyes & Nerves = Sensors** (Crankshaft position, MAP, MAF, oxygen, coolant temperature)
- **Brain = ECM Microcontroller** (decision-making logic)
- **Muscles & Hands = Actuators** (Fuel injectors, spark coils, throttle body)
- **Spinal Cord = Wiring Harness + CAN bus** (communication network)

The body (engine) keeps changing — different load, different temperature, different altitude. Just like a brain surgeon monitors the patient **thousands of times per second** and adjusts medicine doses, the ECM samples sensors **hundreds of times per second** and decides *exactly* how much fuel to inject, *exactly* when to fire the spark plug, to keep the engine running smoothly, efficiently, and cleanly.

If the brain stops working → the patient collapses. If the ECM fails → the engine **does not start, or enters Limp-Home Mode** (limited power so the driver can safely reach a service center).

### 1.3 Key Physical / Performance Constants

- **Operating Voltage:** **12 V DC** (nominal automotive battery), with operating range **6 V – 18 V** (with load-dump transients up to **+40 V**).
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **−40 °C to +125 °C** (under-hood automotive grade, AEC-Q100 qualified).
- **Processor Clock Speed:** Typically **40 MHz – 300 MHz** (modern multi-core 32-bit, e.g., NXP MPC5xxx, Infineon Aurix TC2xx/TC3xx).
- **Control Loop Time (Crank Angle Based):** Fuel injection event triggered at a specific crank angle (e.g., **720° / N**, where N = number of cylinders).
- **Crankshaft Sampling Rate:** Up to **60 teeth × 2 edges = 120 edges per revolution** ⇒ at **6000 RPM**, this is **60 kHz** of crankshaft position events.
- **Fuel Injection Decision Time:** **< 2 ms** typical.
- **Communication Protocols:** **CAN (ISO 11898)** at **500 kbps** standard, **CAN-FD** up to **2 Mbps**, **LIN** at **19.2 kbps**, **FlexRay** up to **10 Mbps**, and **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1 / 1000BASE-T1)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** **"S-A-P"** for ECM core functions: **S**ense → **A**lgo (decide) → **P**ower (actuate).

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Functional Pillars of an ECM

The ECM's operation can be decomposed into three sequential pillars. Every concept or numerical problem on this topic in KTU exams traces back to one of these.

### Pillar 1 — SENSING (Input Stage)
The ECM must know the *current state* of the engine. It does this through a **Sensor Signal Conditioning Front-End** containing:
- **Analog Inputs:** MAP, MAF, IAT, ECT, TPS, O₂ sensor, fuel pressure, oil pressure.
- **Digital / Frequency Inputs:** Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP), Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP), Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) — usually **Hall-effect** or **variable reluctance** giving a **square wave / pulse train**.
- **Communication Inputs:** CAN messages from ABS, BCM, TCM, airbag module.

### Pillar 2 — DECISION (Processing Stage)
The microcontroller (often a **32-bit RISC** core, sometimes dual-core lockstep for ASIL-D) executes:
1. **Crank Angle Synchronization Task** (highest priority, hard real-time, ≪ 1 ms deadline).
2. **Fuel Calculation Task** (closed-loop or open-loop).
3. **Ignition Timing Calculation Task.**
4. **Idle Air Control Task.**
5. **Knock Detection Task** (DSP-intensive, FFT of cylinder pressure / block vibration).
6. **OBD-II Diagnostics Task** (lambda probe closed-loop trim).
7. **CAN / LIN Communication Task.**
8. **Background diagnostics & self-test.**

These tasks are scheduled by a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)** like **AUTOSAR OS** or a **crank-angle-triggered scheduler** (most common in production ECMs).

### Pillar 3 — ACTUATION (Output Stage)
Low-voltage digital logic from the MCU (3.3 V / 5 V, few mA) cannot drive a fuel injector or spark coil directly. A **Power Driver Stage** is used:
- **Low-Side Drivers (LSD):** MOSFET switches, common for injectors, relays.
- **High-Side Drivers (HSD):** Smart high-side switches (e.g., BTS5xxx series) for loads.
- **H-Bridges / Half-Bridges:** Used for DC motors (throttle body, EGR, VNT turbo).
- **Ignition IGBT / Darlington:** Special **400 – 600 V, 20 A peak** switches for inductive-discharge ignition coils.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind the Core Loops

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic — The Two Most Important Closed-Loop Controls in the ECM:**
> 1. **Lambda (Air-Fuel Ratio) Closed-Loop Control**
> 2. **Idle Speed Closed-Loop Control**

### Loop A — Lambda (Air-Fuel Ratio) Closed-Loop Control
- **Why?** To keep the catalytic converter at its optimal operating temperature and stoich ratio (**λ = 1**, A/F = **14.7 : 1** for petrol). A 1% deviation from λ = 1 increases HC and CO emissions by **15 – 20%**.
- **How?** A **wideband O₂ sensor (UEGO)** produces a current proportional to λ. The ECM runs a **PI (Proportional-Integral) controller** that adjusts the **Short Term Fuel Trim (STFT)** to drive the measured λ to 1.00. The trim is clamped to ±**25%** for diagnostics.

### Loop B — Idle Speed Closed-Loop Control
- **Why?** When the driver releases the accelerator, the throttle closes. The ECM must keep the engine at the target idle RPM (e.g., **750 ± 25 RPM**) by bleeding air past the throttle via the **Idle Air Control Valve (IACV)** or a **Drive-by-Wire throttle plate angle**.
- **How?** A **PID controller** regulates IACV duty cycle based on the error **e(t) = (RPM_target − RPM_actual)**. RPM is derived by counting crank teeth per unit time.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam favorites for the 14-mark analytical questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (Petrol) | $A/F_{stoich} = 14.7$ | mass of air per kg of fuel | dimensionless |
| 2 | Equivalence Ratio | $\lambda = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | $\lambda = 1$ stoich, $\lambda<1$ rich, $\lambda>1$ lean | dimensionless |
| 3 | Engine Speed from Crank Teeth | $N = \frac{60 \cdot f_{crank}}{T_{crank}}$ | $f_{crank}$ = pulse freq, $T_{crank}$ = total teeth per rev | RPM |
| 4 | Cylinder Air Mass (Speed-Density) | $m_{air} = \frac{\eta_{vol} \cdot P_{IMEP} \cdot V_d}{R \cdot T_{IMEP}}$ | Speed-Density (MAP-based) | kg / stroke |
| 5 | Base Fuel Pulse Width | $t_{inj} = \frac{m_{fuel}}{ \dot{m}_{inj} } = \frac{m_{air}}{\lambda \cdot (A/F)_{stoich} \cdot \dot{m}_{inj}}$ | Injector pulse width | ms |
| 6 | PID Controller Output | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Standard parallel form | V or % duty |
| 7 | Discrete PI (used in ECU) | $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p(e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k]$ | Euler-integrated PI | — |
| 8 | Ignition Dwell Time | $t_{dwell} = \frac{L \cdot I_{peak}}{V_{bat} - V_{sat}}$ | Time to charge coil to peak current | ms |
| 9 | Spark Advance Angle | $\theta_{spark} = \theta_{MBT} - \theta_{knock\_retard}$ | Knock-limited spark advance | degrees BTDC |
| 10 | Crankshaft Position Resolution | $\Delta\theta = \frac{360^{\circ}}{T_{crank}}$ | Per tooth angular resolution | degrees / tooth |
| 11 | Knock Sensor Resonance | $f_{knock} \approx 7\,\text{kHz}$ (typical) | Cylinder-block vibration frequency | Hz |
| 12 | CAN Bus Bit Time | $t_{bit} = \frac{1}{f_{CAN}}$ | At 500 kbps, $t_{bit} = 2\,\mu s$ | seconds |
| 13 | Lambda Sensor Voltage | $V_{\lambda} \in 0.1$ V (lean) to $0.9$ V (rich) | Narrowband O₂ sensor output | Volts |
| 14 | Catalyst Optimal Temp. | $T_{cat} \approx 400\,^{\circ}\text{C} - 800\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Light-off to full conversion window | °C |
| 15 | Fuel Injector Dead Time | $t_{dead} \approx 0.8$ – $1.5$ ms | Mechanical + solenoid lag | ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formulas 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9 are the most frequently tested in KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.** Master the **units, the meaning of every variable, and the "why"** behind each.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ECM is the *defining invention* that allowed the modern automobile to simultaneously achieve:
- **> 40%** thermal efficiency (with turbo + GDI + Atkinson cycle + VVT, all ECM-controlled).
- **< 1 g/km** NOx emissions (with ECM-controlled EGR + SCR + DPF).
- **Self-diagnosis** via OBD-II (legally mandated since **1996 in the US** and **2001 (Euro 3) in Europe**).

A modern ECM contains **~ 50 million lines of code** (more than a Boeing 787!) — testimony to how a single embedded system has absorbed virtually all mechanical/hydraulic engine control into software.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **canonical KTU 14-mark problem**: given engine sensor inputs, calculate the fuel injection pulse width. We derive this step-by-step with every algebraic transition written explicitly.

## 3.1 The Canonical Problem Statement (KTU-Style)

> A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a displacement $V_d = 1.998$ L, volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol} = 0.88$, intake manifold pressure $P_{IMEP} = 65$ kPa, intake manifold temperature $T_{IMEP} = 333$ K ($60°$C), R = **287 J/(kg·K)** for air. The engine runs at **N = 3000 RPM** in closed-loop at **λ = 1.00**. The fuel injector delivers **$\dot{m}_{inj}$ = 4.8 g/s** at nominal pressure. Calculate the **injector pulse width** $t_{inj}$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation

### Step 1 — Determine Mass of Air Trapped per Cylinder per Stroke

We use the **Speed-Density equation** (derived from the ideal gas law applied to the intake manifold volume):

$$P_{IMEP} \cdot V_{IMEP} = m_{air} \cdot R \cdot T_{IMEP}$$

For the volume of air entering one cylinder per intake stroke, we use the displaced volume $V_d$ scaled by volumetric efficiency $\eta_{vol}$ (since η_vol accounts for pressure losses, residual gases, and incomplete filling):

$$V_{IMEP} = \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d$$

Therefore:

$$m_{air} = \frac{P_{IMEP} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d}{R \cdot T_{IMEP}}$$

Substituting the numbers (always convert L → m³: 1.998 L = $1.998 \times 10^{-3}$ m³; kPa → Pa: 65 kPa = $65{,}000$ Pa):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air} &= \frac{65{,}000 \cdot 0.88 \cdot 1.998 \times 10^{-3}}{287 \cdot 333} \\
&= \frac{114.2866}{95{,}571} \\
&\approx 1.196 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/cycle} \\
&\approx 1.196 \text{ g per cylinder per intake stroke}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Mass of Air per Second (Engine-Wide)

A **4-stroke engine** completes one intake stroke per cylinder every **2 crank revolutions**. Therefore each cylinder ingests $m_{air}$ g per **2 crank revs**, i.e., per **2 × (60/N) seconds**.

The total air mass flow rate into the engine is:

$$\dot{m}_{air,total} = \frac{N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot m_{air}}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{air,total} &= \frac{3000 \cdot 4 \cdot 1.196 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= \frac{14.352}{2} \\
&= 7.176 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Determine Mass of Fuel Required for Stoichiometric Combustion

At λ = 1.00, we use the **stoichiometric air-fuel ratio of 14.7 : 1** (mass basis). Therefore:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air,total}}{14.7}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{fuel} &= \frac{7.176}{14.7} \\
&\approx 0.4882 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Determine Total Injector Pulse Width per Second

Each injector delivers fuel at a maximum mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{inj}$ (when fully open). The injector is **PWM-modulated** with a duty cycle $D = t_{inj} / T_{period}$. For continuous injection at this rate, the **total ON time per second** required is:

$$t_{ON,total} = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{inj}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{ON,total} &= \frac{0.4882}{4.8} \\
&\approx 0.10171 \text{ s} \\
&= 101.71 \text{ ms per second}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Distribute Pulse Width Among Cylinders per Injection Event

For a **4-cylinder engine**, there are **2 injection events per crank revolution** (since each cylinder fires once per 2 revs, but in modern systems we often use **sequential injection** — one injection per cylinder per cycle = 4 injections per 2 revs). The engine does **N/60 revolutions per second**, and each cylinder fires every **2 crank revs** ⇒ each cylinder fires **N/120 times per second**.

$$n_{inj,per\,cyl} = \frac{N}{120} = \frac{3000}{120} = 25 \text{ injections/s per cylinder}$$

Since there are 4 cylinders firing sequentially, the **total injections per second** = $4 \times 25 = 100$ injections/s.

Therefore, the **per-injection-event pulse width** is:

$$t_{inj,per\,event} = \frac{t_{ON,total}}{n_{inj,total}} = \frac{101.71}{100}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{inj,per\,event} &\approx 1.0171 \text{ ms} \\
&\approx 1.02 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Apply the Injector Dead-Time Correction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In KTU problems, the injector dead time $t_{dead} \approx 1.0$ ms is a frequent "trap"!** The actual commanded pulse width is the *opening* time (not the *closing* time), so we ADD the dead time:

$$t_{cmd} = t_{inj,per\,event} + t_{dead}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{cmd} &= 1.0171 + 1.0 \\
&\approx 2.02 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Apply Battery Voltage Compensation (Optional KTU Twist)

If the problem mentions low battery voltage $V_{bat}$, the dead time increases because the solenoid pulls slower. Many KTU problems use the empirical correction:

$$t_{dead,\,corrected} = t_{dead} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{bat,\,nominal}}{V_{bat}}\right)$$

For $V_{bat,\,nominal} = 14$ V and $V_{bat} = 12$ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{dead,\,corrected} &= 1.0 \cdot \frac{14}{12} \\
&\approx 1.167 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the final commanded pulse width becomes:

$$t_{cmd,\,final} = 1.0171 + 1.167 \approx 2.18 \text{ ms}$$

## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Step | Quantity Computed | Formula Used | Numerical Result |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m_{air}$ per cylinder per stroke | $\frac{P \eta_{vol} V_d}{R T}$ | **1.196 g** |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{air,total}$ | $\frac{N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot m_{air}}{2}$ | **7.176 g/s** |
| 3 | $\dot{m}_{fuel}$ | $\frac{\dot{m}_{air,total}}{14.7}$ | **0.4882 g/s** |
| 4 | Total ON time per second | $\frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{inj}}$ | **101.71 ms/s** |
| 5 | Per-event pulse width | $\frac{t_{ON,total}}{n_{inj}}$ | **1.02 ms** |
| 6 | + Dead time | $t_{inj} + t_{dead}$ | **2.02 ms** |
| 7 | + Voltage compensation | $t_{dead} \cdot (V_{nom}/V_{bat})$ | **2.18 ms** |

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Define the term "Engine Control Module (ECM)" and list any **four** major input sensors that an ECM processes in a modern petrol engine.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Engine Control Module (ECM) is the central embedded electronic control unit of an internal combustion engine. It receives inputs from various engine sensors, executes real-time control algorithms, and commands actuators to regulate fuel injection, ignition timing, and emissions.

**Four major input sensors (0.375 mark each):**
1. **Crankshaft Position Sensor (CKP)** — gives engine RPM and piston position.
2. **Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor (MAP)** — measures intake manifold pressure for fuel calculation (speed-density method).
3. **Mass Air Flow Sensor (MAF)** — directly measures air mass entering the engine.
4. **Wideband Oxygen Sensor (UEGO / Lambda Sensor)** — provides closed-loop air-fuel ratio feedback.
5. *(Optional, for extra credit)* Throttle Position Sensor (TPS), Engine Coolant Temperature (ECT), Knock Sensor, Camshaft Position Sensor (CMP).

---

### Question 2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Explain the difference between "open-loop" and "closed-loop" fuel control strategies in an ECM. Under what engine conditions is open-loop control used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Open-Loop (1.5 marks) | Closed-Loop (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback used?** | No | Yes (from O₂/lambda sensor) |
| **Strategy** | Pre-calibrated lookup tables (MAP/RPM → fuel pulse) | PI controller adjusts fuel trim using O₂ feedback |
| **Accuracy** | Approximate; ignores aging, altitude, sensor drift | High; self-correcting |
| **Emissions** | Higher HC, CO during transition | Optimal, λ held at 1.00 |

**Open-loop conditions (mentioned briefly):** Cold start, wide-open throttle (WOT), high RPM / high load, and during warm-up phase before the O₂ sensor reaches operating temperature (**> 350 °C**).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — Fuel Injection Pulse Width Calculation

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]**

A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder, 2.0 L petrol engine runs at **3200 RPM** under a partial load. Given the following data:
- Volumetric efficiency, $\eta_{vol} = 0.85$
- Intake manifold pressure, $P = 60$ kPa
- Intake manifold temperature, $T = 340$ K
- Gas constant for air, $R = 287$ J/(kg·K)
- Equivalence ratio, $\lambda = 1.05$ (slightly lean)
- Injector flow rate, $\dot{m}_{inj} = 5.0$ g/s at rated pressure
- Injector dead time, $t_{dead} = 1.0$ ms
- Battery voltage = **12 V** (nominal = 14 V)

**Compute:**
- **(a) [7 marks]** The mass of air per cylinder per intake stroke, the total fuel mass flow rate, and the **base** injector pulse width per injection event.
- **(b) [7 marks]** The injector dead-time correction, the **battery-voltage-compensated** pulse width, and a brief justification of why dead-time compensation is essential in modern GDI engines.

#### Model Solution for Part A

**Part (a) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [2 marks]** — Mass of air per cylinder per intake stroke (Speed-Density):

$$m_{air} = \frac{P \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d}{R \cdot T}$$

Converting $V_d = 2.0$ L $= 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³ and $P = 60{,}000$ Pa:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air} &= \frac{60{,}000 \cdot 0.85 \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{-3}}{287 \cdot 340} \\
&= \frac{102.0}{97{,}580} \\
&\approx 1.0453 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg} \\
&= 1.0453 \text{ g per cylinder per intake stroke}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 [2 marks]** — Total air mass flow rate (4-stroke ⇒ 2 crank revs per cycle per cylinder):

$$\dot{m}_{air,total} = \frac{N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot m_{air}}{2}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{air,total} &= \frac{3200 \cdot 4 \cdot 1.0453 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \\
&= \frac{13.380}{2} \\
&\approx 6.690 \text{ g/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 [1.5 marks]** — Fuel mass flow rate at $\lambda = 1.05$:

Recall that **λ = 1.05 means the actual A/F is 1.05 × 14.7 = 15.435**, i.e., lean. Therefore:

$$\dot{m}_{fuel} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air,total}}{A/F_{actual}} = \frac{6.690}{15.435} \approx 0.4335 \text{ g/s}$$

(Equivalently, $\dot{m}_{fuel} = \dot{m}_{air,total} / (14.7 \cdot \lambda)$ — both are correct.)

**Step 4 [1.5 marks]** — Per-event injector pulse width:

Total injections per second = $n_{cyl} \times N / 120 = 4 \times 3200 / 120 \approx 106.67$ injections/s.

$$t_{inj,base} = \frac{\dot{m}_{fuel}}{\dot{m}_{inj} \cdot n_{inj,total}} = \frac{0.4335}{5.0 \times 106.67 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{inj,base} &= \frac{0.4335}{0.5333} \\
&\approx 0.8130 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

So $t_{inj,base} \approx$ **0.813 ms**.

---

**Part (b) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Step 1 [2 marks]** — Apply dead-time correction. The actual commanded pulse width is:

$$t_{cmd} = t_{inj,base} + t_{dead} = 0.813 + 1.0 = 1.813 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 [2 marks]** — Apply battery-voltage compensation:

$$t_{dead,\,corrected} = t_{dead} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{nominal}}{V_{bat}}\right) = 1.0 \cdot \frac{14}{12} \approx 1.167 \text{ ms}$$

$$t_{cmd,\,final} = 0.813 + 1.167 = 1.980 \text{ ms} \approx 1.98 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 [3 marks]** — Justification (write any 3 of the following points):

1. The injector solenoid has **mechanical + electromagnetic lag** between the electrical command and actual fuel flow start. At low pulse widths (< 2 ms), this lag dominates — a **30% error** without compensation.
2. In **Gasoline Direct Injection (GDI)** engines, the injector operates against high rail pressure (**200 – 350 bar**), making dead time highly pressure-dependent.
3. **Battery voltage** variation (during cranking, when $V_{bat}$ may drop to **8 V**) slows the solenoid pull-in, increasing dead time by up to **40%**.
4. Without compensation, **cold-start emissions** and **idle stability** suffer dramatically, and OBD-II monitors flag the system as faulty.
5. Modern ECMs store a **3D lookup table of $t_{dead}$ vs. (V_bat, fuel pressure)** and add this value in real time to every injection command.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — Closed-Loop Lambda Control Design

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]**

A modern petrol engine uses a wideband oxygen sensor (UEGO) for closed-loop air-fuel ratio (lambda) control. The lambda sensor has a time constant of **τ = 50 ms** and the catalytic converter requires the A/F ratio to remain within **± 1% of stoich (λ = 1.00)**.

**Answer the following:**
- **(a) [7 marks]** Draw a complete block diagram of the closed-loop lambda control system showing the summing junction, the PI controller, the fuel injector (plant), the engine, the exhaust, and the O₂ sensor. Explain the role of the **PI controller** in this loop.
- **(b) [7 marks]** Using the discrete-time PI equation $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p (e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k]$, compute the controller output progression for **5 samples** assuming:
  - $K_p = 0.8$, $K_i = 12$, $T_s = 0.05$ s
  - Initial output $u[0] = 50$ (%)
  - Error sequence: $e[0] = 0.05$, $e[1] = 0.04$, $e[2] = 0.025$, $e[3] = 0.01$, $e[4] = 0.0$ (errors are deviations of λ from 1.00)

  **Tabulate** $u[k]$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ and comment on **steady-state performance**.

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) Solution [7 marks]:**

**Block Diagram (4 marks)** — Draw a closed-loop with:
- Reference $R = 1.00$ (λ target) → Σ-junction (positive) → minus feedback λ_meas.
- Σ-output is error $e = (1.00 - \lambda_{meas})$.
- $e$ enters the **PI controller** (proportional + integrator path).
- PI output $u$ (%) drives the **Fuel Injector Driver** → controls fuel pulse width.
- Injector alters the actual A/F in the **Combustion Chamber**.
- Combustion produces **Exhaust gases** measured by the **UEGO Oxygen Sensor**.
- Sensor voltage/current is converted to $\lambda_{meas}$ and fed back to the Σ-junction (negative feedback).

**Role of PI Controller (3 marks):**
- **Proportional Term (K_p):** Provides an **immediate correction** proportional to the current error. A higher $K_p$ gives faster response but may cause oscillation.
- **Integral Term (K_i):** Eliminates **steady-state error**. Without the integrator, λ would settle at a value slightly off 1.00 due to injector dead time and altitude compensation errors.
- Together, the **PI controller** ensures λ converges to **1.00 ± 0.5%** within **2 – 3 seconds** of closed-loop entry.

---

**Part (b) Solution [7 marks]:**

Given: $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p (e[k] - e[k-1]) + K_i T_s e[k]$
With $K_p = 0.8$, $K_i = 12$, $T_s = 0.05$ s, so $K_i T_s = 12 \times 0.05 = 0.6$.

**Sample k = 0** (initial): $u[0] = 50.000$ (given).

**Sample k = 1:**
- $e[1] - e[0] = 0.04 - 0.05 = -0.01$
- $K_i T_s e[1] = 0.6 \times 0.04 = 0.024$
- $K_p (e[1] - e[0]) = 0.8 \times (-0.01) = -0.008$
- $\Delta u = -0.008 + 0.024 = 0.016$
- $u[1] = 50.000 + 0.016 = 50.016$

**Sample k = 2:**
- $e[2] - e[1] = 0.025 - 0.04 = -0.015$
- $K_i T_s e[2] = 0.6 \times 0.025 = 0.015$
- $K_p (e[2] - e[1]) = 0.8 \times (-0.015) = -0.012$
- $\Delta u = -0.012 + 0.015 = 0.003$
- $u[2] = 50.016 + 0.003 = 50.019$

**Sample k = 3:**
- $e[3] - e[2] = 0.01 - 0.025 = -0.015$
- $K_i T_s e[3] = 0.6 \times 0.01 = 0.006$
- $K_p (e[3] - e[2]) = 0.8 \times (-0.015) = -0.012$
- $\Delta u = -0.012 + 0.006 = -0.006$
- $u[3] = 50.019 - 0.006 = 50.013$

**Sample k = 4:**
- $e[4] - e[3] = 0.0 - 0.01 = -0.01$
- $K_i T_s e[4] = 0.6 \times 0.0 = 0.0$
- $K_p (e[4] - e[3]) = 0.8 \times (-0.01) = -0.008$
- $\Delta u = -0.008 + 0.0 = -0.008$
- $u[4] = 50.013 - 0.008 = 50.005$

**Tabulated Output (3 marks):**

| k | e[k] | K_p (e[k]-e[k-1]) | K_i T_s e[k] | u[k] (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.050 | — | — | 50.000 |
| 1 | 0.040 | -0.008 | 0.024 | 50.016 |
| 2 | 0.025 | -0.012 | 0.015 | 50.019 |
| 3 | 0.010 | -0.012 | 0.006 | 50.013 |
| 4 | 0.000 | -0.008 | 0.000 | 50.005 |

**Comment on Steady-State Performance (1 mark):**
- The controller **drives the error to zero within 4 samples (= 200 ms)**.
- The output $u[k]$ converges to **~ 50 %** fuel duty cycle — a stable steady state.
- No oscillation, no overshoot — confirms a **well-tuned PI** (good damping).
- The integral term ensured **zero steady-state error**, which is exactly the goal of closed-loop lambda control.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in ECM Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "per cylinder" factor in Speed-Density** — many students forget the $n_{cyl}/2$ term for 4-stroke engines. **Deduct 2 marks** if missed.
> 2. **Mixing up λ with A/F** — Remember: $\lambda = (A/F)_{actual} / 14.7$. If the question says λ = 1.05, the **A/F is 15.435**, not 1.05.
> 3. **Ignoring the injector dead time** — A 3-mark deduction. Always ADD it to the calculated pulse width.
> 4. **Mixing up CAN-FD baud rate (2 Mbps) with classical CAN (500 kbps)** — these are different protocols; not interchangeable.
> 5. **Crankshaft tooth count** — At 60-tooth wheel, resolution is **6° per tooth**, not 1°. Many students miss this.
> 6. **PI vs PID confusion** — Lambda loop uses PI (derivative amplifies noise from O₂ sensor ripple); idle speed uses PID (smooth, slow signal).
> 7. **Units trap** — Always convert **kPa → Pa** and **L → m³** in ideal gas law calculations. Forgetting this causes 10× errors.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — ECM (Module 4)**

### A. Definitions & Architecture
- ECM = **Embedded ECU** controlling engine functions; **Hard Real-Time** system.
- Three pillars: **Sense → Algorithm → Power Actuation**.
- Operating voltage: **12 V (6 – 18 V range)**, temperature: **−40 °C to +125 °C**.
- Modern ECMs use **32-bit RISC MCUs**, often **multi-core lockstep (Aurix TC3xx)** for ASIL-D.

### B. Sensors
- **CKP & CMP** → crank-angle synchronization (highest priority interrupt).
- **MAP / MAF** → air mass calculation.
- **O₂ (UEGO)** → closed-loop λ control.
- **Knock** → DSP-based FFT, **7 kHz** typical band.
- **TPS, ECT, IAT** → operating state.

### C. Actuators
- **Low-Side Driver (LSD)** for injectors, relays.
- **H-Bridge** for throttle, EGR, VNT turbo vanes.
- **Ignition IGBT / Darlington** for spark coils (400 – 600 V).

### D. Key Numbers (Memorize)
- Stoich A/F (petrol) = **14.7**.
- λ = 1 → stoich; λ < 1 → rich; λ > 1 → lean.
- Closed-loop λ = **1.00 ± 1%**.
- Crank tooth resolution = **360° / T** (e.g., 6° for 60-tooth wheel).
- CAN: 500 kbps; CAN-FD: 2 Mbps; FlexRay: 10 Mbps; LIN: 19.2 kbps.
- Fuel injector dead time: **0.8 – 1.5 ms**.

### E. Key Formulas
- Speed-Density: $m_{air} = \frac{P \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot V_d}{R \cdot T}$
- Air mass flow (4-stroke): $\dot{m}_{air} = \frac{N \cdot n_{cyl} \cdot m_{air}}{2}$
- Fuel pulse width: $t_{inj} = \frac{m_{air}}{14.7 \cdot \lambda \cdot \dot{m}_{inj}}$
- Battery compensation: $t_{dead,\,comp} = t_{dead} \cdot \frac{V_{nom}}{V_{bat}}$
- Discrete PI: $u[k] = u[k-1] + K_p \Delta e + K_i T_s e[k]$

### F. Closed-Loop Concepts
- **Lambda loop** uses **PI** controller.
- **Idle speed loop** uses **PID** controller.
- **Knock control** uses **feedback retard** of spark advance.
- Catalyst light-off at **~ 400 °C**, full conversion at **600 – 800 °C**.

### G. Standards & Communication
- **OBD-II** (US, 1996+), **EOBD** (EU, 2001+) — diagnostic protocols.
- **CAN** (ISO 11898), **CAN-FD**, **LIN**, **FlexRay**, **Automotive Ethernet (100/1000BASE-T1)**.
- **AUTOSAR** (Classic + Adaptive) — software architecture standard.
- **ISO 26262** — functional safety (ASIL-A → ASIL-D).
- **AEC-Q100** — IC qualification standard for automotive.

### H. Hot Exam Topics (Predictive)
- Speed-Density fuel calculation derivation.
- Lambda closed-loop PI design and discrete-time computation.
- Injector dead-time compensation with battery voltage.
- CAN bus frame structure & bit timing.
- Difference between open-loop and closed-loop fueling.
- Crank-angle-triggered scheduling vs time-triggered scheduling.
- Draw and explain block diagram of ECM with all sub-systems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transmission Control Module (TCM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT725 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS IN AUTOMOBILES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transmission Control Module (TCM) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Academic Definition The **Transmission Control Module (TCM)** is a dedicated, real-time embedded electronic control unit (ECU) that governs the operation of an automatic transmission (AT), continuously variable transmission (CVT), or dual-clutch transmission (DCT) in a vehicle. The TCM is a microcontroller-based subsystem that acquires data from multiple powertrain sensors, executes closed-loop control algorithms in real-time, and drives electro-hydraulic actuators to determine the optimal **gear ratio**, **shift timing**, **clutch engagement pressure**, and **torque converter lock-up state**. In the KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, the TCM is classified under **Powertrain Domain Controllers** and forms a critical node on the **CAN (Controller Area Network) bus**, communicating bi-directionally with the Engine Control Module (ECM), ABS/ESC module, Body Control Module (BCM), and the OBD-II diagnostic interface. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The TCM is examined as a representative case study of an *automotive embedded system* integrating analog signal conditioning, digital logic control, communication buses, and actuator drivers. Students must understand the input-processing-output triad and the safety-critical nature of shift logic. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are driving a manual-gear car through hilly terrain. Your brain continuously watches: *(i)* the speedometer (vehicle speed), *(ii)* the tachometer (engine RPM), *(iii)* the accelerator pedal position (load demand), and *(iv)* the road gradient (implicit). Based on this, your brain decides: *"Now, I should downshift from 4th to 3rd."* Your left foot operates the clutch, your right hand moves the lever, and your right foot modulates the throttle — all coordinated in split seconds. The **TCM is essentially that driver brain — but engineered into a sealed, ruggedized, fault-tolerant embedded computer**. It replaces the human's neuromuscular coordination with microcontrollers, sensors, and solenoid-driven hydraulic valves. It is **faster, more consistent, fuel-efficient, and never gets tired or distracted**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Performance Metrics (Industry Standard):** > - **Shift decision time:** **< 10 ms** > - **Hydraulic pressure modulation accuracy:** **± 20 kPa** > - **Operating temperature range:** **−40 °C to +125 °C** > - **Communication speed (CAN):** **500 kbps** (typical powertrain CAN) --- ### High-Level Functional Triad The TCM operation can be decomposed into the universal embedded-system triad: | Triad Stage | TCM-Specific Realization | |---|---| | **Input (Sensing Layer)** | Speed sensors, TPS, MAF, brake switch, ATF temp sensor, PRNDL position sensor | | **Processing (Computational Layer)** | 16/32-bit automotive MCU (e.g., Infineon Aurix, NXP S32K) running shift maps, torque management, diagnostics | | **Output (Actuation Layer)** | Shift solenoids (ON/OFF), Pressure Control Solenoids (PWM), Torque Converter Clutch (TCC) solenoid, MIL lamp driver | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 TCM Hardware Architecture — Logical Block Diagram The TCM, although sealed in a single aluminum housing, internally comprises the following logical subsystems: **A. Microcontroller Core (MCU)** Typically a 32-bit automotive-grade processor (e.g., Infineon TriCore TC27x, Renesas RH850, NXP MPC57xx) featuring: - A **Lockstep Core** for ASIL-D (ISO 26262) functional safety compliance. - A **DSP unit** or hardware multiplier for real-time FFT on vibration signals. - Dedicated **CAN / FlexRay / LIN peripheral blocks**. **B. Power Supply & Signal Conditioning Stage** - Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS) buck regulator dropping vehicle **+12 V** battery voltage to **+5 V** and **+3.3 V** rails. - Reverse-battery protection, load-dump suppression (ISO 7637-2 compliant), and watchdog timer reset. **C. Sensor Input Stage** Each analog/digital sensor input is routed through: - An **RC low-pass filter** (cut-off $\approx 1 \text{ kHz}$) for EMI suppression. - A **protection clamp** (TVS diode + Zener). - A **multiplexed 12-bit ADC** for analog channels. - **Digital input buffers** with Schmitt-trigger hysteresis for digital channels. **D. Actuator Driver Stage (The H-Bridge / High-Side Switch Stage)** Solenoids are inductive loads. They are driven using: - **Low-Side N-channel MOSFETs** with **flyback diodes**. - **PWM frequency typically 100 Hz to 2 kHz** for pressure control. - **Peak-and-Hold current profiling** to minimize thermal dissipation. **E. Communication Transceivers** - **CAN Transceiver** (e.g., TJA1042) interfacing MCU CAN peripheral to the physical bus. - **LIN Transceiver** for sub-modules (e.g., gear selector). - Optional **FlexRay transceiver** for high-speed deterministic X-by-Wire systems. **F. Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)** - Stores **calibration tables (shift maps)**, learned adaptive parameters, and Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs). ### 2.2 Sensor Inputs — Detailed Functional Role 1. **Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS):** A Hall-effect or magneto-resistive sensor on the transmission output shaft. Used to compute road speed and current gear ratio. 2. **Engine Speed Sensor (RPM):** Read via CAN from ECM, or directly via crank sensor input. 3. **Throttle Position Sensor (TPS):** A dual-track potentiometer (or non-contact Hall sensor). Indicates driver torque demand. 4. **Transmission Fluid Temperature (TFT) Sensor:** Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor immersed in ATF. 5. **Brake Pedal Switch (BPS):** A digital switch that **prevents gear engagement** unless brake is pressed (shift-interlock safety). 6. **PRNDL (Park/Reverse/Neutral/Drive/Low) Position Sensor:** Multi-position switch providing gear selector state to the TCM. 7. **Line Pressure Sensor:** Monitors actual hydraulic pressure feeding the clutch packs for closed-loop control. ### 2.3 Actuator Outputs — Detailed Functional Role 1. **Shift Solenoids (SS1, SS2):** Typically ON/OFF solenoid valves that direct hydraulic fluid to specific clutch packs or bands to engage/disengage gears. 2. **Pressure Control Solenoids (PCS, EPC):** *Variable Force Solenoids* controlled via **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** to modulate line pressure precisely. 3. **Torque Converter Clutch (TCC) Solenoid:** Engages the mechanical lock-up clutch in the torque converter to eliminate slip and improve fuel economy. 4. **Shift Lock Solenoid:** Prevents mechanical gear lever movement without brake pressed. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table compiles **every mathematically-grounded relationship** examinable for the TCM topic. Memorize the units and boundary conditions. | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $$ i = \frac{N_{in}}{N_{out}} $$ | $i$ = gear ratio, $N_{in}$ = input shaft speed (rpm), $N_{out}$ = output shaft speed (rpm) | Determines current transmission gear state. | | 2 | $$ s = \frac{N_{engine} - N_{turbine}}{N_{engine}} $$ | $s$ = slip ratio, $N_{engine}$ = engine rpm, $N_{turbine}$ = torque converter turbine rpm | Quantifies torque converter efficiency; target is $s \to 0$ for lock-up. | | 3 | $$ P_{line} = \frac{F_{clutch}}{A_{piston}} $$ | $P_{line}$ = hydraulic line pressure (Pa), $F_{clutch}$ = clutch holding force (N), $A_{piston}$ = piston area (m²) | Hydraulic force balance governing clutch engagement. | | 4 | $$ \eta_{TC} = \frac{T_{turbine} \cdot \omega_{turbine}}{T_{pump} \cdot \omega_{pump}} $$ | $\eta_{TC}$ = torque converter efficiency | Measures power transfer loss to optimize TCC engagement. | | 5 | $$ V_{ADC} = \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^n - 1) $$ | $V_{ADC}$ = digital count, $V_{ref}$ = ADC reference voltage (V), $n$ = ADC resolution (bits) | Used in sensor signal acquisition. | | 6 | $$ f_{PWM} = \frac{1}{T_{period}} = \frac{f_{clock}}{PSC \cdot ARR} $$ | $f_{PWM}$ = PWM output frequency (Hz), $PSC$ = prescaler, $ARR$ = auto-reload register | Controls solenoid average current. | | 7 | $$ D_{PWM} = \frac{t_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100\% $$ | $D_{PWM}$ = duty cycle (%), $t_{on}$ = ON time (s) | Modulates average solenoid force. | | 8 | $$ t_{shift} = f(i_{old}, i_{new}, P_{line}, \omega_{e}) $$ | $t_{shift}$ = shift time (s), function of multiple inputs | Empirical shift-time model from shift map. | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In modern automotive design, the TCM is the cornerstone of **driver comfort**, **fuel economy**, and **emissions compliance**. By interfacing with the ECM via CAN, it enables **coordinated torque management** during shifts — momentarily retarding engine torque (via spark timing or throttle reduction) to reduce driveline shock. This synergy is what allows modern 8- and 10-speed automatic transmissions (e.g., ZF 8HP, Toyota Aisin AWR) to achieve shift quality indistinguishable from expert manual drivers, while improving fuel economy by **10–15 %** compared to unsynchronized hydraulic transmissions of the 1990s. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Torque Converter Slip and Lock-Up Decision The torque converter is a fluid coupling that multiplies torque during launch. The **slip ratio** $s$ characterizes how much the turbine is lagging behind the engine (pump) impeller. **Step 1 — Define input variables.** Let the engine (pump impeller) rotate at $N_e$ rpm and the transmission input turbine at $N_t$ rpm. Both quantities are measurable via the engine RPM sensor (from ECM over CAN) and the transmission input speed sensor (TISS). **Step 2 — Express the relative speed difference.** The angular velocity deficit is: $$\omega_{slip} = \omega_{pump} - \omega_{turbine} = \frac{2\pi}{60}(N_e - N_t)$$ **Step 3 — Normalize by the pump speed.** To make slip dimensionless and comparable across engine speeds: $$s = \frac{\omega_{pump} - \omega_{turbine}}{\omega_{pump}} = \frac{N_e - N_t}{N_e}$$ **Step 4 — Lock-up decision logic.** The TCM compares $s$ against a calibrated threshold map. The decision rule is: $$\text{IF } s < s_{threshold}(T_{ATF}, \theta_{TPS}) \Rightarrow \text{Engage TCC}$$ where $s_{threshold}$ is typically in the range $\mathbf{5\%}$ to $\mathbf{15\%}$ depending on engine load and transmission fluid temperature. Below this threshold, the converter is hydraulically inefficient, so mechanical lock-up is commanded. **Step 5 — Compute efficiency.** The mechanical power transferred across the converter is: $$P_{turbine} = T_t \cdot \omega_t$$ The input power from the pump is: $$P_{pump} = T_p \cdot \omega_p$$ Therefore the converter efficiency is: $$\eta_{TC} = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{pump}} = \frac{T_t \cdot \omega_t}{T_p \cdot \omega_p} = \frac{T_t}{T_p} \cdot (1 - s)$$ > **Why this matters:** The product $T_t/T_p$ is the *torque multiplication ratio* (can exceed 2.0 at stall), while $(1-s)$ penalizes efficiency due to slip. At lock-up, $s \to 0$ and $\eta_{TC} \to 1.0$ — which is why modern TCMs aggressively lock up the converter as early as 2nd or 3rd gear. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: PWM Duty Cycle for Pressure Control The TCM controls line pressure by varying the average current through a *Variable Force Solenoid* (VFS). The VFS regulates a pilot hydraulic pressure, which is then amplified by a regulator valve. **Step 1 — Current-to-pressure transfer function.** The solenoid force on the armature is approximately: $$F_{sol} = k_1 \cdot I_{avg} - k_2 \cdot x$$ where $I_{avg}$ is the average coil current (A), $x$ is the armature displacement (m), and $k_1, k_2$ are solenoid-specific constants. **Step 2 — Current averaging via PWM.** For a PWM-driven solenoid with peak current $I_{peak}$, the average current is: $$I_{avg} = I_{peak} \cdot D_{PWM} \quad \text{(for unidirectional drive with flyback)}$$ **Step 3 — Hydraulic pressure relationship.** The pilot pressure $P_{pilot}$ generated by the VFS is: $$P_{pilot} = \frac{F_{sol}}{A_{armature}} = \frac{k_1 \cdot I_{avg}}{A_{armature}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitution.** Substituting the current relation: $$P_{pilot} = \frac{k_1 \cdot I_{peak}}{A_{armature}} \cdot D_{PWM}$$ This yields the **fundamental proportional control law**: $$\boxed{P_{pilot} \propto D_{PWM}}$$ **Step 5 — Worked numerical example.** Assume $k_1 = 10 \text{ N/A}$, $I_{peak} = 1.0 \text{ A}$, $A_{armature} = 50 \text{ mm}^2 = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$. For a target pilot pressure of $P_{pilot} = 400 \text{ kPa} = 4 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$: $$D_{PWM} = \frac{P_{pilot} \cdot A_{armature}}{k_1 \cdot I_{peak}} = \frac{(4 \times 10^5)(50 \times 10^{-6})}{(10)(1.0)}$$ $$D_{PWM} = \frac{20}{10} = 2.0$$ Since $D_{PWM}$ cannot exceed $100\%$, the actuator cannot deliver the requested pressure — indicating an actuator sizing problem. **Step 6 — Rescaling the example.** Suppose we require a more realistic $P_{pilot} = 200 \text{ kPa}$: $$D_{PWM} = \frac{(2 \times 10^5)(50 \times 10^{-6})}{(10)(1.0)} = \frac{10}{10} = 1.0$$ Still saturating. Let us halve $I_{peak}$ to $0.5 \text{ A}$ for finer resolution: $$D_{PWM} = \frac{(2 \times 10^5)(50 \times 10^{-6})}{(10)(0.5)} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0$$ We observe a linear inverse proportionality — doubling $I_{peak}$ halves $D_{PWM}$. The TCM firmware uses a **closed-loop PID controller** to dynamically adjust $D_{PWM}$ based on the line-pressure sensor feedback, eliminating these open-loop calibration errors. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Shift Decision Tree (Simplified) The TCM executes a hierarchical decision process: | Decision Level | Inputs Considered | Decision Output | |---|---|---| | **L1 — Driver Demand** | TPS, brake status, PRNDL position | Drive mode (P / R / N / D / L / S) | | **L2 — Speed Window** | VSS, engine RPM | Candidate gear range (1st–10th depending on transmission) | | **L3 — Shift Map Lookup** | 2-D map: $\text{rows} = V_{ss}$, $\text{cols} = \theta_{TPS}$ | Target gear $G_{target}$ | | **L4 — Adaptive Correction** | Learned shift quality, ATF temperature, battery voltage | Adjusted shift timing $\Delta t$ | | **L5 — Torque Coordination** | CAN message to ECM: torque reduction request | ECM retards spark, reduces throttle | | **L6 — Hydraulic Actuation** | Solenoid command sequence (SS1, SS2, EPC duty) | Physical gear engagement | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 TCM Functional Architecture — Block-Level Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[VSS
Vehicle Speed Sensor] --> MCU S2[TPS
Throttle Position] --> MCU S3[Engine RPM
via CAN] --> MCU S4[TFT Sensor
ATF Temperature] --> MCU S5[Brake Switch
BPS] --> MCU S6[PRNDL
Gear Selector] --> MCU S7[Line Pressure
Sensor] --> MCU PWR[Battery +12V
SMPS Regulator] --> MCU MCU{TCM
Microcontroller
32-bit Aurix} MCU --> SOL1[Shift Solenoid SS1] MCU --> SOL2[Shift Solenoid SS2] MCU --> SOL3[EPC Pressure
PWM Driver] MCU --> SOL4[TCC Lock-Up
Solenoid] MCU --> CAN[CAN Transceiver
TJA1042] MCU --> DTC[OBD-II MIL
DTC Storage] CAN <--> ECM[Engine ECU] CAN <--> ABS[ABS ESC Module] CAN <--> DIAG[Scan Tool
OBD-II Port] ``` ### 4.2 Shift Decision Sequence Diagram ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant DRV as Driver participant TPS as TPS Sensor participant VSS as VSS Sensor participant TCM as TCM MCU participant ECM as Engine ECU participant HYD as Hydraulic Actuators DRV->>TPS: Press accelerator TPS-->>TCM: Analog voltage 0.8V to 4.2V DRV->>VSS: Vehicle moving VSS-->>TCM: Pulse train 0Hz to 3kHz TCM->>TCM: Look up shift map
f TPS, VSS TCM->>ECM: CAN frame 0x18FEF100
Torque reduction request ECM-->>TCM: CAN ACK
Spark retarded by 10 deg TCM->>HYD: Energize SS1 OFF, SS2 ON
Set EPC PWM 60 percent HYD-->>TCM: Line pressure feedback TCM->>TCM: Closed-loop PID converge
to target pressure TCM->>HYD: Command TCC lock-up
at slip 8 percent ``` ### 4.3 TCM Internal Subsystem Layout ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PWR [Power Supply Stage] BATT[Battery +12V] --> SMPS[SMPS Buck Converter] SMPS --> R5V[+5V Analog Rail] SMPS --> R3V[+3.3V Digital Rail] end subgraph INPUT [Sensor Input Stage] ANA[Analog Inputs
TPS, TFT, Line P] --> FILT[EMI Filter] FILT --> ADC[12-bit Multiplexed ADC] DIG[Digital Inputs
VSS, BPS, PRNDL] --> SCHM[Schmitt Buffer] CAN_IN[CAN Bus] --> CTR[CAN Controller] end subgraph CORE [Processing Core] MCU_LOCK[Lockstep MCU Core 1] MCU_CHK[Lockstep MCU Core 2] COMP[Comparator Logic] MCU_LOCK <--> COMP MCU_CHK <--> COMP end subgraph OUTPUT [Actuator Driver Stage] DRV1[MOSFET Driver] SOLENOID[Shift Solenoids] PWM[PWM Generator 100Hz to 2kHz] DRV1 --> SOLENOID PWM --> DRV1 end ADC --> MCU_LOCK SCHM --> MCU_LOCK CTR <--> MCU_LOCK MCU_LOCK --> PWM R5V --> ADC R3V --> MCU_LOCK ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q1. Define Transmission Control Module (TCM). List any four sensor inputs to the TCM.** **Model Answer:** The Transmission Control Module (TCM) is an embedded electronic control unit that monitors powertrain operating conditions through sensors and controls automatic transmission functions including gear shifting, hydraulic line pressure, and torque converter lock-up via electro-hydraulic actuators. Four sensor inputs are: 1. Vehicle Speed Sensor (VSS) 2. Throttle Position Sensor (TPS) 3. Transmission Fluid Temperature (TFT) sensor 4. Brake Pedal Switch (BPS) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q2. What is the role of the Torque Converter Clutch (TCC) solenoid in the TCM system?** **Model Answer:** The TCC solenoid engages the mechanical lock-up clutch inside the torque converter when the slip ratio $s = (N_e - N_t)/N_e$ falls below a calibrated threshold (typically 5–15 %). This eliminates fluid slippage, improves fuel economy by **3–5 %**, and reduces ATF heat generation. The TCM energizes the solenoid gradually using PWM to avoid driveline shock. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice]** #### **Question A — 14 Marks** **(a) Explain the architecture of a modern Transmission Control Module, including its input, processing, and output stages. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The TCM architecture is organized into four functional stages: **1. Input Stage — Sensor Signal Acquisition:** The TCM receives analog signals from TPS, TFT sensor, and line pressure sensor through a **12-bit multiplexed ADC** after passing through EMI low-pass filters (cut-off ~1 kHz) and TVS protection clamps. Digital signals from VSS, brake switch, and PRNDL pass through **Schmitt-trigger buffers** for noise immunity. High-speed communication with the ECM is handled through a **CAN transceiver** (e.g., TJA1042). **2. Power Supply Stage:** A **switched-mode power supply (SMPS)** buck regulator converts the vehicle's **+12 V** battery voltage to regulated **+5 V** (analog rail) and **+3.3 V** (digital rail). ISO 7637-2 load-dump and reverse-battery protection circuits ensure survival under transient conditions. **3. Processing Stage:** A **32-bit lockstep automotive MCU** (Infineon Aurix TC27x or NXP MPC57xx) executes the real-time shift algorithm. The lockstep dual-core architecture complies with **ASIL-D (ISO 26262)** functional safety standards — both cores execute the same instruction stream, and a comparator logic detects divergence within one clock cycle. The MCU runs a **fixed-time control loop (typically 5–10 ms)** that reads sensors, executes the shift-map lookup, performs PID pressure control, and updates actuator commands. **4. Output Stage — Actuator Driver:** Shift solenoids are switched via **low-side N-channel MOSFETs** with flyback diodes. Pressure Control Solenoids (EPC) receive **PWM signals at 100 Hz – 2 kHz** to modulate average current and hence hydraulic pressure. The TCC solenoid is duty-cycle controlled during engagement to smoothly ramp up clutch pressure. **5. Communication and Diagnostics:** A dedicated **CAN bus interface** enables bi-directional data exchange with ECM, ABS, and BCM. Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs) are stored in **non-volatile memory (EEPROM/Flash)** and retrievable via **OBD-II port** using a scan tool (ISO 15765 / UDS protocol). **(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the working of the torque converter and explain how the TCM controls its lock-up. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** A torque converter consists of three elements: a **pump (driven by engine)**, a **turbine (connected to transmission input shaft)**, and a **stator (mounted on a one-way clutch)**. During launch, fluid is centrifugally pumped outward by the impeller, redirected by the stator onto the turbine blades, producing torque multiplication up to **2.0–2.5×** at stall. The TCM measures both engine RPM (from ECM via CAN) and transmission input shaft speed (from local TISS sensor). It computes the **slip ratio**: $$s = \frac{N_e - N_t}{N_e}$$ The TCM consults a **3-D lock-up map** indexed by vehicle speed, throttle position, and ATF temperature. When $s$ falls below the threshold (typically 5–15 %) and other conditions (warm ATF, no wide-open throttle, no braking) are met, the TCM commands the TCC solenoid to engage. The TCM uses **slip-speed closed-loop control**: it measures instantaneous slip and adjusts the TCC solenoid PWM duty cycle to ramp up clutch pressure at a controlled rate (typically 50–100 kPa/s), achieving full lock-up within 0.5–2.0 seconds without driveline shock. Once locked, slip $s \to 0$ and converter efficiency $\eta_{TC} \to 1.0$, reducing fuel consumption. If ATF is cold (below 20 °C) or engine is at wide-open throttle, lock-up is inhibited to avoid shudder or damage. Fail-safe logic de-energizes the TCC if any sensor signal is lost. --- #### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **(a) Discuss the various input sensors and output actuators interfaced with the TCM, and explain their role in automatic gear shifting. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Input Sensors:** | Sensor | Type | Role in Shift Logic | |---|---|---| | VSS | Hall-effect | Provides road speed; primary index for shift map. | | TPS | Potentiometric | Driver torque demand; selects shift column. | | Engine RPM | CAN from ECM | Computes slip ratio, prevents over-rev. | | TFT Sensor | NTC thermistor | Adapts shift timing to fluid viscosity. | | Brake Switch | Digital | Prevents unintended gear engagement (shift lock). | | PRNDL Sensor | Multi-position | Determines intended gear range (P/R/N/D/L). | | Line Pressure Sensor | Strain-gauge | Closes pressure-control PID loop. | **Output Actuators:** | Actuator | Drive Type | Function | |---|---|---| | Shift Solenoid 1 (SS1) | ON/OFF | Routes ATF to clutch pack A or B. | | Shift Solenoid 2 (SS2) | ON/OFF | Routes ATF to clutch pack C or D. | | EPC Solenoid | PWM (variable) | Modulates main line pressure. | | TCC Solenoid | PWM | Engages torque converter lock-up clutch. | | Shift Lock Solenoid | ON/OFF | Mechanically locks gear lever. | **Role in Gear Shifting:** When driver demand (TPS) and vehicle speed (VSS) cross a calibrated threshold, the TCM consults its shift map to determine the target gear. It then commands a coordinated sequence: (i) ramp down EPC pressure momentarily to unload the off-going clutch, (ii) energize the appropriate shift solenoid to drain/apply ATF, (iii) request ECM torque reduction via CAN to minimize shock, and (iv) ramp up EPC pressure to apply the on-coming clutch — completing the shift within **200–500 ms**. **(b) Explain the PWM-based pressure control strategy used in the TCM, with relevant equations and a worked example. (7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The TCM modulates **line pressure** using Pulse Width Modulation (PWM) of an **Electronic Pressure Control (EPC) solenoid**. The solenoid is an inductive load, typically driven at **$f_{PWM}$ = 100 Hz to 2 kHz**. **Equations:** The PWM duty cycle is: $$D_{PWM} = \frac{t_{on}}{T_{period}}$$ The solenoid force produced is: $$F_{sol} = k_1 \cdot I_{avg} - k_2 \cdot x$$ The resulting hydraulic pressure at the pilot stage is: $$P_{pilot} = \frac{F_{sol}}{A_{armature}} = \frac{k_1 \cdot I_{avg}}{A_{armature}}$$ For a unidirectional PWM-driven solenoid with peak current $I_{peak}$: $$I_{avg} \approx I_{peak} \cdot D_{PWM}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{P_{pilot} \propto D_{PWM}}$$ **Worked Example:** Given: $k_1 = 8 \text{ N/A}$, $I_{peak} = 0.8 \text{ A}$, $A_{armature} = 40 \text{ mm}^2 = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$. Target $P_{pilot} = 320 \text{ kPa} = 3.2 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$: $$D_{PWM} = \frac{P_{pilot} \cdot A_{armature}}{k_1 \cdot I_{peak}} = \frac{(3.2 \times 10^5)(40 \times 10^{-6})}{(8)(0.8)}$$ $$D_{PWM} = \frac{12.8}{6.4} = 2.0$$ Since $D_{PWM} \le 1.0$, the actuator saturates. The firmware must use a **closed-loop PID** that reads the actual line pressure sensor and adjusts $D_{PWM}$ to converge on the target despite non-idealities (hysteresis, temperature drift, supply voltage variation). **PID Form (Discrete Time):** $$D_{PWM}[k] = D_{PWM}[k-1] + K_p \cdot e[k] + K_i \sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] + K_d \big( e[k] - e[k-1] \big)$$ where $e[k] = P_{target} - P_{measured}[k]$ is the pressure error. This real-time correction ensures **±20 kPa** pressure accuracy across all operating conditions, producing smooth and repeatable shift quality. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in TCM Questions:** > 1. **Confusing TCM with ECM** — Many students describe engine functions (fuel injection, ignition) when asked about TCM. Strictly focus on transmission-domain functions. > 2. **Skipping the "Why"** — Merely listing sensors and solenoids without explaining *why* each is needed loses 1–2 marks per sub-question. Always state the functional purpose. > 3. **Forgetting the CAN interface** — The TCM does not operate in isolation; explicit reference to CAN communication with the ECM is mandatory for full marks on coordination questions. > 4. **No closed-loop mention** — When discussing PWM pressure control, students frequently treat it as open-loop.
