Always emphasize the **PID closed-loop** with line-pressure sensor feedback. > 5. **Units omission** — In numerical derivations, missing units (Pa, kPa, mm²) lead to mark deductions under KTU valuation strictness. > 6. **Skipping safety/fail-safe** — Any discussion of shift logic that omits the brake-interlock or fail-safe de-energization is incomplete. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **TCM** is a **dedicated powertrain ECU** controlling automatic transmission functions — gear shifting, line pressure, and torque converter lock-up. - **Sensor inputs**: VSS, TPS, Engine RPM (via CAN), TFT, Brake switch, PRNDL, Line pressure sensor. - **Actuator outputs**: Shift solenoids (ON/OFF), EPC pressure solenoid (PWM), TCC solenoid (PWM), Shift-lock solenoid. - **Core formulas**: - Slip ratio: $s = (N_e - N_t)/N_e$ - Gear ratio: $i = N_{in}/N_{out}$ - Hydraulic force: $P = F/A$ - PWM duty cycle: $D_{PWM} = t_{on}/T_{period}$ - Converter efficiency: $\eta_{TC} = (T_t \cdot \omega_t)/(T_p \cdot \omega_p)$ - **Architecture**: 32-bit lockstep MCU (ASIL-D) + SMPS power stage + ADC input + MOSFET driver output + CAN transceiver. - **Communication**: Powertrain CAN bus at **500 kbps**; UDS protocol for OBD-II diagnostics. - **Safety features**: Brake-shift interlock, fail-safe de-energization, redundant lockstep cores, ISO 26262 compliance. - **Shift quality** depends on **coordinated torque reduction** request from TCM to ECM during gear transitions. - **Closed-loop pressure control** (PID) is essential for shift consistency — open-loop PWM is insufficient. - **Operating envelope**: −40 °C to +125 °C; vibration-resistant aluminum housing; IP6K9K sealing. - **Industry examples**: ZF 8HP, Aisin AWF8, GM 10L80 — all use TCMs as separate ECUs or integrated within the Transmission Control Assembly (TCA).

topics.title: Brake and Stability Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT725 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS IN AUTOMOBILES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Brake and Stability Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Brake and Stability Control System — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Brake and Stability Control System** is a closed-loop, embedded electro-hydraulic (or electro-mechanical) subsystem within a vehicle's chassis domain that continuously monitors wheel kinematics, vehicle dynamics, and driver inputs via distributed sensors, and modulates hydraulic brake-line pressure at individual wheel corners through an Electronic Control Unit (ECU) to prevent wheel lock-up during braking, regulate tractive force during acceleration, and restore directional stability (yaw equilibrium) during cornering or evasive maneuvers.

The umbrella term covers three integrated sub-systems working on a shared sensor bus and hydraulic modulator:

| Sub-System | Core Function | Trigger Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **ABS** (Anti-lock Braking System) | Prevents wheel lock-up | Hard braking, low-μ surface |
| **EBD** (Electronic Brake-force Distribution) | Optimizes front/rear brake split | Every brake event |
| **ESC/ESP** (Electronic Stability Control) | Corrects yaw instability | Oversteer / Understeer detected |
| **TCS** (Traction Control System) | Prevents drive-wheel slip | Acceleration on low-μ surface |
| **BA** (Brake Assist) | Amplifies emergency braking | Panic-brake pedal velocity |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Slippery Floor Ballet:**
> Imagine you are running across a wet marble floor wearing socks. The instant you push too hard, your foot shoots forward and you fall. Now imagine you had a tiny invisible coach riding on each foot who could:
> 1. Feel that your foot is about to slide (slip detection)
> 2. Instantly loosen the grip slightly to let the foot rotate again
> 3. Re-apply the grip the moment rolling is restored
>
> That is exactly what **ABS** does for a car tire. The brake is the "grip," and the embedded controller is the invisible coach.

A **Stability Control System** is analogous to a ballroom dancer's balance reflex: when the body (vehicle) begins to spin faster than the feet (steering) intend, the brain (ESC ECU) selectively presses the foot nearest to the direction of the fall, restoring equilibrium without the dancer consciously thinking about it.

## 1.3 Physical & Regulatory Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metrics (Industry Benchmarks):**
> - **Optimal braking slip ratio (λ_opt):** **0.10 – 0.30** (i.e., 10 % – 30 %)
> - **Peak friction coefficient slip (μ_peak):** occurs at λ ≈ 0.15 on dry asphalt
> - **Maximum usable brake deceleration:** **0.8 g – 1.0 g** on dry tarmac, **0.3 g – 0.5 g** on snow
> - **FMVSS 126 (US Federal Mandate):** All new light vehicles ≤ 4 536 kg must have ESC since **2012**
> - **ECE R13-H (UN/ECE):** Mandates ABS for Category-1 vehicles (passenger cars)
> - **ABS cycle frequency:** **4 – 20 Hz** (typical 8 Hz)
> - **ESC intervention latency:** **≤ 20 ms** from sensor fault to actuator command

---

## 1.4 Why This Module Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

In the **NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education** framework, this topic directly satisfies:

- **CO4 (K3):** *Apply embedded control principles to automotive subsystems.*
- **CO5 (K4):** *Analyze vehicle dynamic control algorithms using sensor data fusion.*

It bridges **Module 3 (Sensor & Actuator Interfacing)** with **Module 5 (ADAS & Autonomous Features)**, making it a **high-weightage, frequently repeated KTU exam topic**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Tire-Road Friction (Foundation of All Brake Control)

The cornering and braking forces a tire can transmit to the road are governed by the **friction circle** (or friction ellipse) concept. The maximum combined longitudinal + lateral force is bounded by the normal load **F_z** times the **friction coefficient μ**.

The fundamental performance curve of a tire is the **μ – λ (slip) characteristic**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Slip Ratio Definition (Braking Mode):**
> $$\lambda = \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v}$$
> where *V_v* is the vehicle's linear velocity and *V_w* is the wheel's tangential velocity at the contact patch.

- When λ = 0 → wheel is **free-rolling** (no brake torque).
- When λ = 1 → wheel is **fully locked** (skidding).
- When 0 < λ < 1 → wheel is in the **partial-slip region** where longitudinal force can still be developed.

A second, lateral slip exists during cornering — the **slip angle α**. During combined braking + cornering, the available friction must be **vectorially shared** between longitudinal and lateral demands.

## 2.2 ABS — Working Principle (Three-Phase Pressure Modulation)

ABS operates as a **closed-loop bang-bang controller** with three discrete hydraulic phases, repeating at 4–20 Hz:

1. **Pressure Build (Apply):** ECU energizes the **Inlet Valve (IV)**; brake pressure rises toward the driver's pedal demand.
2. **Pressure Hold (Maintain):** ECU closes both the **Inlet Valve** and **Outlet Valve (OV)**; pressure is held constant in the wheel cylinder.
3. **Pressure Release (Dump):** ECU opens the **Outlet Valve** and energizes the **Return Pump**; pressure is dumped back to the master cylinder / accumulator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "A-H-R" or "Apply-Hold-Release":**
> Every ABS cycle is one **A-H-R** sequence. Examiners love asking students to draw the pressure-vs-time waveform with the labeled phases.

### Threshold Logic for Phase Switching

| Trigger | From Phase | To Phase |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel deceleration **α_w < −1.5 g** (reference threshold) | Build | **Hold** |
| Wheel deceleration **recovers (α_w ≥ −0.3 g)** | Hold | **Release (Dump)** |
| Wheel re-accelerates to within slip window | Release | **Build** |

The **reference slip λ_ref** is typically dynamically computed as a function of vehicle deceleration:
$$\lambda_{ref} \approx k_1 + k_2 \cdot a_{vehicle}$$
where *k_1* ≈ 0.05 (offset) and *k_2* is a tunable gain (≈ 0.1 s²/m).

## 2.3 EBD — Electronic Brake-force Distribution

EBD replaces the mechanical **load-sensitive proportioning valve** of older vehicles with a software algorithm. As the vehicle decelerates, weight transfers forward (dynamic axle-load shift), increasing the front-axle load and decreasing the rear-axle load. EBD continuously re-balances the rear-wheel pressure to prevent rear-wheel lock-up (which would induce a fatal **spin-out**).

$$F_{z,rear}(t) = F_{z,rear,static} - \Delta F_z \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_{load}}\right)$$

The shift magnitude ΔF_z depends on wheelbase *L*, CG height *h_g*, and deceleration *a_x*:

$$\Delta F_z = \frac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h_g}{L}$$

## 2.4 ESC/ESP — Vehicle Dynamics Control

ESC is a **supervisory control layer** that runs *above* ABS and TCS. Its control law is built on the **bicycle model** (two-wheel linearized dynamic model):

$$\text{Yaw Error} = \psi_{measured} - \psi_{reference}$$

The reference yaw rate is computed from steering angle δ and vehicle speed *V*:
$$\psi_{ref} = \frac{V \cdot \delta}{L \cdot \left(1 + K \cdot V^2\right)}$$
where *K* is the **understeer gradient** (a property of the vehicle's suspension geometry):
$$K = \frac{m \cdot (b \cdot C_{\alpha,r} - a \cdot C_{\alpha,f})}{L \cdot C_{\alpha,f} \cdot C_{\alpha,r}}$$

When **ψ_measured > ψ_ref** → vehicle is **oversteering** → brake the **front-outer wheel**.
When **ψ_measured < ψ_ref** → vehicle is **understeering** → brake the **rear-inner wheel**.

## 2.5 TCS — Traction Control System

TCS uses the same hardware as ABS but operates on the **drive axle** during acceleration:
- If drive-wheel slip λ > λ_limit (typically 0.15) → **reduce engine torque** (via CAN throttle command) AND/OR **apply brake** to the spinning wheel.
- This prevents the drive wheel from "burning rubber" and from causing **loss-of-traction-induced yaw disturbance**.

## 2.6 Sensor & Actuator Suite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Favorite Block Diagram — Memorize the Signal Flow:**

| Sensor | Physical Quantity | Typical Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Wheel Speed Sensor** (Hall-effect or magneto-resistive) | Wheel angular velocity ω | Square wave, frequency f = *z* · ω / 2π |
| **Steering Angle Sensor** | Steering wheel angle δ | CAN message (typically 0° – 1440°) |
| **Yaw Rate Sensor** (MEMS gyroscope) | ψ̇ (rad/s) | Analog 0–5 V or CAN |
| **Lateral Accelerometer** | a_y (m/s²) | Analog or CAN |
| **Master Cylinder Pressure Sensor** | P_mc (bar) | Analog 0.5 – 4.5 V ratiometric |
| **Brake Pedal Switch** | Pedal position | Discrete ON/OFF |

| Actuator | Function |
|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Modulator Unit (HMU)** | Contains IV, OV, DV (Dump Valve), and return pump |
| **Master Cylinder / Brake Booster** | Converts pedal force to hydraulic pressure |
| **Wheel Brake (Caliper + Disc)** | Converts hydraulic pressure to friction torque |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exams — Single Page Revision Table:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{V_v - V_w}{V_v}$ | Braking slip ratio | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega_w \cdot r - V_v}{V_v}$ | Slip in terms of wheel speed | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\alpha_w = \dfrac{V_w(t) - V_w(t-\Delta t)}{\Delta t}$ | Wheel deceleration | m/s² |
| 4 | $\Delta F_z = \dfrac{m \cdot a_x \cdot h_g}{L}$ | Dynamic axle-load transfer | N |
| 5 | $\psi_{ref} = \dfrac{V \cdot \delta}{L \cdot (1 + K \cdot V^2)}$ | Reference yaw rate (bicycle model) | rad/s |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{m \cdot (b \cdot C_{\alpha,r} - a \cdot C_{\alpha,f})}{L \cdot C_{\alpha,f} \cdot C_{\alpha,r}}$ | Understeer gradient | rad/(m/s²) |
| 7 | $F_{x,max} = \mu \cdot F_z$ | Maximum longitudinal tire force | N |
| 8 | $f_{wheel} = z \cdot \dfrac{\omega_w}{2\pi}$ | Tone-wheel frequency | Hz |
| 9 | $T_{brake} = 2 \cdot P_w \cdot A_p \cdot \mu_{pad} \cdot r_{eff}$ | Brake torque per wheel | N·m |
| 10 | $\tau_{ESC} \leq 20\,\text{ms}$ | ESC actuation latency target | ms |

*Note:* For all *C_α* cornering stiffness terms in formulas 5 and 6, the variables represent **C-sub-alpha** (cornering stiffness) — written as *C* with subscript alpha; similarly for *C_sub_alpha_f* and *C_sub_alpha_r* representing front and rear cornering stiffness.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Impact Statistics (Source: NHTSA, IIHS):**
> - ESC reduces **single-vehicle crash risk by 49 %** for SUVs and **33 %** for cars.
> - ABS reduces **fatal motorcycle crashes by 31 %**.
> - Modern ESC systems add only **~3 kg** and **~$75** to vehicle BOM cost.
> - The Bosch ESP® 9.0 ECU has a processing latency of **< 12 ms**.

The same control architecture is now evolving into **Integrated Chassis Control (ICC)** which fuses ABS, ESC, TCS, AFS (Active Front Steering), and Active Suspension into a single domain controller running **AUTOSAR-compliant** real-time software.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimal-Slip Reference Threshold

The ABS algorithm's reference slip ratio λ_ref is not constant — it adapts to road friction. Below is the formal derivation used in production ECUs.

### Step 1: Define the Tire Force-Slip Curve

The longitudinal tire force F_x as a function of slip λ is modelled by the **Pacejka "Magic Formula"** simplified form:
$$F_x(\lambda) = D \cdot \sin(C \cdot \arctan(B \cdot \lambda))$$
where:
- *B* = stiffness factor
- *C* = shape factor
- *D* = peak factor (proportional to μ · F_z)

### Step 2: Compute Peak Slip by Differentiation

To find the slip at which F_x is maximum, differentiate with respect to λ and set equal to zero:
$$\frac{dF_x}{d\lambda} = D \cdot C \cdot \cos(C \cdot \arctan(B \cdot \lambda)) \cdot \frac{B}{1 + (B \cdot \lambda)^2}$$

Setting this to zero requires:
$$C \cdot \arctan(B \cdot \lambda_{peak}) = \frac{\pi}{2}$$
Therefore:
$$\arctan(B \cdot \lambda_{peak}) = \frac{\pi}{2C}$$
$$\lambda_{peak} = \frac{1}{B} \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\pi}{2C}\right)$$

### Step 3: Numerical Evaluation for Typical Tire

For a passenger-car tire, typical magic-formula coefficients are *B* = 10, *C* = 1.6:
$$\lambda_{peak} = \frac{1}{10} \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\pi}{3.2}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{10} \cdot \tan(0.9817)$$

$$= \frac{1}{10} \cdot 1.519$$

$$\approx 0.152$$

This matches the empirical observation that **peak braking force on dry asphalt occurs at ~15 % slip**.

### Step 4: Derive the Reference Slip for the ECU

The ECU does not know *B* and *C* a priori. It uses a **sliding-mode observer** to estimate the peak by detecting inflection of wheel deceleration. The reference threshold is then set as:
$$\lambda_{ref}(t) = \lambda_{peak,est}(t) - \Delta\lambda_{hysteresis}$$
with hysteresis Δλ ≈ 0.03 to prevent chattering.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Bicycle-Model Yaw Reference

### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram

Consider a two-wheel equivalent vehicle with:
- Total mass *m* (kg)
- Yaw moment of inertia *I_z* (kg·m²)
- Distance *a* from CG to front axle, *b* from CG to rear axle
- Wheelbase *L* = *a* + *b*
- Front and rear cornering stiffness *C_α,f*, *C_α,r* (N/rad)

### Step 2: Force Balance (Lateral Direction)

Lateral force equilibrium at CG:
$$m \cdot V \cdot (\dot{\psi} + \dot{\beta}) = F_{y,f} + F_{y,r}$$

where *β* is the sideslip angle at the CG.

For small angles, *F_y,f* ≈ *C_α,f · α_f* and *F_y,r* ≈ *C_α,r · α_r*, with slip angles:
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{a \cdot \dot{\psi}}{V}$$
$$\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{b \cdot \dot{\psi}}{V}$$

### Step 3: Moment Balance (Yaw Direction)

$$I_z \cdot \ddot{\psi} = a \cdot F_{y,f} - b \cdot F_{y,r}$$

### Step 4: Steady-State Solution

For steady cornering, set β̇ = 0 and ψ̈ = 0. Solving the two equations for ψ̇:
$$\dot{\psi}_{ref} = \frac{V \cdot \delta}{L + K \cdot V^2}$$
where the **understeer gradient K** is:
$$K = \frac{m \cdot (b \cdot C_{\alpha,r} - a \cdot C_{\alpha,f})}{L \cdot C_{\alpha,f} \cdot C_{\alpha,r}}$$

### Step 5: Physical Interpretation

- If *K* > 0 → vehicle is **understeering** (push wide); requires more steering as speed rises.
- If *K* < 0 → vehicle is **oversteering** (snap); requires less steering as speed rises.
- If *K* = 0 → vehicle is **neutral-steer**.

For typical passenger cars, *K* ≈ **0.0005 to 0.002 rad·s²/m**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Brake-Control Calculations

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked numerical examples a KTU examiner may set:**

### Example A — Slip Ratio Calculation

Given: Vehicle speed *V_v* = 25 m/s, wheel angular speed ω_w = 90 rad/s, tire radius *r* = 0.32 m.

**Step 1:** Compute wheel tangential velocity.
$$V_w = \omega_w \cdot r = 90 \times 0.32 = 28.8 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply slip formula.
$$\lambda = \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v} = \frac{25 - 28.8}{25} = \frac{-3.8}{25}$$

$$\lambda = -0.152 \text{ (negative ⇒ wheel spinning faster ⇒ tractive / TCS scenario)}$$

### Example B — Axle-Load Transfer

Given: *m* = 1500 kg, *a_x* = 8 m/s², *h_g* = 0.55 m, *L* = 2.6 m.

$$\Delta F_z = \frac{1500 \times 8 \times 0.55}{2.6} = \frac{6600}{2.6} \approx 2538 \text{ N}$$

So during hard braking, **~254 kg** of effective load shifts from rear to front axle.

### Example C — Reference Yaw Rate

Given: *V* = 20 m/s, δ = 0.05 rad (~2.86°), *L* = 2.6 m, *K* = 0.001 rad·s²/m.

$$\psi_{ref} = \frac{20 \times 0.05}{2.6 \times (1 + 0.001 \times 400)}$$

$$= \frac{1.0}{2.6 \times 1.4}$$

$$= \frac{1.0}{3.64} \approx 0.275 \text{ rad/s} \approx 15.7 \text{ °/s}$$

The ESC ECU will compare the measured yaw rate from the gyroscope against this 15.7 °/s reference.

## 3.4 ECU Sampling and Scheduling — Real-Time Constraint

The ABS/ESC ECU must execute one control loop within a deterministic time budget:

| Task | Execution Time | Period |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel-speed ISR (4 wheels) | 50 µs | 5 ms |
| Vehicle-speed estimator | 200 µs | 10 ms |
| Slip-ratio computation | 150 µs | 10 ms |
| State machine update | 300 µs | 10 ms |
| Valve-driver PWM update | 100 µs | 5 ms |
| CAN transmit to other ECUs | 250 µs | 20 ms |
| **Total worst-case** | **1.05 ms** | **5 ms** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPU Utilization Check:**
> $$\text{CPU Utilization} = \frac{1.05 \text{ ms}}{5 \text{ ms}} = 21\%$$
> This leaves a **79 % safety margin**, satisfying the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) requirement** for worst-case execution under the Liu & Layland test:
> $$U \leq n \cdot (2^{1/n} - 1)$$
> For *n* = 7 tasks, the bound is **41.2 %**, comfortably exceeded.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Integrated ABS/ESC System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    sensA["Wheel Speed Sensor FL"] --> ecA["Front Left Signal Conditioner"]
    sensB["Wheel Speed Sensor FR"] --> ecB["Front Right Signal Conditioner"]
    sensC["Wheel Speed Sensor RL"] --> ecC["Rear Left Signal Conditioner"]
    sensD["Wheel Speed Sensor RR"] --> ecD["Rear Right Signal Conditioner"]

    sensA2["Steering Angle Sensor"] --> canBus["CAN Bus 500 kbps"]
    sensB2["Yaw Rate Gyroscope"] --> canBus
    sensC2["Lateral Accelerometer"] --> canBus
    sensD2["Master Cylinder Pressure Sensor"] --> canBus

    ecA --> escEcu["ABS ESC ECU Freescale S32K344"]
    ecB --> escEcu
    ecC --> escEcu
    ecD --> escEcu
    canBus --> escEcu

    escEcu --> stateMachine{"ABS / TCS / ESC State Machine"}

    stateMachine -->|Inlet Valve PWM| ivFL["Front Left Inlet Solenoid"]
    stateMachine -->|Inlet Valve PWM| ivFR["Front Right Inlet Solenoid"]
    stateMachine -->|Inlet Valve PWM| ivRL["Rear Left Inlet Solenoid"]
    stateMachine -->|Inlet Valve PWM| ivRR["Rear Right Inlet Solenoid"]

    stateMachine -->|Outlet Valve PWM| ovFL["Front Left Outlet Solenoid"]
    stateMachine -->|Outlet Valve PWM| ovFR["Front Right Outlet Solenoid"]
    stateMachine -->|Outlet Valve PWM| ovRL["Rear Left Outlet Solenoid"]
    stateMachine -->|Outlet Valve PWM| ovRR["Rear Right Outlet Solenoid"]

    stateMachine -->|PWM Control| retPump["Return Pump Motor Driver"]

    ivFL --> brakeFL["Wheel Brake Caliper FL"]
    ivFR --> brakeFR["Wheel Brake Caliper FR"]
    ivRL --> brakeRL["Wheel Brake Caliper RL"]
    ivRR --> brakeRR["Wheel Brake Caliper RR"]

    stateMachine -->|CAN Throttle Command| ecuECM["Engine ECM Torque Reduction"]
    stateMachine -->|Torque Vector| dcu["Drive Control Unit for AWD"]
```

## 4.2 ABS Hydraulic Pressure Cycle — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Build
    Build --> Hold : Wheel deceleration below threshold
    Hold --> Release : Wheel deceleration recovers
    Release --> Build : Wheel re-acceleration completed
    Build --> Hold : Repeated trigger
    Hold --> Release : Repeated trigger
    Release --> Build : Repeated trigger
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for the exam:** Each transition arrow corresponds to one of the three ABS phases. The closed loop represents the bang-bang cycling at ~8 Hz.

## 4.3 ESC Intervention Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Read Steering Angle delta"] --> computeRef["Compute Reference Yaw Rate psi_ref"]
    computeRef --> readMeas["Read Measured Yaw Rate psi_meas"]
    readMeas --> error["Yaw Error e = psi_meas - psi_ref"]
    error -->|e greater than threshold| overSteer{"e greater than zero"}
    error -->|e less than negative threshold| underSteer{"e less than zero"}
    overSteer -->|Yes| actA["Brake Front Outer Wheel"]
    underSteer -->|Yes| actB["Brake Rear Inner Wheel"]
    actA --> engineCmd["Reduce Engine Torque via CAN"]
    actB --> engineCmd
    engineCmd --> endNode["Update at next 5 ms cycle"]
    overSteer -->|No| endNode
    underSteer -->|No| endNode
```

## 4.4 Sensor-to-Actuator Signal Flow (KTU Board Drawing Standard)

```
[DRIVER]                              [ROAD]
    |                                    |
[Brake Pedal]                     [Tire Contact Patch]
    |                                    |
    v                                    v
[Master Cylinder]  ----PRESSURE--->  [Wheel Brake]
    |                                    |
    v                                    ^
[P_mc Sensor]                          [Wheel Speed Sensor]
    |                                    |
    +-------[ CAN / SPI Bus ]--------+   |
                       |                |
                       v                |
              [ ABS / ESC ECU ]---------+
                       |
        +--------------+--------------+
        |              |              |
   [Solenoid       [Return        [Pump Motor
    Valves]        Pump]          Driver]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Always label arrows with the physical variable (*Pressure, Wheel Speed, CAN Message*). Examiners award 2 marks per correctly labeled signal flow on a full system block diagram.

## 4.5 Memory Aid — The "BRAKES" Acronym for KTU Viva

> [!NOTE]
> **B** – Brake pedal switch & pressure sensor
> **R** – Return pump + accumulator
> **A** – ABS valve block (IV, OV)
> **K** – (sneak in) Yaw rate sensor **K**inematic measurement
> **E** – ESC ECU + Engine torque CAN
> **S** – Steering angle + Slip computation
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each question carries 3 marks. Answer in approximately 80–100 words with a labeled diagram where applicable.*

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the **braking slip ratio λ** and explain why the maximum longitudinal tire force occurs at a slip ratio of approximately 15 % rather than 100 %.

**Model Answer:**

The braking slip ratio is defined as:
$$\lambda = \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v}$$
where *V_v* is vehicle linear velocity and *V_w* is the wheel tangential velocity (*V_w* = ω·*r*).

At λ = 1 the wheel is fully locked (kinetic friction), which is **lower** than the **static friction** available when the tire is still rolling with a small slip. The μ–λ curve peaks between 0.10 and 0.30 (≈ 0.15 on dry asphalt) because rubber deformation hysteresis produces maximum energy dissipation at that partial slip. Beyond λ ≈ 0.30, μ decreases rapidly into the unstable region. Hence ABS controls wheel slip within the 10–30 % window to maintain peak friction.

*(3 marks — complete with formula, diagram, and explanation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
List the **major components of a hydraulic ABS modulator unit** and state the function of each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inlet Valve (IV)** | Normally-open solenoid; when energized, isolates the wheel cylinder from the master cylinder |
| 2 | **Outlet Valve (OV)** | Normally-closed solenoid; when energized, releases wheel pressure to the accumulator |
| 3 | **Return Pump** | Re-pumps fluid from the accumulator back to the master cylinder during pressure-release phase |
| 4 | **Accumulator** | Low-pressure reservoir for released brake fluid |
| 5 | **ECU** | Runs the slip-control state machine and drives the solenoids |
| 6 | **Wheel Speed Sensors** | Provide ω feedback for each of the 4 wheels |

*(3 marks — all six components named with one-line functions)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> *Each Part B question is worth 14 marks and contains two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks, OR a full 14-mark design problem.*

---

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a block diagram, describe the **closed-loop operation of an Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)**. Identify each block and explain the three phases of brake-pressure modulation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A passenger car of mass **1500 kg** is traveling at **25 m/s**. The driver initiates emergency braking. The ABS computes a reference slip ratio of **0.18**. The wheel radius is **0.32 m**.
1. Calculate the **target wheel angular velocity** during the controlled-braking phase.
2. If the coefficient of friction μ = 0.85 and the wheel load is 4500 N during braking, determine the **maximum longitudinal braking force**.
3. If the brake hydraulic pressure required is **40 bar**, calculate the **required caliper piston area** for the front wheel (assume two pistons, μ_pad = 0.4, effective radius r_eff = 0.12 m).

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question A

**(a) [7 Marks] Block Diagram and Three-Phase Operation:**

**Block Diagram:**
```
[PEDAL] → [MASTER CYLINDER] → [INLET VALVE] → [WHEEL CYLINDER] → [BRAKE CALIPER]
   ↑                                                                       ↓
   ←←←←←←←←← [ABS ECU] ←←←← [WHEEL SPEED SENSOR] ←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←←
```

**Three Phases:**

**Phase 1 — Pressure Build (Apply):** The driver presses the brake pedal. The ECU detects that wheel slip is below the reference (0.18). The Inlet Valve (IV) is de-energized (normally-open) so brake fluid flows freely from the master cylinder to the wheel cylinder. Brake pressure rises and wheel deceleration increases.

**Phase 2 — Pressure Hold (Maintain):** When wheel deceleration α_w falls below a critical threshold (≈ −1.5 g) or when wheel slip exceeds λ_ref = 0.18, the ECU energizes both the IV (closes it) and the OV. Pressure in the wheel cylinder is held constant. This prevents further lock-up.

**Phase 3 — Pressure Release (Dump):** If the wheel still decelerates too rapidly, the OV is energized, opening the dump path to the accumulator. The return pump drives the fluid back to the master cylinder. Wheel re-accelerates. Once within the slip window, the cycle returns to Phase 1.

**Cycle Frequency:** Approximately **8 Hz**, producing the characteristic pulsation felt at the brake pedal.

*(7 marks — full diagram with labeled arrows + three phases explained)*

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Solution:**

**Part 1 — Target wheel angular velocity:**

Slip ratio definition (braking):
$$\lambda = \frac{V_v - V_w}{V_v}$$

We require λ = 0.18, *V_v* = 25 m/s. Solve for *V_w*:
$$V_w = V_v \cdot (1 - \lambda) = 25 \times (1 - 0.18)$$
$$V_w = 25 \times 0.82 = 20.5 \text{ m/s}$$

Now *V_w* = ω·*r*, so:
$$\omega_{target} = \frac{V_w}{r} = \frac{20.5}{0.32}$$
$$\omega_{target} = 64.06 \text{ rad/s} \approx 612 \text{ RPM}$$

**Part 2 — Maximum longitudinal braking force:**
$$F_{x,max} = \mu \cdot F_z = 0.85 \times 4500$$
$$F_{x,max} = 3825 \text{ N}$$

**Part 3 — Required caliper piston area:**

Brake torque from hydraulic pressure:
$$T_{brake} = 2 \cdot P_w \cdot A_p \cdot \mu_{pad} \cdot r_{eff}$$

This torque must equal the friction torque from the tire:
$$T_{brake} = F_{x,max} \cdot r = 3825 \times 0.32 = 1224 \text{ N·m}$$

Now solve for *A_p*:
$$A_p = \frac{T_{brake}}{2 \cdot P_w \cdot \mu_{pad} \cdot r_{eff}}$$

$$A_p = \frac{1224}{2 \times 40 \times 10^5 \times 0.4 \times 0.12}$$

Note: **40 bar = 40 × 10⁵ Pa = 4 × 10⁶ Pa**

$$A_p = \frac{1224}{2 \times 4 \times 10^6 \times 0.4 \times 0.12}$$

$$A_p = \frac{1224}{3.84 \times 10^5}$$

$$A_p \approx 3.19 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 = 31.9 \text{ cm}^2$$

Since there are two pistons, each piston area:
$$A_{piston} = \frac{31.9}{2} \approx 15.95 \text{ cm}^2$$

Piston diameter:
$$d = \sqrt{\frac{4 \cdot A_{piston}}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 15.95 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi}} \approx 0.045 \text{ m} \approx 45 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Answer:** Each caliper piston should have a diameter of **≈ 45 mm**.

*(7 marks — three sub-parts, each fully worked)*

---

### QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **bicycle model** used in Electronic Stability Control. Derive the expression for the **reference yaw rate** of a vehicle during steady-state cornering, clearly defining all symbols.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vehicle is traveling at **30 m/s** on a curve. The steering angle is **0.06 rad**. The wheelbase *L* = 2.7 m, and the understeer gradient *K* = 0.0015 rad·s²/m. Compute:
1. The **reference yaw rate** for ESC comparison.
2. The **sideslip angle β** at the CG, assuming steady-state and small-angle conditions (front cornering stiffness *C_α,f* = 80 000 N/rad, rear *C_α,r* = 70 000 N/rad, mass *m* = 1600 kg, *a* = 1.2 m, *b* = 1.5 m).
3. If the measured yaw rate is **0.42 rad/s**, determine whether the ESC should apply the **front-outer** or **rear-inner** brake and explain why.

---

### MODEL ANSWER — Question B

**(a) [7 Marks] Bicycle Model and Yaw Reference Derivation:**

The bicycle model is a two-degree-of-freedom simplification that lumps each axle into a single wheel. The state variables are:
- Sideslip angle β at the CG (rad)
- Yaw rate ψ̇ (rad/s)

**Assumptions:**
1. Small slip angles so lateral force ≈ *C_α* · α
2. Constant forward speed *V*
3. Symmetric left-right vehicle

**Governing Equations:**

Lateral force equilibrium at CG:
$$m \cdot V \cdot (\dot{\psi} + \dot{\beta}) = C_{\alpha,f} \cdot \alpha_f + C_{\alpha,r} \cdot \alpha_r$$

Yaw moment equilibrium about CG:
$$I_z \cdot \ddot{\psi} = a \cdot C_{\alpha,f} \cdot \alpha_f - b \cdot C_{\alpha,r} \cdot \alpha_r$$

Slip angles at front and rear tires:
$$\alpha_f = \delta - \beta - \frac{a \cdot \dot{\psi}}{V}$$
$$\alpha_r = -\beta + \frac{b \cdot \dot{\psi}}{V}$$

For **steady-state cornering**, set β̇ = 0 and ψ̈ = 0. Solving simultaneously yields:
$$\dot{\psi}_{ref} = \frac{V \cdot \delta}{L + K \cdot V^2}$$

where the understeer gradient is:
$$K = \frac{m \cdot (b \cdot C_{\alpha,r} - a \cdot C_{\alpha,f})}{L \cdot C_{\alpha,f} \cdot C_{\alpha,r}}$$

If *K* > 0 → understeer, if *K* < 0 → oversteer, if *K* = 0 → neutral steer.

*(7 marks — complete derivation with all symbols defined)*

**(b) [7 Marks] Numerical Solution:**

**Part 1 — Reference yaw rate:**

$$\psi_{ref} = \frac{V \cdot \delta}{L + K \cdot V^2}$$

$$\psi_{ref} = \frac{30 \times 0.06}{2.7 + 0.0015 \times 900}$$

Denominator: *L* + *K·V²* = 2.7 + 1.35 = 4.05

$$\psi_{ref} = \frac{1.8}{4.05} \approx 0.444 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Part 2 — Sideslip angle β:**

For steady-state, β is obtained from the lateral force balance:
$$\beta = \frac{b - m \cdot a \cdot V^2 / (L \cdot C_{\alpha,r}) \cdot (1/V)}{L \cdot (1 + K \cdot V^2)} \cdot \delta$$

Simpler working form using β ≈ 0 approximation:
$$\beta \approx \delta - \frac{L \cdot \psi_{ref}}{V}$$

$$\beta \approx 0.06 - \frac{2.7 \times 0.444}{30}$$

$$\beta \approx 0.06 - 0.04 = 0.02 \text{ rad} \approx 1.15°$$

**Part 3 — ESC intervention decision:**

Measured yaw rate ψ_meas = **0.42 rad/s**
Reference yaw rate ψ_ref = **0.444 rad/s**

Yaw error *e* = ψ_meas − ψ_ref = 0.42 − 0.444 = **−0.024 rad/s** (negative)

Since ψ_meas < ψ_ref, the vehicle is **understeering** (plowing wide through the corner; the front tires have lost lateral grip and the vehicle is not rotating as much as the steering input demands).

**Corrective action:** ESC should brake the **rear-inner wheel** (the wheel on the inside of the turn, at the rear axle). This creates a yaw moment in the direction of the intended turn, helping the vehicle rotate and reducing understeer.

*(7 marks — three sub-parts with full justification of the ESC decision)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas (as observed in KTU Dec 2023 valuation reports):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion** of pressure from bar to Pa (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa). Always convert before substituting into N·m equations.
> 2. **Sign convention slip ratio error:** Students often write *V_w − V_v* instead of *V_v − V_w*. The sign determines whether braking or driving torque is implied.
> 3. **Missing the hysteresis offset Δλ** in ABS reference computation — examiners award 1 mark specifically for mentioning it.
> 4. **Not defining all symbols** in the bicycle model. Every new symbol (*a, b, L, C_α, I_z, δ*) must be defined in the answer.
> 5. **Confusing EBD with ABS** in long-answer questions. EBD = *brake force distribution during normal braking*; ABS = *anti-lock modulation during emergency braking*. They are different functions running on the same hardware.
> 6. **Omitting the "why" behind ESC intervention.** Simply saying "apply brake" without identifying which wheel and why loses 2–3 marks.
> 7. **Calculation precision:** Always retain at least 4 significant figures in intermediate steps and present the final answer to 3 sig figs with units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print this page before entering the exam hall:**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- **ABS** uses a **3-phase bang-bang controller:** Apply → Hold → Release.
- **Optimal slip ratio** λ_opt ≈ **0.15 on dry asphalt**, ≈ 0.10 on wet, ≈ 0.05 on ice.
- **Slip ratio formula:** λ = (V_v − V_w) / V_v (braking) or λ = (V_w − V_v) / max(V_v, V_w) (traction).
- **ESC core logic:** compare ψ_meas vs ψ_ref; brake front-outer for oversteer; brake rear-inner for understeer.
- **Yaw reference formula:** ψ_ref = V·δ / (L + K·V²).
- **Understeer gradient K** has units of rad·s²/m; typical passenger car: 0.0005 to 0.002.

### Sensors You Must Be Able to Draw and Explain
- Wheel Speed Sensor (Hall-effect, tone wheel, *f = z·ω / 2π*)
- Steering Angle Sensor (multiturn potentiometer or Hall + gear)
- Yaw Rate Sensor (MEMS gyroscope)
- Lateral Accelerometer (MEMS capacitive)
- Master Cylinder Pressure Sensor (ratiometric 0.5–4.5 V)
- Brake Pedal Switch (dual-contact for plausibility)

### Actuators You Must Be Able to Draw
- Inlet Valve (normally open, holds pressure when energized)
- Outlet Valve (normally closed, dumps pressure when energized)
- Return Pump (DC motor driven, recharges accumulator)
- Wheel Brake Caliper (floating or fixed)

### Critical Standards & Regulations
- **FMVSS 126** — ESC mandatory in US light vehicles since 2012.
- **ECE R13-H** — ABS mandatory for passenger cars.
- **ISO 26262** — ASIL-D for brake-by-wire ECUs.
- **ASIL-D target latency:** < 20 ms sensor-to-actuator.

### Equations Flash-Card (Memorize These Five)
1. λ = (V_v − V_w) / V_v
2. ΔF_z = m·a_x·h_g / L
3. ψ_ref = V·δ / (L + K·V²)
4. K = m·(b·C_α,r − a·C_α,f) / (L·C_α,f·C_α,r)
5. f_wheel = z·ω / (2π)

### One-Line Takeaway
> **"Brake control systems transform a passive mechanical brake into a smart, sensor-aware, closed-loop actuator that maximizes friction, prevents lock-up, and keeps the vehicle on the driver's intended path."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Navigation and Telematics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT725 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS IN AUTOMOBILES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Navigation and Telematics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Navigation in Automobiles?

**Navigation** in an automotive context refers to the embedded system's ability to continuously determine the vehicle's geographic position, plot it on a digital map, and provide turn-by-turn route guidance from a source to a destination. It is the real-time spatial awareness subsystem of the car.

From the KTU 2024 syllabus perspective, **Automotive Navigation** is an embedded application of the **Global Positioning System (GPS)** integrated with **dead reckoning sensors** (wheel speed, gyroscope/IMU), **digital map databases**, and a **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** running on a dedicated ECU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):** *Automotive Navigation is the process of determining the real-time geographic position of a vehicle using satellite-based positioning systems augmented by onboard sensors, mapping the position onto a digital road network, and providing optimized route guidance to the driver.*

## 1.2 What is Telematics?

**Telematics** is the integrated use of **telecommunications** and **informatics** (data processing). In automobiles, it refers to the embedded system that collects, transmits, receives, and processes vehicle data over a **wireless network** (GSM/4G/5G) between the vehicle and a remote server (cloud/backend).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Syllabus Standard):** *Automotive Telematics is a vehicle-mounted embedded system comprising a wireless communication modem, a GPS receiver, and a microcontroller that together enable bidirectional data exchange between the vehicle and a remote service center for applications such as fleet management, emergency response (eCall), remote diagnostics, insurance telemetry, and stolen-vehicle recovery.*

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Navigation Analogy — "The Blindfolded Hiker with a Compass and Map":**
Imagine you are blindfolded on a mountain. You cannot see, but you carry three things:
1. A **compass** (gyroscope/IMU) → tells you *which direction* you are facing.
2. A **pedometer** (wheel-speed sensor) → tells you *how far* you have walked.
3. A **walkie-talkie** to a friend on a satellite (GPS) → the friend says *"You are 3 km from me, 5 km from another friend, and 4 km from the third."*

By drawing three circles of those radii, only **one point** is common — your exact location. This is **trilateration**, the principle behind GPS navigation.

**Telematics Analogy — "The Doctor's Health Monitoring Watch":**
Just as a smartwatch continuously reports your heart rate, oxygen level, and fall detection to your doctor over a cellular network, **telematics continuously reports a vehicle's speed, location, fuel level, engine health, and crash status to a service center** over a cellular network. The "doctor" (cloud server) can prescribe actions — dispatch an ambulance, alert the owner, or schedule maintenance.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} \approx 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **GPS signal frequency (L1 band):** $f_{L1} = 1575.42 \text{ MHz}$
- **GPS signal frequency (L2 band):** $f_{L2} = 1227.60 \text{ MHz}$
- **GPS signal frequency (L5 band):** $f_{L5} = 1176.45 \text{ MHz}$
- **Number of GPS satellites in MEO orbit:** Minimum **24** (operationally 31+)
- **Orbital altitude of GPS satellites:** $\approx 20{,}200 \text{ km}$
- **Orbital period:** $\approx 11 \text{ hours } 58 \text{ minutes}$ (half a sidereal day)
- **Standard update rate for vehicle navigation:** **1 Hz** (1 position update per second)
- **Standard accuracy of civilian GPS (SPS):** $\leq 7.8 \text{ m}$ (95% probability) horizontally
- **eCall response time mandated by EU:** Target **< 60 seconds** from incident to PSAP (Public Safety Answering Point)
- **CAN bus data rate (Classical):** **500 kbps**; (CAN-FD): up to **2 Mbps**

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 expects students to clearly distinguish between **Navigation** (localization + guidance) and **Telematics** (wireless data exchange). These are often confused because the *same GPS module* serves both, but the *purpose* and *communication partner* differ.

## 1.5 Why Navigation and Telematics Matter in Modern Vehicles

| Aspect | Navigation Role | Telematics Role |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Sensor | GPS + IMU + Wheel speed | GPS + GSM/4G modem + CAN bus |
| Communication | One-way (satellite → vehicle) | Two-way (vehicle ↔ cloud) |
| Output | Position on map + voice guidance | Data packets to remote server |
| Key Application | Driver assistance (ADAS level 0–1) | Fleet management, eCall, UBI |
| Latency Requirement | Low (real-time) | Variable (sec–min) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of an Automotive Navigation System

The navigation ECU integrates three subsystems:

1. **Positioning Subsystem** — GPS receiver + IMU + wheel-speed sensor.
2. **Map Subsystem** — Digital map stored in flash/HDD/SSD with road network topology (graph data structure: nodes = intersections, edges = road segments).
3. **Guidance Subsystem** — Routing algorithm (Dijkstra / A*) + HMI (display + voice).

> [!NOTE]
> The positioning subsystem is fundamentally a **sensor fusion** problem. GPS gives absolute position but is noisy and may be blocked in tunnels; IMU gives relative motion but drifts over time. A **Kalman Filter** fuses both to yield a smooth, continuous estimate.

## 2.2 GPS — Operating Principle (Trilateration)

GPS uses **one-way time-of-flight (ToF)** ranging. Each satellite continuously broadcasts a signal containing:
- The satellite's **ephemeris** (its own precise orbital position)
- The **almanac** (coarse orbit data of all satellites)
- A **pseudo-random noise (PRN) code** unique to that satellite
- A **timestamp** from the satellite's atomic clock

The receiver measures the **time delay** $\Delta t$ between signal transmission and reception. Since the signal travels at $c$ (speed of light), the **pseudorange** is:

$$\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$$

> [!WARNING]
> The term **pseudo**range (not "range") is used because the receiver's clock is not synchronized with the satellite's atomic clock. Therefore, an unknown **clock bias** $b$ contaminates the measurement:
> $$\rho = r + c \cdot b$$
> where $r$ is the true geometric range and $b$ is the receiver clock error in seconds.

## 2.3 Position Fix from Satellites

For a 3D position fix $(x, y, z)$ plus clock bias $b$, we need **at least 4 satellites** to solve 4 unknowns.

The pseudorange equation for satellite $i$:

$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot b$$

for $i = 1, 2, 3, 4$.

Linearizing around an approximate position $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0)$ and solving by least-squares yields the user's ECEF (Earth-Centered Earth-Fixed) position.

## 2.4 Dilution of Precision (DOP)

**DOP** is a dimensionless number that expresses how satellite geometry affects position accuracy. Better geometry (satellites spread out) → lower DOP → better accuracy.

| DOP Type | Stands For | Measures |
|---|---|---|
| GDOP | Geometric | Combined position + time |
| PDOP | Positional | 3D position only |
| HDOP | Horizontal | Latitude + Longitude only |
| VDOP | Vertical | Altitude only |
| TDOP | Time | Clock bias only |

$$\text{GDOP} = \sqrt{\text{trace}(H^T H)^{-1}}$$

where $H$ is the geometry matrix of unit vectors from receiver to satellites. A DOP **< 2** is excellent, **2–5** is good, **> 10** is poor.

## 2.5 Dead Reckoning (DR)

Used when GPS is unavailable (tunnels, urban canyons). Vehicle position is updated by integrating sensor data:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cdot \cos(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t$$
$$y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \cdot \sin(\theta_k) \cdot \Delta t$$
$$\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \cdot \Delta t$$

where $v_k$ is speed (from wheel-speed sensor), $\theta_k$ is heading (from gyroscope/IMU), and $\omega_k$ is yaw rate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Dead reckoning **drifts** because each integration accumulates sensor error. Drift rate of a MEMS gyroscope is typically **0.01–1°/s**. A Kalman filter re-corrects DR using GPS when the GPS signal returns.

## 2.6 Map Matching

Map matching aligns the GPS/DR track with the digital road network. The most common method is the **point-to-curve** approach:
- For each GPS point, find the nearest road segment within a threshold (typically **15–30 m**).
- Use a Hidden Markov Model (HMM) or weighted-Dijkstra to pick the most probable road given continuity of travel.

## 2.7 Telematics System Architecture

A typical automotive telematics control unit (TCU) contains:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| MCU / SoC | Runs application logic (often ARM Cortex-A class) |
| GNSS Module | GPS/GLONASS/Galileo/BeiDou reception |
| Cellular Modem | 4G LTE / 5G modem with SIM slot (eUICC) |
| CAN Transceiver | Reads vehicle bus (CAN, LIN) |
| Accelerometer (3-axis) | Detects crash (for eCall) |
| Backup Battery | Operates modem after main power loss (eCall) |
| Audio I/O | Hands-free voice for emergency call |
| Gyro (sometimes) | Rollover detection |

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\rho = c \cdot \Delta t$ | Pseudorange from time-of-flight | $\rho$ in **meters** |
| 2 | $\rho_i = r_i + c b$ | Pseudorange with receiver clock bias $b$ | $b$ in **seconds** |
| 3 | $r_i = \sqrt{(x_i-x)^2+(y_i-y)^2+(z_i-z)^2}$ | True geometric range to satellite $i$ | **meters** |
| 4 | GDOP, PDOP, HDOP, VDOP, TDOP | Geometry-based accuracy degradation | **dimensionless** |
| 5 | $x_{k+1} = x_k + v_k \cos\theta_k \Delta t$ | Dead reckoning X-update | **meters** |
| 6 | $y_{k+1} = y_k + v_k \sin\theta_k \Delta t$ | Dead reckoning Y-update | **meters** |
| 7 | $\theta_{k+1} = \theta_k + \omega_k \Delta t$ | Dead reckoning heading update | **radians** |
| 8 | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s | Speed of light (signal propagation) | **m/s** |
| 9 | $f_{L1} = 1575.42$ MHz | GPS L1 carrier frequency | **MHz** |
| 10 | $f_d = \frac{v}{\lambda} \cos\alpha$ | Doppler shift from satellite motion | **Hz** |
| 11 | Min 4 satellites | Required for 3D position + clock | **count** |
| 12 | $T_{orbit} = 11\text{ h }58\text{ min}$ | GPS satellite orbital period | **hours** |
| 13 | eCall target latency | Crash-to-PSAP notification time | **< 60 s** |

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems):** Navigation data feeds adaptive cruise control and lane-keeping.
- **Fleet Management:** Logistics companies (e.g., DHL, FedEx) use telematics to track 100,000+ vehicles in real time, optimizing routes and fuel.
- **Insurance (UBI — Usage-Based Insurance):** Telematics records driving behavior (hard braking, speeding) to personalize premiums. Companies like Progressive (Snapshot) use this.
- **eCall (EU mandated since 2018):** All new cars in the EU must auto-dial 112 with location in a crash. Reduces emergency response time by **~40% in rural areas** and saves an estimated **2,500 lives/year in Europe**.
- **Stolen Vehicle Tracking:** Systems like OnStar can remotely slow a stolen car.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Pseudorange Equation from First Principles

### Step 1 — Signal Transmission and Reception
A GPS satellite $S_i$ transmits a signal at time $t^s$ (satellite time). The receiver captures it at time $t^r$ (receiver time). The propagation delay is:

$$\Delta t_i = t^r - t^s$$

### Step 2 — Convert Time Delay to Range
Since the signal is electromagnetic radiation traveling at $c$:

$$\rho_i = c \cdot \Delta t_i = c \cdot (t^r - t^s)$$

### Step 3 — Account for Clock Bias
In reality, the receiver clock has an unknown offset $b$ relative to GPS system time, and the satellite clock has a small known offset $\delta_i$ (corrected using ephemeris). Therefore:

$$t^r_{\text{true}} = t^r_{\text{measured}} + b$$
$$t^s_{\text{true}} = t^s_{\text{measured}} + \delta_i$$

The corrected pseudorange becomes:

$$\rho_i = c \cdot \bigl[(t^r + b) - (t^s + \delta_i)\bigr] = c(t^r - t^s) + c(b - \delta_i)$$

Since $\delta_i$ is known and small, we group $b - \delta_i \approx b$ (absorbed into receiver bias):

$$\rho_i = r_i + c \cdot b$$

where $r_i$ is the **true geometric slant range**.

### Step 4 — Expand Geometric Range
The true range from receiver $(x, y, z)$ to satellite $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ in ECEF coordinates:

$$r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2}$$

### Step 5 — Final Pseudorange Equation

$$\boxed{\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot b}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Solving the GPS Equations (Linearization Method)

We have **4 unknowns** $(x, y, z, b)$ and **4 equations** (one per satellite). Direct solution is non-linear. We linearize using Taylor expansion.

### Step 1 — Initial Guess
Take an approximate position $(x_0, y_0, z_0)$ and bias $b_0$. Compute the predicted pseudorange:

$$\rho_{i,0} = \sqrt{(x_i - x_0)^2 + (y_i - y_0)^2 + (z_i - z_0)^2} + c b_0$$

### Step 2 — Taylor Expansion (First Order)
For small corrections $(\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z, \Delta b)$:

$$\rho_i = \rho_{i,0} + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x}\Delta x + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y}\Delta y + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z}\Delta z + \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial b}\Delta b + \text{higher order terms}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Partial Derivatives

$$\frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial x} = \frac{-(x_i - x_0)}{r_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial y} = \frac{-(y_i - y_0)}{r_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial z} = \frac{-(z_i - z_0)}{r_{i,0}}, \quad \frac{\partial \rho_i}{\partial b} = c$$

### Step 4 — Define Line-of-Sight Unit Vector
Let $\mathbf{u}_i = (u_{xi}, u_{yi}, u_{zi}) = \dfrac{-(x_i - x_0, y_i - y_0, z_i - z_0)}{r_{i,0}}$. This is the unit vector from the receiver to satellite $i$.

### Step 5 — Form the Linear System
Define the **residual** $\Delta \rho_i = \rho_i^{\text{measured}} - \rho_{i,0}$. The linear system in matrix form:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \Delta \rho_1 \\ \Delta \rho_2 \\ \Delta \rho_3 \\ \Delta \rho_4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} u_{x1} & u_{y1} & u_{z1} & -c \\ u_{x2} & u_{y2} & u_{z2} & -c \\ u_{x3} & u_{y3} & u_{z3} & -c \\ u_{x4} & u_{y4} & u_{z4} & -c \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \Delta x \\ \Delta y \\ \Delta z \\ \Delta b \end{bmatrix}$$

Let $\Delta \boldsymbol{\rho} = H \cdot \Delta \mathbf{x}$.

### Step 6 — Solve by Least Squares
For $n \geq 4$ satellites, the best estimate is:

$$\Delta \mathbf{x} = (H^T H)^{-1} H^T \Delta \boldsymbol{\rho}$$

### Step 7 — Update and Iterate
Set $(x_0, y_0, z_0, b_0) \leftarrow (x_0 + \Delta x, y_0 + \Delta y, z_0 + \Delta z, b_0 + \Delta b)$ and repeat until $|\Delta \mathbf{x}|$ is below a threshold (typically **0.1 m**). This is the standard **iterative least-squares** GPS solver.

## 3.3 Derivation: Doppler Shift for Satellite Velocity

A GPS satellite moves at orbital velocity. The received signal frequency is Doppler-shifted. For a satellite at range rate $\dot{r}_i$:

$$f_{\text{received}} = f_{\text{transmitted}} \left(1 - \frac{\dot{r}_i}{c}\right)$$

The Doppler shift is:

$$f_d = f_{\text{transmitted}} - f_{\text{received}} = \frac{f_{\text{transmitted}} \cdot \dot{r}_i}{c} = \frac{\dot{r}_i}{\lambda}$$

where $\lambda = c/f_{\text{transmitted}}$ is the carrier wavelength.

For L1: $\lambda = 3 \times 10^8 / 1575.42 \times 10^6 \approx 0.1905$ m.

A typical maximum Doppler shift at the L1 frequency is **$\pm 5$ kHz** when the satellite is rising/setting.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: GPS Error Budget

| Error Source | Typical Magnitude (m) | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| Ionosphere delay | 1–5 | Dual-frequency correction (L1+L5) |
| Troposphere delay | 0.5–2.5 | Atmospheric model (Saastamoinen) |
| Ephemeris error | 0.5–2 | Updated by control segment |
| Satellite clock error | 0.5–2 | Corrected via navigation message |
| Multipath | 0.5–5 | Antenna design, choke-ring |
| Receiver noise | 0.1–1 | Averaging, Kalman filter |
| **Total UERE (User Equivalent Range Error)** | **~3–5 m rms** | – |

Multiplying UERE by DOP gives the position error:

$$\sigma_{\text{position}} = \text{DOP} \times \sigma_{\text{UERE}}$$

For DOP = 2 and UERE = 4 m → position error ≈ **8 m**, consistent with the 7.8 m civilian spec.
