# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define automotive telematics. List any four applications.
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
Automotive telematics is an embedded system that integrates **telecommunications and informatics** in a vehicle, enabling bidirectional wireless data exchange between the vehicle and a remote service center using a cellular modem, GPS receiver, and CAN bus interface.

**Four applications:**
1. **eCall** — Automatic emergency call on crash.
2. **Fleet management** — Real-time vehicle tracking.
3. **Remote diagnostics** — Engine health monitoring.
4. **Stolen vehicle tracking and immobilization.**

### Question 2: Why are at least four GPS satellites required for a 3D position fix?
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**
The pseudorange equation for satellite $i$ is:
$$\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot b$$
This equation has **4 unknowns** — three position coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and the receiver **clock bias** $b$. Each satellite provides **one independent equation**. Therefore, a minimum of **4 satellites** is required to form a system of 4 equations in 4 unknowns, solvable for both the 3D position and the clock offset. Three satellites can only give a 2D fix (assuming altitude is known) and leave the clock error unresolved.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): Navigation and GPS Working

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the Global Positioning System (GPS). Discuss the three segments of GPS.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the pseudorange equation for a GPS receiver. Explain why the term "pseudo" is used and why a minimum of four satellites is required for a 3D position fix.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Three Segments of GPS:**

The GPS consists of three segments:

1. **Space Segment:** A constellation of at least 24 satellites in **6 orbital planes** at an altitude of about **20,200 km** (Medium Earth Orbit). Each satellite circles the Earth twice per sidereal day and carries:
   - Atomic clocks (rubidium/cesium)
   - L-band transmitters (L1 = 1575.42 MHz, L2 = 1227.60 MHz, L5 = 1176.45 MHz)
   - Solar panels and thrusters for station-keeping

2. **Control Segment:** Operated by the U.S. Department of Defense. Includes:
   - **Master Control Station (MCS)** at Schriever AFB, Colorado
   - **Monitor stations** worldwide that track satellites
   - **Ground antennas** that upload corrected ephemeris and clock data to satellites

3. **User Segment:** The GPS receiver in the vehicle, which:
   - Receives signals from **4 or more satellites**
   - Measures pseudoranges
   - Computes position, velocity, and time (PVT)

**Working Principle:** Each satellite broadcasts a unique PRN code modulated on the L1 carrier. The receiver correlates a local replica of the code with the incoming signal. The shift required for maximum correlation gives the **propagation time** $\Delta t$. Multiplying by $c$ gives the pseudorange. With 4+ pseudoranges, the receiver solves the trilateration equations to obtain $(x, y, z, b)$.

**(b) Derivation of Pseudorange Equation:**

Let satellite $S_i$ have ECEF coordinates $(x_i, y_i, z_i)$ and the receiver have $(x, y, z)$. The signal leaves the satellite at GPS time $t^s$ and arrives at the receiver at $t^r$. The true range is:

$$r_i = c \cdot (t^r_{\text{true}} - t^s_{\text{true}})$$

However, the receiver's clock is not synchronized. Let $b$ be the receiver's clock bias. Then:

$$t^r_{\text{true}} = t^r_{\text{measured}} + b$$
$$t^s_{\text{true}} = t^s_{\text{measured}} + \delta_i \quad (\delta_i \approx 0, \text{ corrected via nav message})$$

So the **measured pseudorange** is:

$$\rho_i = c \cdot (t^r_{\text{measured}} - t^s_{\text{measured}}) = c \cdot (t^r_{\text{true}} - t^s_{\text{true}}) - c \cdot b + c \cdot \delta_i$$
$$\rho_i = r_i - c \cdot b + c \cdot \delta_i \approx r_i + c \cdot b'$$

where $b' = \delta_i - b$ is the lumped bias. The geometric range is:

$$r_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2}$$

Therefore the **pseudorange equation** is:

$$\boxed{\rho_i = \sqrt{(x_i - x)^2 + (y_i - y)^2 + (z_i - z)^2} + c \cdot b}$$

**Why "Pseudo":** Because of the unknown clock bias $b$, the measurement $\rho_i$ is **not** the true geometric range — it is "falsely" inflated by the term $c \cdot b$. Hence the prefix *pseudo*.

**Why Four Satellites:** The equation has 4 unknowns — $x, y, z$ (3D position) and $b$ (clock bias). Each satellite yields one equation. Therefore at least **4 linearly independent equations** are needed, requiring **4 satellites**.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Telematics and eCall

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 14 Marks]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the block diagram of an automotive Telematics Control Unit (TCU) and explain the function of each block.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the working of the **eCall** system in detail. Discuss the MSD (Minimum Set of Data) transmitted and the role of the EU mandate.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram of TCU:**

A Telematics Control Unit consists of the following functional blocks:

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| **MCU / SoC** (e.g., ARM Cortex-A53) | Runs telematics application software, handles AT commands, manages CAN stack |
| **GNSS Module** | Receives GPS/GLONASS/Galileo signals; outputs NMEA-0183 protocol data |
| **Cellular Modem** (4G LTE/5G) | Establishes packet-switched and circuit-switched data links with the cloud; supports VoLTE for voice |
| **CAN Transceiver** (e.g., TJA1051) | Interfaces MCU to vehicle CAN bus; reads OBD-II data (RPM, speed, DTCs) |
| **3-axis Accelerometer** | Crash detection (threshold typically **> 4g**) and rollover sensing |
| **Backup Battery** (Li-ion, ~10 min holdup) | Powers modem after main battery is severed in severe crash |
| **Audio Codec + Mic + Speaker** | Hands-free voice for emergency call |
| **SIM / eUICC** | Stores cellular identity; eUICC allows remote SIM provisioning |
| **RF Antenna** | Combined GNSS + cellular antenna (often shark-fin on roof) |

**Working:** The MCU continuously polls the CAN bus for vehicle state and the accelerometer for crash signatures. On crash detection, the backup battery powers the modem, which dials the emergency number and transmits a **voice call** plus a **data packet (MSD)** containing GPS coordinates to a PSAP.

**(b) eCall System Working:**

**eCall (emergency Call)** is an automatic emergency-call system mandated in the **European Union since 31 March 2018** for all new M1 and N1 category vehicles.

**Trigger Conditions:**
- Crash detected by accelerometer ($> 4g$)
- Airbag deployment signal from airbag ECU via CAN
- Rollover detected by gyroscope
- Manual trigger by pressing the eCall button

**MSD (Minimum Set of Data):**
The MSD is an SMS-like data packet (~140 bytes) containing:
- **GPS Position** (latitude, longitude)
- **Vehicle VIN** (17-character identifier)
- **Timestamp** of the event
- **Direction of travel** (e.g., N, NE)
- **Service type** (eCall vs test call)
- **Activation type** (automatic or manual)
- **Recent vehicle data** (speed, seatbelt status)

**Sequence:**
1. Crash detected → TCU powered by backup battery.
2. TCU dials **112** (the EU emergency number) over circuit-switched voice.
3. MSD sent via in-band modem (3GPP TS 26.267).
4. PSAP operator answers, sees location and VIN on screen.
5. Voice channel opens; occupant (if conscious) talks to operator.
6. Ambulance, fire, or police dispatched to the precise GPS coordinates.

**EU Mandate Impact:**
- Mandatory for all new type-approved cars in the EU from 31 March 2018.
- Estimated to **save ~2,500 lives per year in Europe** by reducing emergency response time, especially in rural areas.
- Similar systems: **ERA-GLONASS** (Russia), **E911** (USA — telematics-based NG911).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing *"telematics = GPS"* — this loses 1–2 marks. Telematics is **GPS + cellular communication + cloud**, not just GPS.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Saying *"3 satellites are enough for GPS"* — this is incorrect for 3D fix. The examiner expects the **clock bias argument**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the **L1 frequency** with the carrier used in GSM/LTE. **L1 = 1575.42 MHz** is GPS-only.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing pseudorange as just $r$ instead of $r + cb$. This loses the "pseudo" justification marks.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Missing the **backup battery** in the TCU diagram — frequently asked and easily forgotten.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Navigation = Localization + Map + Route Guidance** (local, in-vehicle).
- **Telematics = Wireless data exchange** (vehicle ↔ cloud, requires cellular modem).
- **GPS has 3 segments:** Space, Control, User.
- **GPS uses 24+ satellites** in MEO at **20,200 km**, period **11 h 58 min**.
- **L1 = 1575.42 MHz**, L2 = 1227.60 MHz, L5 = 1176.45 MHz.
- **Pseudorange equation:** $\rho_i = r_i + c \cdot b$ — note the **clock bias $b$** is the reason for "pseudo".
- **Minimum 4 satellites** are required for 3D position + clock fix.
- **DOP** (HDOP, PDOP, GDOP) measures geometry quality; **< 2 is excellent**.
- **DOP × UERE = position error** (UERE ≈ 3–5 m rms).
- **Dead reckoning** uses wheel speed + gyro; drifts over time, corrected by Kalman filter when GPS returns.
- **Map matching** aligns GPS track with the digital road network (HMM-based).
- **TCU blocks:** MCU, GNSS, Cellular modem, CAN, Accelerometer, Backup battery, Audio, SIM.
- **eCall** is **EU-mandated**, dials **112**, transmits **MSD** (GPS + VIN + timestamp).
- **eCall backup battery** holds the system alive for ~10 minutes after main power loss.
- **Civilian GPS accuracy:** ~**7.8 m horizontal (95%)**; military (PPS) is more accurate.
- **Doppler shift at L1:** up to **±5 kHz** — used by receiver to narrow search space.
- **Dijkstra / A-star** are the standard shortest-path routing algorithms for navigation.
- **Telematics data rate** of typical OBD-II upload: 10–100 kbps; **CAN classic: 500 kbps**.
- **Common telematics apps:** eCall, fleet mgmt, UBI insurance, SVT, OTA updates, remote diagnostics.
- **Inertial sensors** (IMU) help in tunnels, urban canyons, and parking garages where GPS is blocked.
- **V2X (DSRC / C-V2X)** is the future extension of telematics to peer-to-peer vehicle communication.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle to Vehicle Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT725 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS IN AUTOMOBILES
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Vehicle to Vehicle Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communication** is a mesh networking protocol defined under the broader **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS)** framework, where automotive embedded Electronic Control Units (ECUs) autonomously exchange kinematic, geospatial, and telematic data over short-range, low-latency wireless links to enable cooperative awareness, collision avoidance, and coordinated maneuvering without dependence on cellular or satellite infrastructure.

In the context of KTU PEMUT725 (Embedded Systems in Automobiles) Module 4, V2V forms a critical sub-domain of the **Connected Vehicle Ecosystem (CVE)** and is formally classified under the umbrella term **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything)** as defined by the **3GPP Release 14 / 16** and the **SAE J3061** cybersecurity framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 4.4):** Students must be able to distinguish between the three reference architectures of V2V — *Distributed (Ad-hoc)*, *Centralized (Cellular)*, and *Hybrid* — and articulate the rationale for IEEE 802.11p versus C-V2X (PC5 sidelink) selection in modern OEM deployments.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Convoy of Silent Spotters"

Imagine you are driving through a foggy mountain pass at night. You cannot see beyond 30 meters, but you are part of a 50-car convoy. If every driver suddenly becomes a **silent spotter** who whispers into a walkie-talkie every 100 milliseconds: *"My name is Car-7, I am 200 meters ahead, moving at 80 km/h, braking in 1.2 seconds, my tire pressure is 32 PSI"* — then everyone in the convoy builds a **real-time mental map** of cars they cannot physically see. This is exactly what V2V does: it is the *digital nervous system* of the road, turning each car into both a **broadcaster** and a **receiver** of safety-critical telemetry.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Fog" in the Analogy is Literal:** In dense traffic, occluded intersections, or adverse weather, line-of-sight (LoS) sensors like radar and LiDAR physically fail. V2V transcends this physical limitation by relaying state information through **Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS)** electromagnetic propagation, giving drivers a superhuman 360° awareness horizon of up to **300 meters**.

### 1.3 Standardized Communication Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis)

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source Standard |
|---|---|---|
| Operational Frequency Band | **5.85 – 5.925 GHz** (5.9 GHz band) | FCC / ETSI EN 302 571 |
| Channel Bandwidth | **10 MHz** (also 20 MHz) | IEEE 802.11p |
| Maximum EIRP | **33 dBm** (≈ 2 Watts) | ETSI / FCC |
| Beacon Transmission Rate | **10 Hz** (every 100 ms) | SAE J2945/1 |
| Communication Range | **300 m** typical, up to 1 km | IEEE 1609 |
| Maximum Latency | **100 ms** (safety-critical) | SAE J3061 |
| Data Rate | **3 – 27 Mbps** | IEEE 802.11p |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5.9 GHz Band is the "Goldilocks Frequency"** of V2V — it is high enough to support wide bandwidth (and thus anti-multipath modulation schemes like OFDM) yet low enough to bend around obstacles and survive rain fade, making it superior to millimeter-wave (77 GHz radar) and far more responsive than sub-1 GHz IoT bands.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture — The Five-Layer V2V Stack

V2V communication is not a single protocol but a **layered protocol stack** standardized by the **IEEE 1609 WAVE (Wireless Access in Vehicular Environments)** family. The KTU examiner expects students to articulate the function of each layer:

**Layer 1 — Physical Layer (PHY): IEEE 802.11p**
- Modified Wi-Fi PHY operating in the 5.9 GHz band.
- Uses **Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM)** with 52 subcarriers (48 data + 4 pilot).
- Supports BPSK, QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM.
- Symbol Time = **8 µs** (4× the legacy 802.11a for better resilience to inter-symbol interference caused by high Doppler shifts in vehicular mobility).

**Layer 2 — MAC Layer: IEEE 1609.4**
- Manages multi-channel operation: **Control Channel (CCH: 178)** for safety messages and **Service Channels (SCH: 174, 176, 180, 182)** for non-safety data.
- Implements the **Guard Interval (GI)** of **4 ms** between channel switches to prevent cross-channel interference.

**Layer 3 — Network & Transport: IEEE 1609.3 (WAVE Short Message Protocol – WSMP) / IPv6 / UDP**
- **WSMP** is the KTU-high-yield protocol — it bypasses the full TCP/IP handshake to deliver sub-100 ms latency.
- **Basic Safety Messages (BSM)** are the primary payload, standardized by **SAE J2735**.

**Layer 4 — Application: SAE J2945/1**
- Defines the **use cases** (Forward Collision Warning, Blind Spot Warning, Emergency Electronic Brake Lights, Do-Not-Pass Warning).

**Layer 5 — Security: IEEE 1609.2**
- Provides **digital signatures** (ECDSA over the NIST P-256 curve) for every BSM to prevent spoofing attacks.

### 2.2 The Basic Safety Message (BSM) — Anatomy of a V2V Packet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BSM Structure (SAE J2735 Part 1):** Every V2V-equipped vehicle broadcasts a BSM **10 times per second** containing two parts:
> 1. **Part 1 (Mandatory, ~40 bytes):** Vehicle position (latitude/longitude to 1/10th microdegree), speed, heading, acceleration, brake system status, vehicle size, steering wheel angle.
> 2. **Part 2 (Optional, variable):** Event flags (hard braking, airbag deployment), path history, path prediction.

The BSM is the *heartbeat* of V2V — without it, the cooperative awareness matrix collapses.

### 2.3 The Three V2X Sub-Domains

V2V does not operate in isolation. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to map V2V within the broader V2X taxonomy:

1. **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Car ↔ Car (the topic at hand).
2. **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Car ↔ Roadside Unit (RSU), e.g., traffic signal preemption.
3. **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian):** Car ↔ Pedestrian smartphone, using the same 5.9 GHz band.
4. **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network):** Car ↔ Cellular tower (4G/5G) for HD map updates and telematics.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In the table below, absolute value and "such that" are written using `\vert` and `\mid` instead of the pipe symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX / Symbolic Form | Engineering Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-Space Path Loss (dB) | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ | Range prediction for BSM reception; $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz. |
| 2 | Received Signal Strength | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \text{FSPL} - L_{misc}$ | Link budget calculation for V2V antenna. |
| 3 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{P_r}{N_0 B}\right)$ | Maximum achievable data rate per 10 MHz channel. |
| 4 | SNR Threshold (QPSK, BER $\le 10^{-5}$) | $\text{SNR}_{min} = 9.6 \text{ dB}$ | Minimum carrier-to-noise ratio for BSM decoding. |
| 5 | Maximum Doppler Shift | $f_d = \dfrac{v \cdot f_c}{c}$ | Symbol time design; $v$ = relative velocity, $c = 3\times 10^8$ m/s. |
| 6 | Coherence Time | $T_c \approx \dfrac{0.423}{f_d}$ | Determines OFDM symbol duration. |
| 7 | Round-Trip Latency Budget | $T_{RT} = 2T_{prop} + 4T_{proc} + T_{GI}$ | Safety message end-to-end delay. |
| 8 | Beacon Rate / Channel Load | $\lambda = \dfrac{N \cdot R_b}{B}$ | Congestion control; $N$ = neighbor count, $R_b$ = beacon rate. |
| 9 | Packet Error Rate (PER) | $\text{PER} = 1 - (1 - \text{BER})^n$, $n$ = bits/packet | Reliability metric; KTU target $\text{PER} \le 10\%$. |
| 10 | Range Resolution | $\Delta R = \dfrac{c}{2B}$ | Distance discrimination in V2V ranging. |

### 2.5 Why V2V is Engineering-Critical (Real-World Utility)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> According to the **U.S. NHTSA (National Highway Traffic Safety Administration) Report DOT HS 812 014**, V2V technology has the potential to address **81% of all unimpaired light-vehicle crash scenarios**, saving an estimated **1,083 lives annually** in the U.S. alone. This is why V2V is a **mandatory technology** under the **European GSR 2 (General Safety Regulation 2)** effective July 2024.

The engineering utility spans:
- **ADAS Enhancement:** V2V augments on-board radar/camera by providing *intention data* (e.g., the car ahead is *about* to brake, not just *currently* braking).
- **Platooning:** Truck convoys maintain 3-meter gaps at 90 km/h, reducing fuel consumption by **10–15%**.
- **Intersection Safety:** The **Intersection Movement Assist (IMA)** warning prevents 700,000+ side-impact crashes per year.
- **Emergency Vehicle Preemption:** V2V allows ambulances to clear intersections 6 seconds faster.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Reliable V2V Communication Range

**Problem Statement:** A V2V transmitter broadcasts with EIRP $= 33$ dBm at $f_c = 5.9$ GHz. The receiver sensitivity is $-92$ dBm (typical for IEEE 802.11p at 6 Mbps QPSK). Assuming free-space propagation, derive the maximum line-of-sight (LoS) communication range.

**Given Data:**
- $P_t + G_t = 33$ dBm (combined EIRP).
- $P_{r,min} = -92$ dBm (QPSK, 6 Mbps sensitivity per IEEE 802.11p).
- $f_c = 5.9$ GHz $= 5900$ MHz.
- $L_{misc} \approx 2$ dB (connector + cable loss).

**Step 1 — Compute the Maximum Allowable Path Loss.**

The link budget equation is:

$$
P_r = P_t + G_t - \text{FSPL} - L_{misc}
$$

For the receiver to just decode the signal, $P_r = P_{r,min}$. Rearranging:

$$
\text{FSPL}_{max} = (P_t + G_t) - P_{r,min} - L_{misc}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\text{FSPL}_{max} = 33 \text{ dBm} - (-92 \text{ dBm}) - 2 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\text{FSPL}_{max} = 33 + 92 - 2 = 123 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Free-Space Path Loss Formula.**

The free-space path loss in decibels is:

$$
\text{FSPL}(d) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45
$$

where $d$ is in kilometres and $f$ is in MHz. Substituting $f = 5900$ MHz:

$$
20\log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.771 = 75.42 \text{ dB}
$$

$$
\text{FSPL}(d) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 75.42 + 32.45 = 20\log_{10}(d) + 107.87
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and Solve for $d$.**

$$
20\log_{10}(d) + 107.87 = 123
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 123 - 107.87 = 15.13
$$

$$
\log_{10}(d) = \dfrac{15.13}{20} = 0.7565
$$

$$
d = 10^{0.7565} = 5.71 \text{ km}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The theoretical free-space V2V range is **≈ 5.7 km**. In real urban environments with multipath fading, building shadowing, and NLoS conditions, the practical range collapses to **300–500 m** — this is precisely why V2V is classified as a *short-range* (DSRC) technology and not a long-range cellular system.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: BSM Latency Budget for Forward Collision Warning

**Problem Statement:** A V2V-equipped car at 90 km/h on a highway must receive a BSM from the lead car 100 m ahead, decode it, run a collision-prediction algorithm, and alert the driver. Compute the total end-to-end latency and verify it satisfies the **100 ms safety budget**.

**Given Data:**
- Vehicle speed: $v = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s.
- Inter-vehicle spacing: $d = 100$ m.
- Modulation: QPSK at 6 Mbps (conservative).
- BSM payload size: $L = 200$ bytes $= 1600$ bits.
- Algorithm processing delay: $T_{proc} = 20$ ms (ECU + sensor fusion).

**Step 1 — Propagation Delay.**

$$
T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{c} = \dfrac{100}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.33 \text{ µs}
$$

This is negligible compared to processing delays.

**Step 2 — Transmission (Air) Time.**

$$
T_{tx} = \dfrac{L}{R_b} = \dfrac{1600 \text{ bits}}{6 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 266.67 \text{ µs}
$$

**Step 3 — Queueing + Channel Access Delay (WAVE EDCA, contention-based).**

In a moderately loaded channel (50 neighbors), the average backoff delay is empirically modeled as:

$$
T_{queue} \approx 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Decoding + Verification Delay (including ECDSA signature check).**

ECDSA verification on an automotive HSM takes approximately:

$$
T_{dec} = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Guard Interval (Channel Switch).**

$$
T_{GI} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Total End-to-End Latency.**

$$
T_{total} = T_{prop} + T_{tx} + T_{queue} + T_{dec} + T_{GI} + T_{proc}
$$

$$
T_{total} \approx 0.00033 + 0.267 + 15 + 8 + 4 + 20
$$

$$
T_{total} \approx 47.27 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** $T_{total} = 47.27$ ms $\ll$ 100 ms safety budget. The system has **52.73 ms of headroom** for additional sensor fusion or driver-warning hysteresis. This is the engineering reason V2V was designed around sub-100 ms latency — to give the human driver or autonomous controller enough time to react before the **Time-To-Collision (TTC)** threshold is breached.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) vs C-V2X (PC5 Sidelink)

| Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (3GPP PC5) | KTU Examiner's Take |
|---|---|---|---|
| Origin | IEEE (2004, updated 2020) | 3GPP Release 14 (2017) | DSRC = legacy Western; C-V2X = modern global. |
| Spectrum | 5.9 GHz dedicated (75 MHz) | 5.9 GHz + ITS bands (shared) | C-V2X offers spectrum flexibility. |
| Latency | **< 50 ms typical** | **< 20 ms** (5G NR-V2X) | C-V2X wins for autonomous driving. |
| Range | ~300 m LoS | ~500 m with relay | C-V2X has 1.6× range advantage. |
| Mobility Support | Up to 200 km/h | Up to 500 km/h | C-V2X superior for high-speed rail. |
| Security | IEEE 1609.2 (ECDSA) | 3GPP AKMA + ECDSA | Both NIST-compliant. |
| Deployment Status | Mandated (US, EU legacy) | Dominant in new OEM platforms (2024+) | KTU students should know *both*. |
| Network Type | Ad-hoc mesh (no infra) | Cellular-assisted or sidelink-only | C-V2X is hybrid by design. |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Channel Congestion in Dense Traffic

**Problem:** A 6-lane highway has 250 vehicles in a 500 m stretch, each transmitting BSMs at 10 Hz. The control channel bandwidth is $B = 10$ MHz. Compute the **Channel Busy Ratio (CBR)** and determine if congestion control is required.

**Solution:**

Each vehicle transmits 200-byte BSMs at 10 Hz. The aggregate offered load is:

$$
\lambda_{total} = N \cdot R_b \cdot L_{BSM} = 250 \times 10 \times 200 \times 8 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
\lambda_{total} = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s} = 4 \text{ Mbps}
$$

Channel capacity at QPSK with SNR margin is approximately 6 Mbps. Therefore:

$$
\text{CBR} = \dfrac{\lambda_{total}}{C} = \dfrac{4}{6} = 0.667 = 66.7\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Result:** A CBR of 66.7% exceeds the KTU/SAE safety threshold of **50%**, triggering **Decentralized Congestion Control (DCC)** algorithms defined in ETSI TS 102 687. The vehicles must dynamically reduce their transmit power (to 20 dBm) and beacon rate (to 5 Hz) to restore channel equilibrium — a real-world V2V stability problem.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 V2V System-Level Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[GNSS Receiver GPS 1Hz] --> G[On-Board Unit OBU Processor]
    B[Inertial Measurement Unit IMU 100Hz] --> G
    C[Wheel Speed Sensors ABS ECU] --> G
    D[Steering Angle Sensor EPS ECU] --> G
    E[Brake Pressure Sensor ESP ECU] --> G
    G --> H[BSM Generator SAE J2735]
    H --> I[Security Module ECDSA Sign]
    I --> J[IEEE 802.11p Radio Modem 5.9 GHz]
    J --> K[Antenna Diversity 2x2 MIMO]
    K --> L[RF Channel 5.9 GHz Air Interface]
    L --> M[Neighboring Vehicle OBU]
    M --> N[BSM Decoder]
    N --> O[ECDSA Verify]
    O --> P[Hazard Analysis Engine]
    P --> Q[Driver HMI Warning]
    P --> R[Actuator ADAS Auto-Brake]

    subgraph Sensors_Source
        A
        B
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph V2V_Stack
        G
        H
        I
        J
        K
    end

    subgraph Receiver_Chain
        L
        M
        N
        O
        P
    end
```

### 4.2 V2V Protocol Stack — Layered Reference Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L5[Application Layer - SAE J2945 - FCW, BSW, EEBL] --> L4
    L4[Message Layer - SAE J2735 - BSM Encoding] --> L3
    L3[Network and Transport - IEEE 1609.3 - WSMP or IPv6 UDP] --> L2
    L2[MAC Layer - IEEE 1609.4 - Multi-Channel EDCA] --> L1
    L1[Physical Layer - IEEE 802.11p - OFDM 5.9 GHz 10 MHz] --> RF[RF Air Interface]
    SEC[Security Layer - IEEE 1609.2 - ECDSA Digital Signature] -.-> L3
    SEC -.-> L2

    L5 --- Note1[Time-critical safety messages 10 Hz beacon]
    L1 --- Note2[52 OFDM subcarriers 8 us symbol time 16.66 us slot]
```

### 4.3 BSM Transmission and Reception Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V1 as Vehicle A OBU
    participant Air as 5.9 GHz Channel
    participant V2 as Vehicle B OBU
    participant V3 as Vehicle C OBU

    Note over V1: 100 ms Beacon Cycle
    V1->>V1: Collect GPS, IMU, CAN bus data
    V1->>V1: Build BSM Part 1 + Part 2
    V1->>V1: ECDSA Sign with private key
    V1->>Air: Transmit on CCH 178 at 33 dBm
    Air-->>V2: BSM received at -75 dBm
    Air-->>V3: BSM received at -82 dBm
    V2->>V2: ECDSA Verify signature
    V2->>V2: Run Forward Collision Algorithm
    V2->>V2: HMI Alert if TTC < 3 sec
    V3->>V3: ECDSA Verify
    V3->>V3: Update local dynamic map
    Note over V1,V3: Entire exchange completes in under 50 ms
```

### 4.4 V2X Taxonomy — V2V within the Broader Ecosystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V2X[Vehicle to Everything V2X] --> V2V1[Vehicle to Vehicle V2V]
    V2X --> V2I[Vehicle to Infrastructure V2I]
    V2X --> V2P[Vehicle to Pedestrian V2P]
    V2X --> V2N[Vehicle to Network V2N]
    V2X --> V2G[Vehicle to Grid V2G]

    V2V1 --> APP1[Safety: FCW, BSW, EEBL]
    V2V1 --> APP2[Efficiency: Platooning, Cooperative ACC]
    V2I --> APP3[Traffic Signal Priority, Curve Warning]
    V2P --> APP4[Vulnerable Road User Protection]
    V2N --> APP5[HD Map, OTA Updates, Telematics]
    V2G --> APP6[Smart Charging, V2H Bidirectional]

    V2V1 -.Uses.-> DSRC1[IEEE 802.11p 5.9 GHz]
    V2V1 -.Or.-> CV2X[C-V2X PC5 Sidelink 5.9 GHz]
    V2N -.Uses.-> CELL[4G LTE or 5G NR]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define *Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communication*. List the two SAE standards that govern the BSM message format and the safety application layer, respectively.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
V2V Communication is a wireless mesh networking paradigm in which vehicles exchange real-time kinematic data (position, speed, heading, acceleration) over short-range radio links to achieve cooperative awareness and collision avoidance. **(1 mark)**
- **SAE J2735** governs the **BSM message format** (encoding, structure of Part 1 and Part 2 fields). **(1 mark)**
- **SAE J2945/1** governs the **safety application layer** (use cases like FCW, BSW, EEBL, performance requirements). **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Why is the **5.9 GHz band** chosen for V2V communication, and what is the typical beacon transmission rate mandated by SAE J2945/1?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The 5.9 GHz band is chosen because it offers an optimal trade-off: sufficient bandwidth for OFDM-based modulation (10–20 MHz channels), reasonable propagation characteristics for non-line-of-sight (NLoS) urban environments, low rain-fade compared to millimeter waves, and dedicated spectrum allocation by regulators (FCC, ETSI) to avoid interference with Wi-Fi and cellular. **(2 marks)**
The mandated beacon transmission rate is **10 Hz (one BSM every 100 ms)**. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Module 4 Choice]**
> **Question A (14 Marks)**
> **Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a V2V communication system**, clearly identifying the roles of the GNSS receiver, IMU, CAN bus, BSM generator, ECDSA security module, and the IEEE 802.11p radio modem. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.A (b)** Derive the **maximum reliable line-of-sight V2V communication range** given: EIRP = 33 dBm, carrier frequency = 5.9 GHz, receiver sensitivity = -92 dBm (QPSK, 6 Mbps), and miscellaneous losses = 2 dB. State two real-world factors that reduce the practical range below the theoretical value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram (4 marks) + Explanation (3 marks):**

**Architecture Components:**

1. **GNSS Receiver:** Provides absolute position (latitude, longitude, altitude) and UTC time synchronisation. Accuracy must be < 1.5 m to satisfy BSM positional resolution. **(1 mark)**
2. **IMU (Inertial Measurement Unit):** 6-axis MEMS sensor providing 100 Hz acceleration and yaw-rate data to interpolate between GPS updates. **(1 mark)**
3. **CAN Bus Interface:** Collects vehicle dynamic state (wheel speed, brake pressure, steering angle, throttle position) from the ABS, EPS, and engine ECUs. **(1 mark)**
4. **BSM Generator (SAE J2735):** Aggregates sensor data into the standardized BSM Part 1 and Part 2 structures. **(0.5 marks)**
5. **ECDSA Security Module:** Signs the BSM with the vehicle's private key (NIST P-256) using the IEEE 1609.2 certificate format. **(0.5 marks)**
6. **IEEE 802.11p Radio Modem:** OFDM-based transceiver operating in the 5.9 GHz band, with a 10 MHz channel bandwidth and 8 µs OFDM symbol time. **(1 mark)**
7. **Antenna System:** Roof-mounted shark-fin antenna with 2×2 MIMO diversity for combating multipath fading. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Range Derivation (5 marks) + Real-world factors (2 marks):**

The detailed derivation follows the **nine-step procedure** of Section 3.1 of these notes. The key intermediate results are:

**Step 1 — Maximum Allowable Path Loss:**
$$
\text{FSPL}_{max} = 33 - (-92) - 2 = 123 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency Term:**
$$
20\log_{10}(5900) = 75.42 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for distance:**
$$
20\log_{10}(d) + 75.42 + 32.45 = 123
$$

$$
20\log_{10}(d) = 15.13 \implies d = 5.71 \text{ km}
$$

**Theoretical LoS range = 5.71 km. (3 marks for the full derivation)**

**Real-world factors that reduce the practical range:** **(2 marks)**
1. **Multipath fading** from building reflections, causing signal nulls and intersymbol interference. *0.5 marks*
2. **Building and foliage shadowing (NLoS obstruction)** reducing the effective Fresnel zone clearance. *0.5 marks*
3. **Receiver noise figure and interference** from non-V2V users in adjacent bands. *0.5 marks*
4. **Antenna pattern degradation** due to vehicle body obstruction (the roof antenna is shadowed by the car's own roof curvature at high elevation angles). *0.5 marks*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 / Module 4 Choice — Alternative]**
> **Question B (14 Marks)**
> **Q.B (a)** Compare **DSRC (IEEE 802.11p)** and **C-V2X (3GPP PC5 sidelink)** technologies across at least six parameters. Justify which technology is preferred for high-speed platooning applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **Q.B (b)** Explain the **security framework of V2V communication** as defined by IEEE 1609.2, covering the role of ECDSA digital signatures, certificate authority hierarchy, and the BSM signing-verification workflow. Why is message authentication critical in V2V, and what are the two main classes of attacks it prevents? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) DSRC vs C-V2X Comparison (5 marks) + Justification (2 marks):**

The complete six-parameter comparison is given in **Section 3.3** of these notes. Key highlights: **(1 mark per major parameter, max 5 marks)**

| Parameter | DSRC | C-V2X |
|---|---|---|
| Latency | 50 ms | 20 ms |
| Range (LoS) | 300 m | 500 m |
| High-speed support | 200 km/h | 500 km/h |
| Spectrum | 5.9 GHz dedicated | Shared 5.9 + ITS |
| Maturity | 20+ years (legacy) | 2017+ (modern OEMs) |
| Network type | Ad-hoc mesh | Sidelink / cellular hybrid |

**Justification for Platooning (2 marks):**
For high-speed truck platooning (90 km/h, 3 m gaps), the **0.5-second reaction time** required for emergency braking propagation across a 10-truck platoon demands ultra-low latency and high reliability. **C-V2X is preferred** because: (i) its 20 ms latency is half of DSRC's 50 ms, providing 30 ms additional safety margin; (ii) its sidelink mode operates without cellular coverage, preserving mesh resilience; (iii) its 5G NR-V2X upgrade path supports **synchronization signal block (SSB)**-based time synchronization essential for sub-3 m spacing; and (iv) modern OEM platforms (post-2022) universally ship C-V2X chipsets (Qualcomm 9150, Autotalks CRATON2). **(2 marks)**

**(b) V2V Security Framework (7 marks):**

1. **ECDSA Digital Signature (2 marks):** Each BSM is signed using the **Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm** on the NIST P-256 curve. The 64-byte signature is appended to the BSM, and the receiver uses the sender's public key (embedded in an IEEE 1609.2 certificate) to verify authenticity. The cryptographic operations are executed in a tamper-resistant Hardware Security Module (HSM) to prevent key extraction.

2. **Certificate Authority (CA) Hierarchy (2 marks):** V2V uses a **Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)** with a multi-tier CA structure: **Root CA → Intermediate CA → Enrollment CA → Pseudonym CA**. Vehicles receive a long-term *enrollment certificate* and rotate **20+ short-term pseudonym certificates** every 5 minutes to prevent tracking while preserving authentication. The **Security Credential Management System (SCMS)** developed by the U.S. DOT manages this hierarchy.

3. **BSM Signing-Verification Workflow (1.5 marks):**
   - **Sign (Tx):** OBU → build BSM → HSM computes hash → HSM signs with current pseudonym private key → append signature to BSM → transmit.
   - **Verify (Rx):** OBU → receive BSM → extract sender's pseudonym certificate → verify certificate chain to root CA → verify signature against BSM hash → discard if invalid.

4. **Why Authentication is Critical (0.5 marks):** V2V messages trigger life-critical actions (autonomous braking). A spoofed BSM claiming "I'm 5 m ahead, moving at 200 km/h" could cause phantom emergency braking → multi-car pileup.

5. **Two Attack Classes Prevented (1 mark):**
   - **Message Forgery (Sybil attack):** Attacker broadcasts fake BSMs from non-existent vehicles to manipulate traffic flow.
   - **Replay Attack:** Attacker captures a valid BSM and rebroadcasts it with stale position data, confusing collision prediction algorithms.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Mark-Loss Hotspots in V2V Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing standards (–2 marks):** Many students interchange SAE J2735 and SAE J2945. Remember: **J2735 = message format**, **J2945 = application behaviour & performance**. Examiners explicitly check this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting IEEE 1609.x (–1 mark):** Writing only "IEEE 802.11p" without mentioning IEEE 1609 (WAVE upper layers) is treated as an incomplete protocol-stack answer.
> 3. **Unit errors in path loss (–1 mark):** The FSPL formula $20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ requires $d$ in **kilometres** and $f$ in **MHz**. Mixing units (e.g., $d$ in metres) is the #1 calculation error.
> 4. **Omitting security layer (–1 mark):** A V2V block diagram without the ECDSA / IEEE 1609.2 module is considered incomplete in the 2024 scheme.
> 5. **Writing "5G for V2V" without qualification (–0.5 marks):** Specify *"C-V2X (3GPP PC5 sidelink)"* — 5G Uu (cellular uplink/downlink) is **V2N**, not V2V.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — V2V Communication**
>
> - V2V is a sub-domain of V2X; it operates in the **5.85–5.925 GHz** band with **10 MHz** channel bandwidth. *(Must-know)*
> - The three layered standards are: **IEEE 802.11p** (PHY/MAC), **IEEE 1609.x** (WAVE upper layers), and **SAE J2735 / J2945** (message & application).
> - Every V2V-equipped vehicle broadcasts a **BSM at 10 Hz** containing position, speed, heading, and acceleration data.
> - The **5.9 GHz band** is a *dedicated ITS spectrum* — not shared with Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz) or cellular.
> - **IEEE 802.11p** uses **OFDM with 8 µs symbol time** (4× legacy 802.11a) to survive vehicular Doppler.
> - **Two BSM Parts:** Part 1 (mandatory, kinematic core) and Part 2 (optional, event-based).
> - **Security is non-negotiable:** Every BSM carries an **ECDSA P-256 digital signature** verified via the **SCMS PKI** hierarchy.
> - **DSRC vs C-V2X:** DSRC is mature but legacy; C-V2X is the modern OEM choice with better latency (20 ms) and 5G NR evolution.
> - **Range:** Theoretical LoS = **5.7 km**; practical urban NLoS = **300–500 m**.
> - **Latency budget:** Safety-critical V2V must complete within **100 ms** (typical: 47 ms).
> - **Congestion control:** If CBR > 50%, DCC algorithms reduce transmit power and beacon rate.
> - **NHTSA estimates** V2V could address **81% of light-vehicle crash scenarios** and save **1,083 lives/year (US)**.
> - **Remember the KTU anchor:** V2V is a *cooperative perception* technology, not a *remote sensing* technology — it relays *intention*, not *measurement*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle to Everything Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT725 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS IN AUTOMOBILES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Vehicle to Everything Communication # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) Communication** is a sophisticated, real-time, short-range wireless communication paradigm that enables bidirectional data exchange between a vehicle and any entity (other vehicles, roadside infrastructure, pedestrians, networks, the power grid, or cloud servers) that may affect the vehicle's operation or be affected by it. The umbrella term **V2X** is formally decomposed by SAE J3061 and ETSI TS 102 636 into the following canonical sub-classifications: | Sub-class | Full Form | Counterpart Entity | |-----------|-----------|--------------------| | **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Other cars, trucks, two-wheelers | | **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | Roadside Units (RSU), traffic lights, signs | | **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Pedestrians (via smartphone/DSRC) | | **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network | Cellular base stations, cloud | | **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid | EV charging stations, smart grid | | **V2D** | Vehicle-to-Device | Wearables, OBD dongles | The two dominant underlying radio access technologies ratified worldwide are: - **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications)** — based on **IEEE 802.11p**, operating in the **5.85–5.925 GHz band** (US FCC allocation of **75 MHz**). - **C-V2X (Cellular V2X)** — 3GPP Release 14, 15, and 16 (5G NR-V2X), using LTE sidelink (PC5 interface) and uplink/downlink (Uu interface). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Hive-Mind Highway":** Imagine every vehicle on the road as a bee in a hive. Each bee broadcasts its speed, heading, and position 10 times every second. The bees don't need a central queen to listen in — they communicate peer-to-peer. When one bee senses danger (sudden braking), it instantly whispers to all bees within a **300 m radius**, who react before the danger even reaches them. **V2X is exactly this — a vehicular "hive-mind" that lets cars, traffic lights, and pedestrians anticipate accidents *before* the cameras or radars can detect them.** ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s - Reserved spectrum (US): **5.850–5.925 GHz = 75 MHz** - Reserved spectrum (EU): **5.855–5.875 GHz = 20 MHz** - Maximum tolerated end-to-end latency for safety messages: **≤ 100 ms** (typically **≤ 20 ms** for pre-crash sensing) - Typical BSM transmission rate: **10 Hz** - Maximum reliable communication range: **~300 m** (urban), **~1000 m** (highway LOS) --- # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why V2X is Architecturally Necessary Modern vehicles already possess on-board sensors (radar, lidar, camera, ultrasonic). However, these sensors suffer from three fundamental limitations: 1. **Line-of-Sight (LoS) Constraint** — radar/cameras cannot "see" around blind corners or through trucks. 2. **Non-Cooperative Targets** — pedestrians, cyclists, and other vehicles are not tracked cooperatively unless they also have sensors. 3. **Sensor Latency** — perception pipelines take **50–200 ms**; collision avoidance needs **< 20 ms**. V2X solves all three by enabling **non-LoS, cooperative, low-latency** situational awareness. ## 2.2 Layered Protocol Stack (ETSI ITS-G5 Architecture) The ETSI EN 302 636 standard defines a 7-layer architecture mapped as: 1. **Application Layer** — collision avoidance, cooperative ACC, emergency brake warning. 2. **Facilities Layer** — message generation (CAM, DENM, BSM). 3. **Networking & Transport Layer** — GeoNetworking (GN), IPv6, TCP/UDP. 4. **Access Layer** — IEEE 802.11p MAC/PHY. 5. **Physical Layer** — OFDM at 5.9 GHz. 6. **Security Layer** — PKI, digital signatures (IEEE 1609.2). 7. **Management Layer** — address allocation, service advertisement. ## 2.3 Core Message Types | Message | Standard | Size | Frequency | Purpose | |---------|----------|------|-----------|---------| | **BSM** (Basic Safety Message) | SAE J2735 | ~400 bytes | **10 Hz** | Position, velocity, acceleration, heading | | **CAM** (Cooperative Awareness Msg) | ETSI EN 302 637-2 | ~250 bytes | **1–10 Hz** | Status broadcasting | | **DENM** (Decentralized Env. Notification) | ETSI EN 302 637-3 | ~800 bytes | Event-triggered | Hazard warning (ice, accident) | | **SPaT** (Signal Phase & Timing) | SAE J2735 | Variable | 10 Hz | Traffic-signal timing | | **MAP** (Map Data) | SAE J2735 | Variable | 1 Hz | Intersection geometry | ## 2.4 DSRC vs C-V2X Comparison | Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (LTE/5G) | |-----------|---------------------|----------------| | Standard | IEEE 802.11p (Wi-Fi derived) | 3GPP Rel. 14/15/16 | | Modulation | OFDM (BPSK–64-QAM) | SC-FDM (LTE) / OFDM (5G NR) | | Channel BW | **10 MHz** (US), **20 MHz** (EU) | **10/20 MHz** | | Latency | ~4 ms (MAC access) | **~1 ms** sidelink | | Range | ~300 m typical | ~400–500 m | | Synchronization | Ad-hoc (no sync needed) | Requires GNSS sync | | Scalability (high density) | Poor (CSMA/CA contention) | Excellent (scheduled) | | Future evolution | Frozen (sunset in US 2024) | **Active roadmap (5G NR-V2X)** | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following equations are **examination-critical** for KTU PEMUT725. Memorize the symbolic form, units, and boundary conditions. | # | Formula | Symbol Key | Application | |---|---------|------------|-------------| | 1 | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ = km, $f$ = MHz | Free-space path loss | | 2 | $d_{\max} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{\text{lat}}}{2}$ | $c$ = 3×10⁸ m/s, $t_{\text{lat}}$ = s | Max range from latency | | 3 | $R_{\text{BSM}} = N_{\text{veh}} \cdot S_{\text{BSM}} \cdot f_{\text{tx}}$ | $N$ = vehicles, $S$ = bytes, $f$ = Hz | Aggregate BSM data rate | | 4 | $P_{\text{rx}} = P_{\text{tx}} + G_{\text{tx}} - L_{\text{path}} + G_{\text{rx}}$ | All in dB/dBi/dBm | Link budget | | 5 | $SNR = \dfrac{P_{\text{rx}}}{N_0 \cdot B}$ | $N_0$ = noise spectral density | Signal-to-noise ratio | | 6 | $N_0 = 10\log_{10}(k T_0) + NF$ | $k$ = Boltzmann const, $NF$ = noise figure | Thermal noise floor | | 7 | $t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d}{c}$ | One-way propagation delay | Radio wave travel time | | 8 | $\text{QoS class latency} \le 100\,\text{ms}$ (ITS-G5 Class A) | Standard mandate | Safety budget | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **ADAS Enhancement**: V2X augments Adaptive Cruise Control by previewing vehicles beyond sensor range. - **Cooperative Driving**: Platooning trucks achieve **< 1 m** inter-vehicle gap (fuel savings 10–15%). - **Smart City Integration**: V2I enables **Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory (GLOSA)**, reducing urban stops by 30%. - **Insurance Telematics**: V2N allows usage-based insurance discounts. - **Emergency Vehicle Priority**: V2I grants signal preemption for ambulances/fire engines. --- # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Communication Range from Latency Budget > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** A safety message must reach a target vehicle within $t_{\text{lat}} = 10\,\text{ms}$ for cooperative collision avoidance. Determine the maximum one-hop communication range. **Step 1 — Identify the physical constraint.** The radio wave must travel from transmitter to receiver in *at most* half of the latency budget (other half reserved for processing, MAC access, and retransmission). $$ t_{\text{prop, max}} = \dfrac{t_{\text{lat}}}{2} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the kinematic relationship for electromagnetic waves.** All radio waves propagate at the speed of light in free air: $$ t_{\text{prop}} = \dfrac{d_{\max}}{c} $$ **Step 3 — Equate and solve for $d_{\max}$.** $$ \dfrac{d_{\max}}{c} = \dfrac{t_{\text{lat}}}{2} $$ $$ d_{\max} = \dfrac{c \cdot t_{\text{lat}}}{2} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.** $$ d_{\max} = \dfrac{(3 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s})}{2} $$ $$ d_{\max} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{6}\,\text{m}}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{d_{\max} = 1.5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{m} = 1500\,\text{km}} $$ **Step 5 — Interpret the result with realistic constraints.** Pure propagation latency allows an enormous range. However, **practical V2X range is governed by link budget (FSPL) and the 23 dBm EIRP limit**, not propagation delay. This derivation proves that *latency alone is not the bottleneck* — **received power is.** --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Free-Space Path Loss at V2X Carrier Frequency **Step 1 — State the FSPL formula for $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz.** $$ FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \quad [\text{dB}] $$ **Step 2 — Input the V2X design values.** - Carrier frequency $f = 5.9 \times 10^{3}\,\text{MHz}$ - Range $d = 0.3\,\text{km}$ (300 m, urban V2X) $$ FSPL = 20\log_{10}(0.3) + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate each term.** $$ 20\log_{10}(0.3) = 20 \times (-0.5229) = -10.458\,\text{dB} $$ $$ 20\log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.7709 = 75.418\,\text{dB} $$ **Step 4 — Sum the components.** $$ FSPL = -10.458 + 75.418 + 32.44 = 97.40\,\text{dB} $$ $$ \boxed{FSPL \approx 97.4\,\text{dB} \text{ at } 300\,\text{m}} $$ **Step 5 — Engineering implication.** With $P_{\text{tx}} = 20\,\text{dBm}$ and $G_{\text{tx}} = G_{\text{rx}} = 3\,\text{dBi}$, received power is: $$ P_{\text{rx}} = 20 + 3 - 97.4 + 3 = -71.4\,\text{dBm} $$ For QPSK decoding at 6 Mbps, required $P_{\text{rx}} \approx -82\,\text{dBm}$ → **margin of 10.6 dB** → link closes successfully. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Aggregate Bandwidth Required for BSM Flooding **Step 1 — State the problem parameters.** - Vehicles in coverage area: $N_{\text{veh}} = 100$ - BSM size: $S_{\text{BSM}} = 400\,\text{bytes} = 3200\,\text{bits}$ - Transmission frequency: $f_{\text{tx}} = 10\,\text{Hz}$ **Step 2 — Per-vehicle BSM bit rate.** $$ R_{\text{per}} = S_{\text{BSM}} \cdot f_{\text{tx}} = 3200 \times 10 = 32{,}000\,\text{bps} $$ $$ \boxed{R_{\text{per}} = 32\,\text{kbps per vehicle}} $$ **Step 3 — Aggregate load assuming all 100 vehicles transmit simultaneously.** $$ R_{\text{agg}} = N_{\text{veh}} \cdot R_{\text{per}} = 100 \times 32{,}000 $$ $$ \boxed{R_{\text{agg}} = 3.2\,\text{Mbps}} $$ **Step 4 — Comparison with channel capacity.** DSRC channel bandwidth = **10 MHz**, raw OFDM capacity ≈ **27 Mbps** (QPSK, rate 1/2) → aggregate BSM traffic consumes **~12% of channel capacity**. This is why DSRC collapses under **high vehicle density > 400/km**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Thermal Noise Floor for V2X Receiver **Step 1 — Boltzmann's constant and standard temperature.** $$ k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}, \quad T_0 = 290\,\text{K} $$ **Step 2 — Thermal noise power spectral density in dBm/Hz.** $$ N_0 = 10\log_{10}(k T_0) + NF $$ $$ kT_0 = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 = 4.002 \times 10^{-21}\,\text{W} $$ $$ 10\log_{10}(4.002 \times 10^{-21}) = -203.98\,\text{dBW/Hz} = -173.98\,\text{dBm/Hz} $$ **Step 3 — With receiver noise figure $NF = 9\,\text{dB}$.** $$ N_0 = -173.98 + 9 = -164.98\,\text{dBm/Hz} $$ **Step 4 — Total noise in 10 MHz channel.** $$ N = N_0 + 10\log_{10}(10 \times 10^{6}) = -164.98 + 70 = -94.98\,\text{dBm} $$ $$ \boxed{N \approx -95\,\text{dBm}} $$ **Step 5 — Required SNR for QPSK (BER 10⁻⁶) ≈ 10.5 dB → required $P_{\text{rx}} \ge -84.5\,\text{dBm}$.** This validates that the link closes under the link budget derived in 3.2. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary | Metric | Computed Value | Status | |--------|----------------|--------| | Max range (latency-limited) | 1500 km | Theoretical ceiling | | Practical range (link budget) | ~300 m urban | Achievable | | FSPL @ 300 m, 5.9 GHz | 97.4 dB | Realistic | | Rx power | −71.4 dBm | 10.6 dB margin | | Per-vehicle BSM rate | 32 kbps | Standard load | | Aggregate rate (100 veh) | 3.2 Mbps | Within capacity | | Noise floor (10 MHz) | −95 dBm | Standard thermal | --- # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 V2X Ecosystem Architecture (Functional Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[V2V
Car to Car]:::v2x node2[V2I
Car to RSU]:::v2x node3[V2P
Car to Pedestrian]:::v2x node4[V2N
Car to Cloud]:::v2x node5[V2G
Car to Grid]:::v2x node6[OBU
On Board Unit]:::obucore node7[GNSS
GPS Module]:::sensor node8[CAN Bus
Vehicle Network]:::sensor node9[RSU
Roadside Unit]:::infra node10[5G Base Station
gNB]:::infra node11[Smart Grid
EVSE]:::infra node12[Pedestrian
Smartphone]:::endnode node13[Cloud Server
ITS Center]:::cloud node14[Another Vehicle
OBU]:::endnode node6 --> node1 node6 --> node2 node6 --> node3 node6 --> node4 node6 --> node5 node6 --- node7 node6 --- node8 node2 --> node9 node4 --> node10 node5 --> node11 node3 --> node12 node4 --> node13 node1 --> node14 classDef v2x fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff classDef obucore fill:#dc2626,stroke:#991b1b,color:#ffffff classDef sensor fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#b45309,color:#000000 classDef infra fill:#059669,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff classDef cloud fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#5b21b6,color:#ffffff classDef endnode fill:#64748b,stroke:#334155,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 DSRC vs C-V2X Protocol Stack Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph stack1[DSRC IEEE 802.11p Stack] direction TB a1[Application Layer]:::dsrc a2[SAE J2735 BSM and J2945]:::dsrc a3[IEEE 1609.4 Multi Channel]:::dsrc a4[IEEE 1609.3 Networking]:::dsrc a5[IEEE 802.11p MAC]:::dsrc a6[IEEE 802.11p PHY OFDM]:::dsrc end subgraph stack2[C-V2X 3GPP Stack] direction TB b1[Application V2X APP]:::cv2x b2[ETSI ITS Facilities CAM DENM]:::cv2x b3[3GPP V2X Layer 3]:::cv2x b4[LTE Sidelink PC5 5G NR]:::cv2x b5[EUTRA MAC Scheduling]:::cv2x b6[EUTRA PHY SC FDMA OFDM]:::cv2x end classDef dsrc fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff classDef cv2x fill:#a855f7,stroke:#6b21a8,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 V2X Message Lifecycle Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant OBU as Vehicle OBU participant GNSS as GNSS Receiver participant CAN as CAN Bus participant RADIO as DSRC C-V2X Radio participant RSU as RSU or Peer OBU OBU->>GNSS: Request position fix GNSS-->>OBU: Latitude Longitude Speed Heading OBU->>CAN: Read vehicle dynamics CAN-->>OBU: Yaw rate Acceleration Brake status OBU->>OBU: Build BSM payload 400 bytes OBU->>RADIO: Sign BSM with PKI private key RADIO->>RSU: Transmit BSM at 10 Hz RSU-->>RADIO: ACK or NACK RADIO->>OBU: Channel busy assessment OBU->>OBU: Backoff and retry if collision ``` ## 4.4 V2X Latency Budget Allocation ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA[Event Detected
0 ms]:::t0 stepB[Sensor Read
1 ms]:::t1 stepC[Message Build
2 ms]:::t2 stepD[MAC Access
4 ms]:::t3 stepE[PHY TX
1 ms]:::t4 stepF[Propagation
less than 1 us at 300 m]:::t5 stepG[RX Processing
2 ms]:::t6 stepH[Decision Actuation
5 ms]:::t7 stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF --> stepG --> stepH classDef t0 fill:#7f1d1d,stroke:#450a0a,color:#ffffff classDef t1 fill:#991b1b,stroke:#450a0a,color:#ffffff classDef t2 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#450a0a,color:#ffffff classDef t3 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#450a0a,color:#ffffff classDef t4 fill:#ef4444,stroke:#450a0a,color:#ffffff classDef t5 fill:#f87171,stroke:#450a0a,color:#ffffff classDef t6 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#450a0a,color:#000000 classDef t7 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#450a0a,color:#000000 ``` --- # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **Define V2X communication. List any FOUR sub-categories with one-line descriptions.** **Model Answer (10 lines):** V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) is a wireless communication paradigm enabling real-time data exchange between a vehicle and surrounding entities. Four sub-categories: 1. **V2V** — Vehicle-to-Vehicle for cooperative collision avoidance. 2. **V2I** — Vehicle-to-Infrastructure via Roadside Units (RSU). 3. **V2P** — Vehicle-to-Pedestrian safety alerts. 4. **V2N** — Vehicle-to-Network for cloud services and OTA updates. 5. *(Optional V2G)* — Vehicle-to-Grid for EV smart charging. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 0.5 each for four sub-categories)* --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **Differentiate between DSRC and C-V2X in terms of PHY layer technology and scalability.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | DSRC | C-V2X | |---|---|---| | PHY | OFDM, 10 MHz channel | SC-FDM (LTE) / OFDM (5G NR) | | MAC | CSMA/CA contention | Scheduled sidelink | | Scalability | Poor at high density | Excellent (semi-persistent scheduling) | | Latency | ~4 ms | ~1 ms | *(3 marks: tabular comparison with technical specifics)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Do NOT confuse DSRC channel bandwidth (10 MHz) with the US allocation (75 MHz).** Many students write "75 MHz channel" — wrong. > - **Always state units in dBm vs dB.** Mixing absolute and relative units loses 1–2 marks. > - **Mention standards explicitly** (IEEE 802.11p, SAE J2735, 3GPP Release 14) — examiners award marks for *standard name recall*. > - **In derivations, write the formula first, then substitute, then evaluate.** Skipping substitution = −1 mark. --- ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the layered architecture of the ETSI ITS-G5 protocol stack used in DSRC-based V2X communication. List the functions of the **Facilities**, **Networking & Transport**, and **Access** layers. **(b) [7 Marks]** A V2X transmitter emits **20 dBm** at **5.9 GHz** with antenna gains of $G_t = 2$ dBi and $G_r = 2$ dBi. Compute the **received power** at a vehicle **250 m away** assuming free-space propagation. Comment on whether the link closes if the receiver sensitivity is **−82 dBm**. #### Model Solution — Part (a) The ETSI ITS-G5 protocol stack has **7 layers**: 1. **Application Layer** — Hosts end-user safety applications like forward collision warning, GLOSA. 2. **Facilities Layer** — Generates and decodes standard messages (**CAM, DENM**); maintains local dynamic map (LDM). 3. **Networking & Transport Layer** — Implements **GeoNetworking (GN)**, IPv6 stack, TCP/UDP; handles multi-hop packet forwarding based on geographic addressing. 4. **Access Layer** — IEEE 802.11p MAC (EDCA with CSMA/CA) and PHY (OFDM). 5. **Physical Layer** — OFDM with 10 MHz channel, 52 subcarriers, BPSK–64-QAM. 6. **Security Layer** — IEEE 1609.2 PKI-based signature verification. 7. **Management Layer** — Address allocation, congestion control. *(Marks: 1 per layer described × 3 + 4 for diagram = 7)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Convert range to km.** $$ d = 250\,\text{m} = 0.25\,\text{km} $$ **Step 2 — Apply FSPL formula.** $$ FSPL = 20\log_{10}(0.25) + 20\log_{10}(5900) + 32.44 $$ $$ FSPL = 20(-0.602) + 20(3.771) + 32.44 $$ $$ FSPL = -12.04 + 75.42 + 32.44 = 95.82\,\text{dB} $$ **Step 3 — Link budget equation.** $$ P_{\text{rx}} = P_{\text{tx}} + G_t - FSPL + G_r $$ $$ P_{\text{rx}} = 20 + 2 - 95.82 + 2 = -71.82\,\text{dBm} $$ **Step 4 — Compare with sensitivity.** $$ P_{\text{rx}} = -71.82\,\text{dBm} \quad \text{vs} \quad S_{\text{min}} = -82\,\text{dBm} $$ $$ \text{Margin} = -71.82 - (-82) = +10.18\,\text{dB} $$ **Conclusion:** The link **closes successfully** with **~10 dB margin**, providing headroom for fading and interference. *(Marks: 2 for formula, 2 for computation, 1 for unit handling, 1 for conclusion, 1 for margin interpretation = 7)* --- ### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **BSM (Basic Safety Message)** format as per SAE J2735. What is the standard transmission frequency, and why is **10 Hz** chosen? **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **aggregate bandwidth** consumed by BSM traffic when **200 vehicles** are within a single RSU's coverage area. Given: BSM size = **400 bytes**, transmission rate = **10 Hz**. Comment on whether this causes channel congestion. #### Model Solution — Part (a) The **BSM Part 1** (mandatory core) contains: | Field | Size | Description | |---|---|---| | Message ID | 1 byte | Identifier (BSM = 0x02) | | Time Stamp | 2 bytes | Minute of the year | | Latitude | 4 bytes | 1/10 microdegree | | Longitude | 4 bytes | 1/10 microdegree | | Elevation | 2 bytes | 0.1 m resolution | | Speed | 2 bytes | 0.02 m/s | | Heading | 2 bytes | 0.0125° resolution | | Acceleration (4 axes) | 4 bytes | 0.01 m/s² | | Yaw rate | 2 bytes | 0.02 °/s | | Brake status | 2 bytes | Brakes, traction, ABS | **Part 1 size ≈ 25 bytes (control) + ~200 bytes (vehicle data) = ~400 bytes.** **Why 10 Hz?** - **Latency requirement**: 100 ms end-to-end → at 10 Hz the maximum inter-message gap is 100 ms. - **Tracking accuracy**: At 100 km/h, a vehicle moves 2.78 m in 100 ms — acceptable position uncertainty. - **Decentralized consensus**: 10 Hz is sufficient for cooperative collision avoidance without overwhelming the channel. *(Marks: 3 for diagram, 2 for field sizes, 2 for 10 Hz justification = 7)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Per-vehicle BSM bit rate.** $$ R_{\text{per}} = 400\,\text{bytes} \times 8\,\text{bits/byte} \times 10\,\text{Hz} = 32{,}000\,\text{bps} $$ **Step 2 — Aggregate load for 200 vehicles.** $$ R_{\text{agg}} = 200 \times 32{,}000 = 6.4 \times 10^{6}\,\text{bps} = 6.4\,\text{Mbps} $$ **Step 3 — Channel capacity comparison.** DSRC channel (10 MHz, QPSK 1/2) → ~6 Mbps usable → **load exceeds capacity**. **Step 4 — Conclusion.** When 200 vehicles coexist, the aggregate BSM traffic **saturates** the DSRC channel. This causes: - **MAC contention** (CSMA/CA backoffs). - **Latency inflation** beyond the 100 ms safety budget. - **Packet loss > 50%**. **Mitigation strategies:** - **Decentralized Congestion Control (DCC)** per ETSI TS 102 687 — adaptively reduce $f_{\text{tx}}$ or $P_{\text{tx}}$. - **Migration to C-V2X** with scheduled sidelink resources → eliminates contention. *(Marks: 2 for per-vehicle rate, 2 for aggregate, 2 for comparison, 1 for mitigation = 7)* --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Frequency Recall Points for KTU PEMUT725 — Module 4** - ✅ **V2X** = Vehicle-to-Everything; sub-classes: **V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G**. - ✅ **DSRC** uses **IEEE 802.11p** at **5.85–5.925 GHz (US)** with **10 MHz channels**. - ✅ **C-V2X** uses **3GPP Release 14/15** sidelink (PC5) + Uu interface; **5G NR-V2X** in Rel. 16. - ✅ **BSM (SAE J2735)** = ~**400 bytes**, **10 Hz**, contains position, speed, heading, acceleration. - ✅ **CAM (ETSI EN 302 637-2)** = European equivalent of BSM, ~250 bytes, 1–10 Hz. - ✅ **DENM (ETSI EN 302 637-3)** = event-triggered, ~800 bytes. - ✅ **Latency budget** = ≤ **100 ms** total; ≤ **20 ms** for pre-crash. - ✅ **Max practical range** ≈ **300 m urban, 1000 m highway LOS**. - ✅ **FSPL @ 300 m, 5.9 GHz ≈ 97.4 dB** (high-yield formula). - ✅ **Noise floor @ 10 MHz ≈ −95 dBm** (with 9 dB NF). - ✅ **Aggregate BSM rate = N_veh × 32 kbps** → congestion beyond ~150 vehicles. - ✅ **DCC (Decentralized Congestion Control)** is mandatory in ETSI ITS-G5. - ✅ **PKI security** via IEEE 1609.2 with **pseudonym certificates** (rotated every 5 min for privacy). - ✅ **GNSS sync** is critical for C-V2X sidelink; DSRC is asynchronous. - ✅ **5G NR-V2X supports 14 dBm higher EIRP** than DSRC in sidelink mode. - ✅ **GLOSA (Green Light Optimal Speed Advisory)** is the canonical V2I application. - ✅ **Platooning** uses V2V to achieve <1 m inter-vehicle gap, saving 10–15% fuel. ---
